name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage for the family lifestyle and wellbeing blog, providing quick access to core sections (Recipes, Activities, Articles, Shop, Planner, Tools), global search, and dynamically loaded featured content.

core_features: Load and display featured recipes, recent wellbeing articles, and highlighted activities using getHomepageFeaturedContent.
Provide global navigation to Recipes, Activities, Articles (implied via Articles link in navigation), Shop, Planner, Tools, and Home via the header.
Expose a global search form in the header that redirects to search.html with the search query as a URL parameter.

Allow navigation from featured recipes, highlighted activities, and recent wellbeing articles to their respective listing pages (recipes.html, activities.html, articles.html if present in nav) using clicks on content blocks.
Provide a footer link to newsletter_preferences.html so users can manage newsletter preferences.
Optionally react to a section-focused URL parameter (section) to bring a specific content section (recipes, activities, articles) into focus on page load.
user_workflows: Browse featured content: 1) User lands on index.html; 2) On page load, the client calls getHomepageFeaturedContent with no parameters; 3) The page renders featuredRecipes in the recipes section, recentWellbeingArticles in the articles section, and highlightedActivities in the activities section; 4) User clicks any item in these sections to navigate to the corresponding listing page (recipes.html, activities.html, or a general articles listing if present).

Access Recipes from homepage: 1) User clicks the 'Recipes' item in the header navigation or a 'View all recipes' control in the featured recipes section; 2) Browser navigates to recipes.html without additional parameters.

Access Activities from homepage: 1) User clicks the 'Activities' item in the header navigation or a 'View all activities' control in the highlighted activities section; 2) Browser navigates to activities.html without additional parameters.

Use global search: 1) User enters a search term into the global search input in the header; 2) User submits the form (via button click or pressing Enter); 3) The form handler URL-encodes the search term and redirects the browser to search.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}; 4) The search page reads the query parameter and performs the search.

Access Shop, Planner, and Tools: 1) User clicks 'Shop', 'Planner', or 'Tools' in the header or corresponding promo blocks on the homepage; 2) Browser navigates to shop.html, planner.html, or tools.html respectively without additional parameters.
Manage newsletter preferences: 1) User scrolls to the footer; 2) User clicks the 'Newsletter Preferences' link; 3) Browser navigates to newsletter_preferences.html without additional parameters.

Deep-link into a specific homepage section: 1) User opens index.html with an optional URL parameter section (e.g., index.html?section=recipes|activities|articles); 2) On load, the homepage reads the section parameter; 3) If the value matches a known section, the page scrolls or focuses to that content block; 4) User then browses or navigates from that section as usual.
interactions: Header navigation clicks: Clicking 'Home' navigates to index.html; clicking 'Recipes' navigates to recipes.html; clicking 'Activities' navigates to activities.html; clicking 'Shop' navigates to shop.html; clicking 'Planner' navigates to planner.html; clicking 'Tools' navigates to tools.html.

Global search submission: User types text into the search input and submits; the onSubmit handler prevents default form posting behavior, constructs search.html?query={encodedInput}, and sets window.location to that URL.
Featured recipe item click: Clicking a recipe card or its associated 'View recipes' control triggers navigation to recipes.html.
Highlighted activity item click: Clicking an activity card or its associated 'View activities' control triggers navigation to activities.html.

Recent wellbeing article item click: Clicking an article item or its 'View articles' control (or Articles section link) triggers navigation to an articles listing page (e.g., articles.html) if defined in the global navigation.
Planner promo click: Clicking the planner promo component triggers navigation to planner.html.
Tools promo click: Clicking the tools promo component triggers navigation to tools.html.
Footer newsletter preferences click: Clicking 'Newsletter Preferences' in the footer triggers navigation to newsletter_preferences.html.

Section focus on load: If index.html is loaded with a section URL parameter, the homepage-section-router component identifies the target section and triggers a scroll or focus to that section once getHomepageFeaturedContent has resolved and the relevant section is rendered.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) initializes by calling getHomepageFeaturedContent with no parameters when the page is loaded or when the homepage-featured-content-loader component is mounted. The returned object provides featuredRecipes (array of Recipe), recentWellbeingArticles (array of Article filtered by category=wellbeing), and highlightedActivities (array of Activity). These arrays populate the corresponding sections. The page does not store state in localStorage; instead, immediate state is derived from the API response and the current URL. The primary use of URL parameters on this page is the optional section parameter on index.html. When present and equal to 'recipes', 'activities', or 'articles', the homepage-section-router component sets an internal view state to mark the corresponding section as active and then scrolls/focuses to it after content is loaded. Outgoing navigation also relies on URL parameters: the global search form redirects to search.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}, where {encodedSearchTerm} is the user input; other navigations use simple URLs without parameters (recipes.html, activities.html, shop.html, planner.html, tools.html, newsletter_preferences.html). The Home page itself does not modify URL parameters; it only reads section on load, and all changes in user context are managed by navigating to different pages with their own URLs and parameters.

components.functionality: Provides global navigation links to Home, Recipes, Activities, Shop, Planner, Tools, and contains the global search form trigger or inline search input.
Collects a search term and redirects the user to search.html with the query parameter set to the entered term.
On page load, calls getHomepageFeaturedContent to retrieve featuredRecipes, recentWellbeingArticles, and highlightedActivities, then passes this data down to corresponding content components.
Displays a list or grid of featured recipes sourced from getHomepageFeaturedContent.featuredRecipes and provides a control to navigate to recipes.html.
Displays a list or grid of highlighted activities sourced from getHomepageFeaturedContent.highlightedActivities and provides a control to navigate to activities.html.
Displays a list of recent wellbeing articles sourced from getHomepageFeaturedContent.recentWellbeingArticles and provides a control to navigate to an article listing page if available.
Highlights access to the Planner feature and provides a navigation control to planner.html.
Highlights access to the Tools section (including features like the Wellness Budget Planner) and provides a navigation control to tools.html.
Reads the section URL parameter on index.html load and, after featured content is loaded, scrolls or focuses to the requested section (recipes, activities, or articles) if specified.
Provides footer navigation links, including 'Newsletter Preferences', which navigates to newsletter_preferences.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom editorial flow: a full-width hero (part of the overall shell, not this component list) sits at the very top, immediately followed by a full-width navigation bar. Beneath the nav, the main content sits in a centered column (~1100–1200px max width) containing, in order: a compact section label/route indicator, a large featured-content area, then several clearly separated sections (recipes, activities, wellbeing articles) and finally promotional blocks for Planner and Tools before a minimal footer. No sidebars; all components stack in a single column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom editorial flow: a full-width hero (part of the overall shell, not this component list) sits at the very top, immediately followed by a full-width navigation bar. Beneath the nav, the main content sits in a centered column (~1100–1200px max width) containing, in order: a compact section label/route indicator, a large featured-content area, then several clearly separated sections (recipes, activities, wellbeing articles) and finally promotional blocks for Planner and Tools before a minimal footer. No sidebars; all components stack in a single column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero (outside this spec) visually occupies roughly the top third of the initial viewport. The header navigation bar below it takes ~8–10% of vertical space. Immediately under the nav, the central content column occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with 15–20% gutters on each side. Within the column, the featured-content block (driven by the data loader) gets the largest vertical allocation, roughly the height of a generous hero card, while subsequent sections (recipes, activities, articles) use shorter, card-based bands. Vertical gaps of 24–40px separate sections; internal padding within sections is typically 24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is deliberately low and calm. Only one or two major pieces of content are visible at a time in the main column. Each component has breathing room: generous white space above and below, comfortable padding around text and images, and no cramped multi-column grids above the fold. Sections like recipes or activities use a small number of large, editorial-style cards rather than many small tiles, keeping the experience relaxed and easy to scan.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey starts at the central hero headline at the very top (outside this spec), then flows down to the full-width navigation bar where users scan section labels horizontally. From there, attention drops into the main column: a small, left-aligned line acts as a subtle label or route indicator, setting context. The eye then lands on the large featured-content area (fed by the loader), which acts as the new focal anchor as the user scrolls. Below that, the eye continues down through clearly separated content bands—featured recipes, highlighted activities, and recent wellbeing articles—before arriving at Planner and Tools promos and, finally, the understated footer.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a modern, editorial landing page. A full-width hero at the very top (outside these components) sets a warm family tone, followed directly by a clean, white navigation bar spanning edge-to-edge. Below this, the page tightens into a centered content column, with generous side gutters framing the core experience. A small line of text near the top-left of the column quietly orients the user, and beneath it a large, magazine-style featured panel fills the column width, showcasing highlighted content with bold typography and imagery. As users scroll, they encounter distinct, well-spaced horizontal bands: a featured recipes block, a playful activities block, and a calm wellbeing articles block—each feeling like its own mini editorial spread. Near the bottom of the column, two understated promo bands for the Planner and Tools sit side-by-side or stacked (depending on screen size), leading into a simple, low-chrome footer. The entire layout feels airy and linear, guiding the user from brand to structure to stories without visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation sits immediately below the full-width hero, spanning the entire viewport width as a horizontal white bar. It is center-aligned on the same implicit 12-column grid as the rest of the page, with the inner content constrained to ~1100–1200px. Within this bar, navigation items (Recipes, Activities, Articles, Shop, Planner, Tools) are arranged in a single horizontal row that can gracefully wrap to a second line on narrower screens, maintaining generous left and right padding (16–24px) around each label. The nav items are visually centered horizontally within the content width, with a comfortable vertical padding of ~16px above and below the labels, creating a band that is visually about 8–10% of the initial viewport height. There is clear white space (~24px) separating the bottom of this nav bar from the start of the main content column below, emphasizing its role as a distinct, structural element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar that scrolls away with the page; it anchors the top of the content stack directly beneath the hero and aligns all downstream content to its central grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is positioned at the top of the main content column, just below the header navigation and slightly separated from it with a vertical gap of ~24px. It is horizontally centered within the content column but aligned visually to the left, occupying roughly 50–60% of the column width on desktop (e.g., a long search field extending from the left edge toward the center, leaving open white space on the right). The input field itself is a single, unobtrusive horizontal bar with 12–16px internal padding and minimal chrome, matching the flat aesthetic. There is ~16–24px of white space above the search form and ~24px below it before the small section label/tagline appears, ensuring it feels accessible but not dominant. On narrower screens, the search form expands to nearly the full column width, maintaining side padding consistent with the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; scrolls with content. It sits as a light functional strip between the structural navigation bar and the editorial content starting below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage featured content loader controls a large, hero-style content area that sits near the top of the main content column, directly below the small section label/tagline and above the themed content sections (recipes, activities, articles). Visually, this area reads as a full-bleed card within the column: it occupies 100% of the column width (respecting the central grid) and roughly 30–40% of the remaining initial viewport height on desktop. The block consists of a prominent image or solid-color panel on the left or full-width, with a bold, large headline and supporting text overlaid or placed to the right, mimicking a magazine feature. There is generous internal padding (~24–32px) and ample whitespace around the text, with ~32–40px of vertical space above and below the block separating it from neighboring elements. This featured area is the main focal point of the content, visually echoing the '100 BEST...' feature in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, but its contents update dynamically based on state; the block itself does not move or resize on scroll beyond standard responsive behavior. It anchors the start of the story-driven content within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured recipes section appears directly below the homepage featured content area, separated by a vertical gap of ~32px to clearly mark a new section. It spans the full width of the central content column, using the same ~1100–1200px max width and center alignment. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Featured Recipes') sits with ~8–16px of space below it before the content cards begin. The section contains a small number (2–4) of large, editorial-style recipe cards arranged in one or two rows depending on screen width. Each card uses generous padding (~16–24px), a large image on top or to the left, and readable text with comfortable line spacing. The entire section has ~24–32px of internal vertical padding, with ~32px of white space separating it from the section below (highlighted activities), maintaining the calm, low-density rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical flow; cards rearrange responsively (e.g., from multi-column to single-column on small screens) but the section remains a clearly bounded horizontal band in the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted activities section sits directly below the featured recipes section, with a consistent vertical gap of ~32px to signal a new content band. It also occupies the full width of the main content column and is center-aligned on the same grid. A left-aligned section title (e.g., 'Activities to Try This Week') anchors the top, followed by a short vertical gap (~8–12px) and then a series of activity cards. These cards are playful but still spacious: 2–3 cards per row on desktop, expanding to full-width on mobile, each with generous internal padding (~16–24px), a small image or icon, and brief descriptive text. The section maintains ample white space between cards (at least 16px horizontally and vertically), and ~24–32px of padding at the top and bottom of the section. A ~32px gap separates this section from the recent wellbeing articles section below, aligning with the consistent editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing directly after recipes. Card layout is responsive, but the section acts as a stable horizontal slice in the main vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent wellbeing articles section is placed below the highlighted activities section with ~32px of vertical separation. It spans the width of the central content column and follows the same center-aligned grid. At the top, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Latest Wellbeing Articles') introduces the section, followed by a small gap (~8–12px) and a list or grid of article previews. Each article preview is styled like a compact editorial snippet: a smaller image or color block on the left (or top on mobile), with title and short excerpt to the right, and generous vertical padding (~16–24px) inside each item. Items are stacked vertically with clear dividers in the form of white space (~16–24px) rather than heavy lines. The overall section has ~24–32px of vertical padding and is followed by ~40px of space before the planner and tools promo blocks, implying a slight transition from editorial content to utility/promotional content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block following the activities section. Article items stack vertically and reflow on smaller screens, but the section remains a clean, scrollable band within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner promo block appears in the lower part of the main content column, beneath the recent wellbeing articles section. On desktop, it sits to the left as one of two horizontally aligned promotional tiles (Planner on the left, Tools on the right), each occupying roughly 48–50% of the column width with a small gap (~16–24px) between them. The tile has a solid background or subtle tint, with comfortable internal padding (~24px) and a compact heading, short description, and call-to-action aligned to the left. There is ~32–40px of vertical space separating this promo row from the articles above and ~24–32px separating it from the footer below. On smaller screens, the tiles stack vertically, each taking full column width with consistent spacing between them, preserving spaciousness and avoiding clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of a promo row near the bottom of the column. It reflows from half-width (side-by-side) on larger screens to full-width stacked on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools promo block is paired with the planner promo block in a shared row below the recent wellbeing articles section. On desktop, it appears on the right side of the row, mirroring the planner block in width and structure, and occupying roughly 48–50% of the main content column. It features a similarly styled tile with ample internal padding (~24px), a concise heading and description, and a clear call-to-action, all left-aligned. The horizontal gap between the planner and tools tiles (~16–24px) keeps them distinct yet related. There is equal vertical spacing above and below the combined promo row, so the pair reads as one cohesive promotional band before the footer. On smaller screens, this tools block stacks directly below the planner promo, maintaining generous vertical spacing (~16–24px) between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as the right-hand tile in a two-column promo layout on large screens and a stacked second tile on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage section router is an underlying, mostly non-visual controller that influences which content is active or highlighted at the top of the main content area. Its presence is surfaced subtly as a small, left-aligned line of text just above the homepage featured content loader, in the role analogous to the '2026 Family Treasure' label in the mockup. This line sits beneath the global search form with a vertical gap of ~16–24px and is aligned to the left edge of the content column. It uses minimal styling—small, uppercase or lightly accent-colored text—taking up only a single line and leaving plenty of whitespace above and below. Changes in router state may update this label (e.g., 'This Week’s Feature', 'For Busy Weeknights') and determine which featured content appears below, but the physical space remains a slim band in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical/state controller; visually manifested as a small text label that stays fixed in size and position near the top of the main content column, directly above the featured content loader.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer links sit at the very bottom of the page, spanning the full viewport width as a simple horizontal band with a white or lightly tinted background. The inner content is centered on the same grid as the rest of the page, constrained to ~1100–1200px. Links are arranged either in a single centered row or in 2–3 compact columns, depending on content, with generous horizontal spacing and minimal typographic styling to maintain the clean aesthetic. Vertical padding of ~24–32px above and below the links keeps the footer light and unobtrusive. There is a clear vertical gap (~32–40px) between the bottom of the Planner/Tools promo blocks and the top of the footer, ensuring it reads as a distinct, closing element rather than a continuation of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the end of the scrollable content. It does not stick or float; it simply terminates the vertical flow with a minimal, structured navigation area.

name: Recipes
title: Recipes
description: Browse, filter, and sort family recipes, then open individual recipe details or send recipes to the planner.

core_features: Load available filter options for recipes (meal type, servings, rating, cook time, calories) using the getRecipeFilterOptions interface.
Read current filter, sorting, and pagination state from URL parameters and use them to query recipes via the listRecipes interface.
Allow users to adjust filters (meal type, maximum cook time, maximum calories per serving, minimum rating, servings, optional health tag) and update results.
Allow users to sort recipe results and update both the displayed list and the URL state.
Display recipe results as cards with key information (title, image, cook time, calories, rating, servings) based on the Recipe data model returned by listRecipes.
Provide pagination for recipe results using the page parameter and totalCount returned by listRecipes.
Allow users to open a specific recipe in recipe_detail.html by clicking a recipe card, passing the recipe id via URL.
Optionally allow users to navigate to planner.html with a selected plan context when they choose to add a recipe to a planner.
user_workflows:
Initial load with optional incoming filters:
1. User navigates to recipes.html, possibly with URL parameters such as mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag, sortBy, and page.
2. The page controller parses all URL parameters and normalises them (e.g., converts numeric parameters to numbers, applies default page when missing).
3. The page calls getRecipeFilterOptions to retrieve the set of available filter option values and labels (meal types, serving options, rating options, cook time options, calorie options).
4. The filter components initialise their selected values from the parsed URL parameters, matching them to the available options returned by getRecipeFilterOptions when possible.
5. The page calls listRecipes with the current filter and sorting values derived from URL parameters (mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag, sortBy, page, and an externally provided pageSize).
6. The recipes grid receives the recipes and totalCount from listRecipes and renders the current page of recipe cards.
7. The applied-filters bar shows any active filters derived from URL parameters, allowing the user to understand the current search context.

Adjusting filters and refreshing results:
1. User changes one or more filters (e.g., selects a different meal type, adjusts maximum cook time, changes minimum rating, changes servings, or adds/removes a health tag) via the filter controls.
2. The filter-sidebar component emits an onFilterChange event with the updated filter values.
3. The page controller updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new filter state, resetting the page parameter to the first page when filters change.
4. After updating the URL, the page controller triggers a new listRecipes call with the updated filter values and current sortBy, page, and pageSize.
5. When the listRecipes response arrives, the recipes grid updates the displayed recipe cards, and the applied-filters bar updates to reflect the new active filters.

Sorting results:
1. User selects a sorting option (for example, by rating, recency, or preparation speed) using the sort control.
2. The sort-dropdown component emits an onSortChange event with the selected sortBy value.
3. The page controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets the page parameter to the first page.
4. The page controller calls listRecipes again with the same filter parameters but the new sortBy and page.
5. The recipes grid re-renders with recipes ordered according to the selected sortBy value.

Pagination of recipe results:
1. User scrolls to the bottom or interacts with pagination controls to navigate to a different page of results.
2. The pagination-controls component emits an onPageChange event with the requested page index.
3. The page controller updates the page query parameter in the URL to the selected page index while leaving other parameters unchanged.
4. The page controller calls listRecipes with the same filters and sortBy, but with the updated page.
5. The recipes grid receives the new page of recipes and renders them, and the pagination-controls recalculates and displays navigation state based on totalCount and pageSize.

Clearing or removing specific filters:
1. User clicks a control in the applied-filters bar to remove a specific active filter (e.g., remove mealType, clear maxCookTimeMinutes, or remove healthTag).
2. The applied-filters-bar component emits an onFilterRemove event indicating which filter key should be cleared.
3. The page controller removes the corresponding parameter from the URL query string (or sets it to an empty state), resets page to the first page, and leaves other parameters unchanged.
4. The page controller calls listRecipes with the updated filter set.
5. The recipes grid updates to show results without that filter, and the applied-filters bar no longer shows the removed filter.

Opening a recipe detail page:
1. User clicks on a recipe card in the grid.
2. The recipe-card component emits an onRecipeClick event containing that recipe's id.
3. The page navigates to recipe_detail.html, appending the id query parameter mapped from the clicked recipe id (e.g., recipe_detail.html?id=<recipeId>).
4. The browser loads the recipe detail page, which can use the id parameter to show full details for the selected recipe (handled outside this page's scope).

Adding a recipe to a planner (contextual flow):
1. In a context where the user is working with a plan (for example, after being directed from planner.html with contextual information available to this page, or after selecting a plan via UI on this page), the recipe-card or a dedicated action component exposes an "Add to Planner" control.
2. User clicks the "Add to Planner" control on a specific recipe.
3. The add-to-planner-action component emits an onAddToPlannerClick event containing the recipe id and the selected plan identifier.
4. The page navigates to planner.html with a planId URL parameter corresponding to the chosen plan and any additional contextual parameters needed by planner.html.
5. The planner.html page (outside this page's scope) uses planId and potentially the recipe context to add or schedule the recipe.
interactions: Changing a filter control (e.g., selecting a meal type option): updates the corresponding in-memory filter state, updates the mealType URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a fresh listRecipes call with the new filter set.

Adjusting numeric or range-based filters (maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings): on each confirmed change (e.g., blur or explicit apply action), updates the relevant URL parameters, resets page to the first page, and re-queries listRecipes.
Selecting or changing a healthTag filter: updates the healthTag URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and re-queries listRecipes.
Using the sort dropdown: updates sortBy in the URL, resets page to the first page, and triggers listRecipes with unchanged filters but updated sortBy.
Clicking a pagination control for a specific page: updates the page URL parameter only, then calls listRecipes with the same filters and sortBy but the new page value.
Clicking a "previous" or "next" pagination button: adjusts the page parameter by the appropriate increment or decrement, then calls listRecipes with the updated page.
Clicking an applied filter pill in the applied-filters bar to remove it: clears the corresponding parameter from the URL, resets page to the first page, and re-calls listRecipes.

Clicking a global "Clear all filters" control (if provided): removes all filter-related parameters from the URL, resets page and optionally sortBy to configured defaults, then calls listRecipes with no filters (or minimal baseline filters).
Clicking on a recipe card (anywhere on the card that is defined as clickable): navigates to recipe_detail.html with the id query parameter set to that card's recipe id.

Clicking the "Add to Planner" action on a recipe card (when available): navigates to planner.html with planId set to the chosen plan and any additional context as URL parameters, without modifying the current filters on recipes.html.

Manually editing the URL query string (e.g., adding or changing mealType or page) and reloading or navigating: the page controller parses the new URL state and initialises filters and pagination accordingly before calling listRecipes.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Recipes page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filters, sorting, and pagination.

URL parameter to state mapping:
- mealType (string): If present, this value is used directly as the mealType parameter for listRecipes. The filter controls for meal type are initialised to this value if it matches one of the mealTypes returned by getRecipeFilterOptions. If absent, no specific mealType filter is applied.
- maxCookTimeMinutes (number): If present, parsed as a number and passed as maxCookTimeMinutes to listRecipes. The cook time filter UI initialises its selection based on this value by matching it to available cookTimeOptionsMinutes from getRecipeFilterOptions. If absent, no limit is applied for cook time via this parameter.
- maxCaloriesPerServing (number): If present, parsed as a number and passed as maxCaloriesPerServing to listRecipes. The calories filter UI initialises from this value by matching it to calorieOptionsPerServing from getRecipeFilterOptions. If absent, no calorie limit filter is applied via this parameter.
- minRating (number): If present, parsed as a number and passed as minRating to listRecipes. The rating filter UI initialises from this value using ratingOptions from getRecipeFilterOptions. If absent, no minimum rating constraint is applied via this parameter.
- servings (number): If present, parsed as a number and passed as servings to listRecipes. The servings filter control initialises from this value using servingOptions from getRecipeFilterOptions where possible. If absent, no serving-based filter is issued via this parameter.
- healthTag (string): If present, passed as healthTag to listRecipes and represented as an active filter in the applied-filters bar and any health-tag-specific UI control. If absent, no healthTag filter is used.
- sortBy (string): If present, passed directly to listRecipes as sortBy. The sort dropdown initialises to the option that matches this value. If absent, the page uses a configured default sort order; this default is reflected in both the dropdown and the sortBy parameter used in listRecipes.
- page (number): If present, parsed as a number and passed to listRecipes as page. If absent or invalid, the page controller defaults to the first page index. Whenever filters or sortBy change, the page parameter is reset to the first page and the URL is updated accordingly.

Additional state and parameters used with interfaces:
- pageSize (number): This value is required by listRecipes but is not controlled by URL in this design. It is provided by an external configuration or environment value and is treated as an opaque parameter by this page. It is used to determine how many recipes to request and, together with totalCount from listRecipes, is used by the pagination-controls component to calculate pagination state.

State update rules:
- Any user action that changes filters (mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag) or sortBy triggers a URL update reflecting the new values and a reset of page to its default.
- Pagination changes update only the page parameter, leaving other parameters unchanged.
- The page listens to URL changes (browser navigation, manual URL edits, or internal updates) and responds by re-parsing state and issuing a new listRecipes call.
- The results from listRecipes (recipes array and totalCount) do not themselves alter the URL; they only influence rendered components.
- Navigation to recipe_detail.html and planner.html uses URL parameters to pass recipe and plan identifiers but does not modify the current Recipes page's URL beyond the navigation itself.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state management for the Recipes page. On initial load and whenever URL parameters change, it parses mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag, sortBy, and page from the query string, retrieves filter metadata via getRecipeFilterOptions, and requests recipe data via listRecipes. It passes responses and derived state down to child components (filter-sidebar, sort-dropdown, recipes-grid, pagination-controls, applied-filters-bar). It also updates URL parameters in response to events from child components.
event_handlers: onInitialLoad: Triggered when recipes.html is first loaded. Calls getRecipeFilterOptions, then parses current URL parameters and calls listRecipes with derived filters, sortBy, page, and externally provided pageSize.
onUrlChange: Triggered when the browser URL query string changes (via user navigation or internal updates). Re-parses parameters, calls listRecipes, and updates child components with new data.

onFilterChange: Subscribed to events from filter-sidebar and applied-filters-bar. Updates relevant URL parameters (mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag), resets page to the first page, and triggers listRecipes.
onSortChange: Subscribed to events from sort-dropdown. Updates sortBy in the URL, resets page to the first page, and calls listRecipes.
onPageChange: Subscribed to events from pagination-controls. Updates the page parameter in the URL and calls listRecipes with unchanged filters and sortBy.
onRecipeClick: Subscribed to events from recipe-card instances. Navigates to recipe_detail.html by setting the id query parameter to the clicked recipe's id.
onAddToPlannerClick: Subscribed to events from add-to-planner-action. Navigates to planner.html with planId and any additional context encoded as URL parameters.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for users to set or change recipe filters including meal type, servings, maximum cook time, maximum calories per serving, minimum rating, and optional healthTag. It initialises its values from URL-based state passed down by the page controller and from the option lists returned by getRecipeFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onMealTypeChange: Fired when the user selects or changes the meal type filter. Emits a structured filter state object to recipes-page-controller's onFilterChange handler.
onServingsChange: Fired when the user adjusts the servings filter. Emits updated filter state to recipes-page-controller.
onMaxCookTimeChange: Fired when the user updates the maximum cook time filter (e.g., via presets or numeric input). Emits updated filter state.
onMaxCaloriesChange: Fired when the user updates the maximum calories per serving filter. Emits updated filter state.
onMinRatingChange: Fired when the user adjusts the minimum rating filter. Emits updated filter state.
onHealthTagChange: Fired when the user selects or clears a health tag filter. Emits updated filter state.
onApplyFilters: Optionally fired when the user confirms filter changes (if the UI uses an explicit apply interaction). Aggregates all filter values and emits them to the page controller.

components.functionality: Displays a summary of currently active filters derived from URL parameters (mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag) and allows users to remove individual filters or clear all filters at once. It receives the current filter state from the page controller and renders it in a concise, interactive format.
event_handlers: onFilterRemove: Fired when the user removes a specific filter representation (e.g., clicking a close icon on a filter pill). Emits the key of the filter to be removed to recipes-page-controller's onFilterChange.
onClearAllFilters: Fired when the user chooses to clear all filters. Emits an instruction to recipes-page-controller to reset all filter-related URL parameters and rely on default filter state.

components.functionality: Allows users to select the sort order of recipe results. The component initialises the selected value from the sortBy parameter parsed by the page controller and presents one or more sorting options defined by external configuration. It passes the selected sortBy value back to the page controller, which then re-queries listRecipes.
event_handlers: onSortChange: Fired when the user selects a different sort option. Emits the chosen sortBy value to recipes-page-controller for URL and data updates.

components.functionality: Displays the list of recipes returned by listRecipes as a grid or list of recipe-card components. It re-renders whenever the recipes or loading state provided by the page controller changes. It does not perform data fetching itself; it purely renders what it receives and delegates interactions (clicks) to recipe-card components.
event_handlers: onRecipeCardClick: Receives bubbling onRecipeClick events from child recipe-card components and forwards them to recipes-page-controller.
onAddToPlannerClick: Receives bubbling onAddToPlannerClick events from child add-to-planner-action components and forwards them to recipes-page-controller.

components.functionality: Represents a single recipe in the recipes-grid. Renders key data from a Recipe entity (such as title, imageUrl, cookTimeMinutes and/or totalTimeMinutes, caloriesPerServing, rating, ratingCount, and servings). When the user clicks the main interactive area of the card, the component emits an event with the recipe's id so the page can navigate to the detail page. It may also host an add-to-planner-action component if the context requires it.
event_handlers: onRecipeClick: Fired when the user clicks the recipe card (or a defined clickable area). Emits the bound Recipe.id to recipes-page-controller via recipes-grid.
onAddToPlannerClick: If an add-to-planner-action is present, this event may be emitted by that child component and bubbled up through the card to recipes-grid and then to recipes-page-controller.

components.functionality: Manages pagination of recipe results. It receives the current page index from URL-derived state, the totalCount of recipes from listRecipes, and the externally provided pageSize. Using these values, it calculates the total number of pages and renders navigation controls (such as previous/next buttons and page number selectors) used to switch between pages.
event_handlers: onPageChange: Fired when the user selects a specific page index. Emits the new page value to recipes-page-controller for URL update and listRecipes invocation.
onNextPage: Fired when the user clicks a 'next' control. Requests the next page index, if available, and emits it to recipes-page-controller.
onPreviousPage: Fired when the user clicks a 'previous' control. Requests the previous page index, if available, and emits it to recipes-page-controller.

components.functionality: Provides an action for sending a particular recipe to a planning context. It may appear on each recipe-card when the page is used in a planner-related context. When the user activates it, the component prepares the recipe id and a selected plan identifier (provided by context or user selection) and requests navigation to planner.html.
event_handlers: onAddToPlannerClick: Fired when the user activates the add-to-planner action. Emits an object containing the Recipe.id and the chosen planner plan identifier to recipes-page-controller, which then performs navigation to planner.html with planId set accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global full-width hero at the very top (site-level), followed by the global full-width navigation bar. The Recipes page content then occupies a centered main column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with generous side gutters. Within this main column, the first element is the recipes-page-controller acting like the small editorial label plus section header. Directly beneath it is a full-width, low-chrome control band that combines the applied-filters-bar and the sort-dropdown, echoing the mockup’s full-width nav strip. Below that, the content splits into a gentle two-column composition inside the main column: a slim filter-sidebar on the left and the recipes-grid on the right. The first recipe-card in the grid can span the full grid width and act as an in-column hero card, similar to the ‘100 BEST…’ feature in the mockup. Pagination-controls sit centered at the bottom of this column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global full-width hero at the very top (site-level), followed by the global full-width navigation bar. The Recipes page content then occupies a centered main column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with generous side gutters. Within this main column, the first element is the recipes-page-controller acting like the small editorial label plus section header. Directly beneath it is a full-width, low-chrome control band that combines the applied-filters-bar and the sort-dropdown, echoing the mockup’s full-width nav strip. Below that, the content splits into a gentle two-column composition inside the main column: a slim filter-sidebar on the left and the recipes-grid on the right. The first recipe-card in the grid can span the full grid width and act as an in-column hero card, similar to the ‘100 BEST…’ feature in the mockup. Pagination-controls sit centered at the bottom of this column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: From the top of the viewport, roughly the first 30–35% is still dominated by the global hero, then ~8–10% by the global nav. Starting below the nav, the recipes-page-controller and the combined applied-filters-bar/sort area together consume roughly another 10–15% of vertical space. The main results area (filter-sidebar + recipes-grid) takes up the bulk of the remaining vertical height. Horizontally, the main content column sits centered and spans about 1100–1200px on desktop, with 15–20% viewport gutters on each side. Inside this column, the filter-sidebar occupies roughly 28–32% of the width, and the recipes-grid uses the remaining 68–72%. Major sections (controller, control band, results, pagination) are separated vertically by 24–40px of white space, aligned to the 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a low, editorial content density: large text, big imagery, and generous white space between sections. Controls are not tightly packed—filter chips have 8–12px padding, the filter-sidebar uses a single, airy column of options, and the recipes-grid shows relatively few, larger cards per viewport (typically two columns on desktop) rather than many small tiles. Vertical spacing between cards and between major blocks is at least 24–32px, maintaining the calm, lifestyle-magazine feel and avoiding a utilitarian, dashboard-like density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the global hero and nav draw initial attention, the eye is guided down into the main column where a small, left-aligned label from the recipes-page-controller orients the user (‘Recipes’, short description, counts), similar to the ‘2026 Family Treasure’ line. Immediately below, the eye travels across the horizontal band of filters and sort controls, scanning left-to-right through applied filter chips and over to the sort-dropdown on the right—this mirrors the mockup’s two-row navigation scanning pattern. From there, the eye flows diagonally down into the recipes-grid: first to the hero-style top recipe-card (if present), then down through the remaining cards. The filter-sidebar on the left provides a stable, quieter reference column during scrolling, while add-to-planner actions in the cards act as localized focal accents. Finally, the gaze settles on the centered pagination-controls at the bottom, signalling the end of the current batch of content.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width photographic hero and simple navigation bar, the Recipes page content sits in a centered, editorial-style column with wide side gutters. At the top of this column, a small, left-aligned text line and heading from the recipes-page-controller introduce the section with a magazine-like subtlety—something like ‘Recipes · Family favorites for every season’ in a compact, elegant style. A comfortable 24–32px gap leads into a full-width, white control band that stretches across the entire column: on the left, applied filter chips form a light, pill-shaped row, while on the right, a simple sort dropdown anchors the bar, all with generous padding and minimal chrome. Below this control band, the space opens into a two-column layout within the same central column: a slim filter-sidebar on the left and a spacious recipes-grid on the right. The filters are stacked cleanly with plenty of breathing room, while the recipes-grid displays large, image-forward cards in two columns, with the first card optionally spanning the full width as a feature recipe—mimicking the mockup’s bold ‘100 BEST…’ card but applied to recipes. Each card feels like a mini editorial tile rather than a cramped utility card. Under the grid, pagination controls are centered with generous vertical spacing, providing a soft visual stopping point. Throughout, the design remains bright, warm, and uncluttered, guiding users gently from context to controls to content and finally to navigation between pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipes-page-controller sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global navigation bar and centered within the overall page width. It spans the full width of the central column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport), leaving equal white gutters on both sides. The top margin from the nav bar is around 32–40px, providing a clear visual separation. Within this space, the primary page title (e.g., ‘Recipes’) is left-aligned and set in a large editorial type, with a smaller, lighter subtitle or meta line above or below it (e.g., ‘Family classics and weeknight favorites’ or ‘326 recipes’). The overall height of this controller block is modest—about 1/6 of the visible main-content area on a typical laptop—so it feels like a refined section label rather than another hero. The bottom margin of ~24–32px separates it from the applied-filters-bar below, maintaining a distinct but connected hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrolling page; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down, without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar begins just below the applied-filters-bar, aligned to the left edge of the main content column. It occupies roughly 28–32% of the central column’s width and extends vertically along the left side of the results area. The top of the sidebar aligns with the top row of the recipes-grid to its right, creating a clean horizontal baseline. There is a consistent horizontal gap (around 24–32px) between the sidebar and the grid, ensuring the two columns feel related but clearly separated. Inside the sidebar, filters are arranged in a single vertical stack with sections (e.g., ‘Meal type’, ‘Dietary needs’, ‘Time’) separated by 24px of vertical space and section headings left-aligned. Each filter control (checkbox, chip group, slider) has internal padding of about 16–24px so nothing feels cramped. The sidebar’s background matches the page (white) with perhaps a very subtle dividing line at its right edge instead of a heavy border or shadow, preserving the flat, editorial look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the sidebar is sticky relative to the viewport: once scrolled into view, it remains pinned near the top (e.g., 80–96px below the global nav) while the recipes-grid scrolls beside it, as long as there is content. On smaller screens, it collapses into a hidden panel accessed via a ‘Filter’ button in the control band, sliding over the content as a full-width or edge-to-edge drawer when opened.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-bar is positioned directly below the recipes-page-controller and spans the full width of the central content column, functioning as a horizontal control band. It is centered with the column, with equal side gutters, and has a white background that subtly contrasts with the hero above due to a thin bottom border line in a light gray rather than a shadow. The bar has vertical padding of roughly 12–16px and horizontal padding of about 24px, making it feel open and breathable. Within the bar, applied filter chips are left-aligned and flow horizontally; if they overflow, they wrap to a second line within the bar while maintaining the same padding. When no filters are active, the left side shows a quiet hint text such as ‘No filters applied’ in a muted tone. The bar’s height is therefore flexible but typically remains compact—1 to 2 chip rows tall. There is about 24px of space below the bar before the filter-sidebar and recipes-grid begin, ensuring it reads as a separate, navigational-like strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The bar is sticky to the top of the main content area: as the user scrolls down past the recipes-page-controller, the applied-filters-bar anchors itself just beneath the global navigation and remains visible while the filter-sidebar and recipes-grid move underneath. This mirrors the behavior of a primary navigation bar and keeps filter feedback in constant view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-dropdown lives inside the same horizontal band as the applied-filters-bar, aligned to the far right within that band. Horizontally, it shares the bar’s total width but is visually anchored to the right edge, leaving the left and middle of the bar for filter chips. Vertically, it is centered within the bar’s height so that the dropdown label and arrow icon sit on the same baseline as the filter chips. On wide screens, the sort control occupies only a small fraction of space (~15–20% of the bar’s width) and reads as a compact, unobtrusive control; on narrower screens, if space is tight, it can drop onto its own line within the bar, still right-aligned. The dropdown uses minimal chrome: a simple text label (e.g., ‘Sort by: Most popular’) with a chevron and a subtle bottom or surrounding outline, rather than a heavy box, maintaining the clean, editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It shares the sticky behavior of the applied-filters-bar because it is contained within it. As the bar pins to the top during scrolling, the sort-dropdown remains accessible in the top-right of the content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipes-grid is positioned to the right of the filter-sidebar, beginning immediately below the applied-filters-bar with about 24px of vertical spacing. Its left edge is aligned just to the right of the sidebar, separated by a consistent horizontal gap of about 24–32px. The grid occupies the remaining width of the central column (approximately 68–72%). On desktop, it is a two-column layout of large cards: each column takes roughly half of the grid width minus internal gutters. Horizontal gaps between columns are around 24px, while vertical gaps between rows are around 32px, reinforcing the airy editorial feel. The very first recipe-card in this grid may be styled as a feature, spanning the full grid width (both columns) and slightly taller, so it visually echoes the full-bleed hero article card in the mockup. The grid extends vertically as long as there are results, with the page background remaining white and uninterrupted; there are no harsh boxes around the entire grid, only the individual cards providing structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the layout: it scrolls as a continuous block of cards. On smaller screens, the grid naturally collapses to a single column centered under the control band, with the filter-sidebar moving to an overlay or stacked position above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each recipe-card is a large, image-forward tile inside the recipes-grid. In the standard grid rows, a card spans one column and is as wide as that column allows (roughly 48–50% of the grid width on desktop). The card background is white with a subtle border radius of about 8–12px and no visible shadow, adhering to the flat aesthetic. The top 55–65% of the card is occupied by a full-bleed recipe image with rounded corners that match or slightly inset from the card’s radius. Below the image, there is an internal content area with approximately 20–24px of padding on all sides. The recipe title is left-aligned, takes 1–2 lines, and is set in a bold editorial type. Beneath it, muted metadata (e.g., ‘30 min · Serves 4 · Kid-friendly’) is arranged in a single or double line with generous line spacing. At the bottom-right corner of this content area sits the add-to-planner-action control, aligned horizontally with any secondary actions on the left (such as ‘View recipe’), leaving breathing room around it. The featured version of the card (typically the first in the grid) spans both columns, making it nearly double the width of a regular card and slightly taller, with a proportionally larger image and headline—this recreates the mockup’s large ‘100 BEST…’ style feature card within the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Cards are fluid within the grid, reflowing as the grid changes columns with viewport size. They do not have fixed or sticky behavior; they animate only through standard hover states (e.g., a subtle image zoom or tint) without adding extra shadows or heavy chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed directly beneath the recipes-grid, centered within the main content column. There is a generous vertical gap (about 40–48px) between the last row of recipe-cards and the pagination area to clearly mark the end of the current list. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal strip consisting of a ‘Previous’ button, a small row of page numbers, and a ‘Next’ button, all aligned on a single baseline. They take up only the central part of the column (roughly 40–50% of its width), leaving whitespace on both sides so they feel light and unobtrusive. Buttons use the same minimalist styling as other controls: flat, with accent-colored text or subtle outlines rather than heavy fills. There is additional bottom padding (around 48–64px) below the pagination before the end of the page or footer, giving the layout a relaxed, finished feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and non-sticky; pagination remains at the bottom of the list and only appears once the user scrolls through the current set of recipes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-planner-action appears as a compact, text-plus-icon control consistently positioned within each recipe-card’s lower-right content area. It is right-aligned inside the card, horizontally aligned with any secondary action on the left, and sits above the card’s bottom padding by about 8–12px to avoid feeling cramped. The control itself is sized as a small pill or button—approximately the width of the text label plus an icon (e.g., a calendar or list icon) with 8–12px horizontal padding and 6–8px vertical padding. It uses the brand’s accent color for the text or border to subtly stand out without overwhelming the card: in the default state, it might be an outlined pill with accent-colored text; on hover, it can fill with the accent color while the text turns white. The positioning ensures it is clearly associated with that specific recipe and easy to scan as users move down the grid. On small screens, it maintains the same relative position at the bottom-right of the card’s text area, stacking above the lower edge with adequate spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each recipe-card; it scrolls naturally with the card and does not float independently. If desired, when a user taps or clicks it, a transient toast or subtle bottom-sheet could appear to confirm the recipe has been added to the planner, but the control itself remains anchored in place within the card.

name: Recipe Detail
title: Recipe Detail
description: Displays full details for a single recipe, lets the user save it to a collection, add it to a planner, and explore related recipes.

core_features: Load and display full recipe details (image, title, description, meal type, time, servings, ingredients, instructions, nutrition summary, tags, ratings) using getRecipeDetail with the recipe ID from the URL.
Display related recipes returned by getRecipeDetail to encourage discovery, with navigation into each related recipe detail via URL parameters.

Provide a Save to Collection flow that lists the user’s recipe collections using getRecipeCollectionsOverview and calls saveRecipeToCollection to either add the recipe to an existing collection or create a new one and add the recipe to it.
Offer navigation to the Saved Lists page after saving to a collection, passing the created or selected collection ID via URL parameters.

Provide an Add to Planner flow that lets the user choose a day of the week and then navigates to the Planner page with the selected plan and day encoded in the URL parameters (the actual scheduling is handled on planner.html).
Provide a clear way to return to the recipes listing or previous context via a Back link that uses URL parameters (e.g., a 'from' parameter) and/or the browser back button.
user_workflows: View a recipe: 1) User opens recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId} from recipes.html, index.html, planner.html, or saved_lists.html. 2) The page reads the id parameter and calls getRecipeDetail(recipeId). 3) On success, the page shows recipe header (title, description, image, basic meta), ingredients, instructions, and nutrition summary. 4) The relatedRecipes from the response are shown as a list of recipe cards; clicking any card navigates to recipe_detail.html?id={relatedRecipeId}.

Save recipe to an existing collection: 1) On the recipe detail page, user clicks a Save to Collection button. 2) The page opens a Save to Collection dialog and calls getRecipeCollectionsOverview() if collection data is not already loaded. 3) The dialog lists all recipe collections with their names and item counts. 4) User selects one collection and confirms Save. 5) The page calls saveRecipeToCollection with recipeId from URL, collectionId from the user’s selection, and no collectionName. 6) On success, the dialog shows a confirmation state including the message and updated collectionItemCount, and offers a View collection action. 7) If the user clicks View collection, the page navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=recipe_collection&listId={collection.id}.

Create a new collection and save recipe: 1) User clicks Save to Collection on the recipe detail page. 2) In the dialog, user chooses the option to create a new collection, entering a collection name. 3) User submits the form. 4) The page calls saveRecipeToCollection with recipeId from URL, collectionId omitted or null, and collectionName set to the entered name. 5) On success, the UI shows confirmation and the newly created collection name and updated item count. 6) User can click View collection to navigate to saved_lists.html?listType=recipe_collection&listId={collection.id}, or close the dialog to stay on the recipe.

Add recipe to a planner plan: 1) User clicks Add to Planner on the recipe detail page. 2) An Add to Planner dialog opens, letting the user choose a day of week (e.g., using the dayOfWeek enum values) and optionally confirm or pick a plan if a planId is present in the URL. 3) When the user confirms, the page navigates to planner.html with URL parameters including planId={selectedPlanId} and optionally recipeId={currentRecipeId} and dayOfWeek={selectedDay}. 4) The planner page, using its own interfaces, reads these parameters and adds or pre-fills the recipe into the selected plan and day.

Return to recipes or previous context: 1) When navigating into recipe_detail.html, the linking page can optionally include a from parameter indicating the source page (for example, from=recipes, from=planner, from=saved_lists). 2) The recipe detail page displays a Back link whose target depends on the from parameter (e.g., recipes.html if from=recipes). 3) If no from parameter is provided, the Back link either uses a default (such as recipes.html) or relies on the browser back button, which returns the user to their previous page.
interactions: Page load and data fetch: On initial load or whenever the id URL parameter changes, the page triggers a data loading interaction that calls getRecipeDetail with recipeId taken from the id parameter. The page shows a loading state until the call returns or an error state if the call fails.

Back navigation interaction: A Back link or button reads any from URL parameter to determine where to send the user (e.g., recipes.html, planner.html, or saved_lists.html). Clicking it navigates using standard URL-based navigation without additional API calls.

Save to Collection interaction: Clicking the Save to Collection button opens a dialog. Opening the dialog triggers getRecipeCollectionsOverview if collection data is not yet available or needs refresh. Inside the dialog, the user can select an existing collection (e.g., via radio buttons or a dropdown) or switch to a new collection form with a text input for collectionName. Submitting the dialog triggers saveRecipeToCollection with the appropriate parameters.

Save to Collection success and follow-up interaction: After saveRecipeToCollection returns success, the dialog updates to show feedback (using the returned message and collectionItemCount). The dialog also exposes a View collection action; clicking this navigates to saved_lists.html with listType=recipe_collection and listId set to the returned collection.id.

Add to Planner interaction: Clicking the Add to Planner button opens a dialog that allows the user to pick a dayOfWeek value and, when applicable, confirm or select a planId (for example, pre-filled from the URL if present). Confirming the dialog constructs a URL to planner.html with at least planId and may include recipeId and dayOfWeek parameters. Navigation is then performed to that URL; no planner APIs are called on this page.

Related recipe navigation interaction: Clicking any related recipe card or link reads that recipe’s id and navigates to recipe_detail.html?id={relatedRecipeId}&from=recipe_detail (or preserves existing from if appropriate). This triggers a fresh call to getRecipeDetail for the new id.

Servings adjustment interaction (optional functional behavior): If a servings control is present, user changes the servings value via input or plus/minus controls. This updates a derived view of ingredient quantities client-side. No APIs are called; the underlying Recipe data remains unchanged.

Error handling interaction: If getRecipeDetail fails (for example, invalid id), the page shows an error message and may display a link back to recipes.html so the user can recover. If getRecipeCollectionsOverview or saveRecipeToCollection fail, the dialog shows an error state and allows retry or cancel.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by URL parameters; no localStorage or similar client storage is used for cross-page state. The required parameter id (string) is read from the query string (recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId}). If id is missing or empty, the page shows an error and does not call getRecipeDetail. When id changes (for example, via navigation to a related recipe), the page re-invokes getRecipeDetail with the new recipeId and re-renders all recipe-bound components. An optional from parameter can be provided (for example, from=recipes, from=index, from=planner, from=saved_lists) to control the behavior of the Back link, determining which page and URL to navigate to when the user clicks Back. Additional optional parameters can refine behavior: collectionId can be passed in to indicate a currently relevant collection (e.g., when arriving from saved_lists.html); components may use this to preselect a collection in the Save to Collection dialog. A planId parameter can be passed when arriving from planner.html to indicate that the user is viewing the recipe in the context of a specific planner plan; the Add to Planner dialog can then default to that plan when constructing the navigation back to planner.html. When the user chooses View collection after saving, the page navigates to saved_lists.html with URL parameters listType=recipe_collection and listId equal to the returned collection.id, allowing saved_lists.html to determine which list to show. When the user confirms the Add to Planner dialog, the page navigates to planner.html with planId set to the chosen plan ID and may also include recipeId (current recipe ID) and dayOfWeek (selected day), so that planner.html can interpret and apply the requested scheduling. All persistent cross-page behaviors (recipe selection, navigation context, list and plan references) are expressed via URL parameters, while transient UI states like open dialogs, loading spinners, and input field contents are managed within the page’s runtime state.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the id and optional context parameters from the URL, calls getRecipeDetail(recipeId) to load the main recipe and relatedRecipes, and coordinates loading and error states for dependent components.

Displays the recipe title, description, primary image, meal type, and any rating summary. Provides a Back link that uses the from URL parameter when available and falls back to a default recipes listing URL when not.

Shows recipe metadata such as prepTimeMinutes, cookTimeMinutes, totalTimeMinutes, servings, caloriesPerServing, healthTags, and tags in a concise summary section. Optionally includes controls to adjust the displayed servings, recalculating ingredient quantities in the ingredients component without changing the underlying Recipe data.

Displays the list of ingredients for the recipe using recipe.ingredients and current servings context. If a servings adjustment is active, this component recalculates display quantities based on the ratio between original recipe.servings and the selected serving count (computation performed locally, not via API).

Shows the ordered preparation steps from recipe.instructions, clearly numbered. Provides basic interactions such as marking a step as in-progress or completed for the current viewing session (client-side only).

Displays recipe.nutritionSummary and key nutritional metrics such as caloriesPerServing when available. If nutritionSummary is empty, this component can remain hidden or show a simple note that no nutrition summary is available.
Provides a Save to Collection button in the recipe detail page. When clicked, it opens the Save to Collection dialog component.

Implements the flow for saving the current recipe into a recipe collection. On first open, it calls getRecipeCollectionsOverview() to load the user’s recipe collections with item counts. The user can select an existing collection or choose to create a new collection by providing a collectionName. On submission, it calls saveRecipeToCollection with recipeId derived from the current URL, and either a selected collectionId or a new collectionName. After a successful save, it displays confirmation using the returned message and collectionItemCount and provides a View collection action which navigates to saved_lists.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Provides an Add to Planner button on the recipe detail page. Clicking it opens the Add to Planner dialog component where the user can choose how to send this recipe to the planner.

Allows the user to set basic scheduling context before navigating to the planner. The dialog reads any existing planId from the URL to preselect a plan when the user comes from planner.html context. It lets the user choose a dayOfWeek (using the allowed enum values) and confirm. On confirmation, it constructs a URL to planner.html including at least planId (selected or provided) and may add recipeId and dayOfWeek as query parameters, then navigates to that URL so that planner.html can handle adding the recipe to the selected plan.

Displays the relatedRecipes array returned by getRecipeDetail as a set of recipe cards or links. Each entry shows core identifying information (e.g., title, image, and basic meta) and links to its own recipe detail page using recipe_detail.html?id={relatedRecipeId}, preserving or setting a suitable from parameter. Clicking a related recipe triggers navigation and, therefore, a new getRecipeDetail call for that id.

Manages and displays error or empty states for the page. If getRecipeDetail returns an error or the id URL parameter is invalid, it shows an appropriate message and may show a link back to recipes.html. For collection-related actions, it displays relevant error messages within the Save to Collection dialog.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main content column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with a strong, photographic recipe hero at the top (recipe-header), followed directly by a full-width meta/actions band (recipe-meta-summary plus the save/add triggers) that behaves like the mockup's navigation bar. Below that, stack long-form content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: ingredients-list, instructions-list, nutrition-summary, then the related-recipes-list as the closing section. Loader and error states occupy this same central column; dialogs float above everything as overlays.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main content column (about 60–70% of viewport width) with a strong, photographic recipe hero at the top (recipe-header), followed directly by a full-width meta/actions band (recipe-meta-summary plus the save/add triggers) that behaves like the mockup's navigation bar. Below that, stack long-form content in a linear top-to-bottom flow: ingredients-list, instructions-list, nutrition-summary, then the related-recipes-list as the closing section. Loader and error states occupy this same central column; dialogs float above everything as overlays.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Let the recipe-header occupy roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport on desktop. Immediately beneath it, allocate a low-profile meta/actions bar at about 8–10% of the viewport height that can become sticky. The remaining vertical space is for the reading experience: each major section (ingredients, instructions, nutrition, related) is separated by 32–48px vertical gaps. Horizontally, keep the main column constrained to ~1100–1200px max with generous left/right gutters (15–20% viewport each side on large screens) and ensure all core components align within this column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a calm, editorial density: few elements per screen, generous white space around each section, and comfortable line spacing. The hero and meta/actions bar are visually bold but not crowded; the lists (ingredients and instructions) use clear vertical rhythm with 8–24px between items/steps. Nutrition and related sections feel like distinct, airy blocks rather than dense tables or grids. Buttons and chips get ample padding and breathing room consistent with the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top to bottom with a gentle, editorial flow: first to the large photographic hero and recipe title, then slightly down to the meta/actions band where users can immediately assess time/servings and act (save/add). From there, the gaze drops into the body of the recipe, introduced by the ingredients section as a subtle section start, then continues to the instructions, which become the new focal area while scrolling. The nutrition-summary offers a contained information block after the main reading, and the related-recipes-list becomes the final visual anchor that invites onward exploration. Sticky behavior of the meta/actions band echoes the mockup’s navigation bar, keeping key actions present without breaking the linear flow.

layout.overall_description: The recipe detail page appears as a warm, editorial article laid out in a single, centered column on a clean white background. At the top of this column, a wide, edge-to-edge photo of the finished dish fills the upper portion of the screen, with the recipe title and a short descriptive line overlaid near the lower-left of the image, evoking a magazine-style feature. Just below this hero image, a slim, full-width white band contains prep and cook details on the left and two prominent action buttons on the right; as users scroll, this band gently pins itself near the top of the viewport, mirroring the mockup’s navigation strip. Below, generous white space leads into the ingredients, presented in a simple, readable list, followed by a similarly spaced, numbered instructions section. Each section feels distinct thanks to consistent spacing and typographic hierarchy rather than heavy lines or boxes. After the steps, a softly framed nutrition card spans the column, and further down, a related recipes section showcases a small row or grid of thumbnail cards with ample breathing room, acting as a visual footer. Dialogs for saving and planning dim the entire page and float centrally when opened, preserving the clean, minimal chrome. Overall, the experience is calm and linear, with strong anchors at the hero, sticky meta/actions bar, and related recipes, all aligned to a restrained, modern family-lifestyle aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loader sits above the entire recipe detail content area, aligned with the centered main column. It appears as a semi-transparent white or very light overlay that covers from the top of the recipe-header down to just above the related-recipes-list area, dimming any underlying content. A spinner or loading indicator is centered horizontally within the column and placed roughly at the vertical center of the viewport, ensuring it is immediately visible regardless of scroll position. The overlay respects the left and right column boundaries (it does not stretch edge-to-edge across the full viewport), preserving the sense of a constrained editorial column even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay anchored to the recipe detail container; typically positioned fixed or absolutely relative to the viewport so it remains centered during scroll while active, and fully hides or blocks interaction with underlying recipe components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipe-header is the first visible component of the recipe detail content, sitting directly beneath any global site masthead or navigation and aligned to the center of the page within the 1100–1200px content width. It spans the full width of this main column, with generous left and right gutters to the viewport edges. Vertically, it occupies roughly the upper third of the first screen on desktop: a large, cinematic photograph of the dish runs full width within the column, with a landscape aspect ratio (around 2:1 or 3:2). The recipe title is positioned over the lower left of the image, in large, bold type, set on top of a subtle gradient or solid overlay band that ensures readability without adding heavy chrome. A short subtitle or description line sits just below the title, aligned with the same left inset (around 24–32px from the image edge), forming a tight text block.
