There is a 24–32px margin below the image before the next component begins, keeping the hero visually distinct and letting it breathe like a magazine opener.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the page; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down, acting as the initial visual anchor but not remaining fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the recipe-header, separated by roughly 24px of vertical white space, the recipe-meta-summary forms a low-profile horizontal band that spans the full width of the main column. It appears on a white background consistent with the page, differentiated primarily by spacing and a subtle bottom border or hairline rule that echoes a navigation bar. On desktop, the left two-thirds of this band is used for concise meta information: preparation time, cook time, total time, servings, difficulty, and possibly tags or meal-type chips laid out in one or two rows. Text is left-aligned with about 16–24px internal padding from the left edge of the column. The content uses small to medium text sizes with a clear hierarchy (labels slightly muted, values slightly bolder), and enough horizontal spacing between items so it feels airy rather than tabular. On narrower screens, meta items wrap into multiple lines while still maintaining the same left alignment and padding. The top of this band sits near the bottom of the initial viewport, and as you scroll, it creates a visual transition from the bold hero to the more functional body sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially sits in normal document flow; once the user scrolls past the hero image, this band (together with its action buttons) becomes sticky and pins itself to just below any global site header, remaining visible at the top of the viewport to provide constant access to key information and actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-to-collection-trigger is placed within the same horizontal band as the recipe-meta-summary, occupying the right side of that strip on desktop. It appears as a primary-styled button, with a warm accent color or subtle filled style, aligned to the far right edge of the main column with about 16–24px padding from the edge. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the meta band, sitting in line with the meta text on the left. There is a comfortable 8–16px horizontal gap between this button and the add-to-planner-trigger to its left or right (depending on ordering), ensuring both are easily distinguishable. On smaller screens, this button can drop beneath the meta text, spanning nearly the full width of the column with generous 12–16px padding and maintaining at least 16px of vertical space above and below. Because it shares the strip with the meta summary, it travels with that band when it becomes sticky, keeping the primary save action always available at the top as the user scrolls the ingredients and instructions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the sticky meta/actions band; it transitions from a standard in-flow button to a persistent, sticky control that remains anchored at the top of the viewport alongside the meta summary when the user scrolls past the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When activated, the save-to-collection-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay above the entire page. A soft, semi-transparent dark scrim covers the viewport from edge to edge, dimming the hero, meta band, and content while still allowing their silhouettes to be seen, reinforcing the sense of context. The dialog itself is positioned in the middle of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, with a max width of about 480–560px (roughly 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop) and a white background with no heavy shadows, just subtle elevation implied by the scrim. It has 24–32px internal padding on all sides. The dialog title is top-left aligned, with the list of user collections or options arranged in a vertical stack below, each row separated by 8–12px to maintain a light, editorial feel. At the bottom, a primary confirmation button and a secondary cancel button sit aligned to the right with ~12–16px spacing. On mobile, the dialog flexes to nearly full width with reduced horizontal margins while maintaining consistent padding and a clear centered position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay centered in the viewport with a full-screen scrim; it does not scroll independently of the viewport unless content within exceeds height, in which case the dialog content may scroll internally while the scrim stays fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-planner-trigger sits directly adjacent to the save-to-collection-trigger within the same meta/actions band, forming a small cluster of primary actions. On desktop, it appears as a secondary-styled button (e.g., outlined or lightly filled) with the same height and typographic treatment as the save button, to create a balanced pair. If save is on the far right, the planner button sits immediately to its left, separated by 8–16px. Both buttons share the same right margin from the column edge, so the pair reads as a unified control zone. On smaller screens, the planner button can stack directly beneath the save button, aligning left or full width with a consistent 8–12px vertical gap. Its proximity to the meta information and save action supports quick planning without visually crowding the top area. Like the save button, it moves with the sticky band, staying visible at the top of the viewport after the user scrolls past the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides with the sticky meta/actions band; becomes a persistent sticky control alongside the save button, anchored to the top of the viewport once the band transitions to sticky state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-planner-dialog mirrors the save-to-collection-dialog in overall placement and styling, maintaining consistency. When invoked, it overlays the entire viewport with the same semi-transparent scrim. The dialog box is centered horizontally and vertically, with a width around 480–560px on desktop, bounded by the main column but visually detached from it via the overlay. It uses generous 24–32px padding, with a title aligned to the top-left, followed by planner-specific controls (such as date selectors or meal slots) arranged in a vertical layout with clear spacing between input groups. The bottom area houses primary and secondary buttons aligned to the right, similar to the save dialog. The content layout within remains uncluttered, using white space and clear labels instead of heavy dividers, aligning with the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered modal overlay with full-screen scrim; behaves identically to the save dialog, remaining positioned in the center of the viewport while the background content is inert.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ingredients-list begins a short distance below the sticky meta/actions band, typically with about 32–40px of vertical white space separating it from the band. It is aligned to the left within the main column and spans the full column width. At the top of this section, a small, understated section label such as 'Ingredients' appears, left-aligned and possibly styled as an overline or small caps, echoing the '2026 Family Treasure' line from the mockup by being short, subtle, and anchored to the left margin. Below this label, the ingredients themselves are presented in a vertical list, each item on its own line with 8–12px spacing between rows. Text is set in a comfortable body size with enough line height to feel open. Any checkboxes or quantity indicators are minimal and aligned consistently to the left, ensuring the overall block reads as a clean column of text. The section has a clear bottom boundary created purely by 32–40px of space before the next section (instructions-list), without heavy borders or boxes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section; it scrolls naturally underneath the sticky meta/actions band, forming the first major content block in the main reading area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The instructions-list is placed immediately after the ingredients-list, separated by a generous 32–40px vertical gap so that each section feels distinct. It is left-aligned within the main column and uses the full column width, mirroring the ingredients section. A 'Instructions' heading or label appears at the top, slightly more prominent than the ingredients label to reinforce the start of the core step-by-step content. Steps are presented as a numbered list, with the numbers aligned in a small gutter on the left and the instruction text forming a clean column to the right, preserving a straight left edge for the text. Each step block has 16–24px of vertical spacing from the next, making the list easy to scan and visually light despite the amount of content. Longer steps wrap within the column width without touching the page edges, and no heavy borders or shaded boxes are used; the separation between steps is handled entirely through spacing. The bottom of this section is marked by another 40px of white space before the nutrition-summary, maintaining the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow block that forms the main reading focus after the user scrolls past the initial hero and ingredients, nestled between the sticky action band above and the nutrition card below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nutrition-summary sits below the instructions-list, separated by approximately 40px of white space. It appears as a distinct, yet understated card-like section that spans the full width of the main column. The background may be a very light tint or remain white with a subtle outline or top border to signal a data block without adding visual heaviness. Inside, there is 24–32px of padding on all sides. A 'Nutrition' heading is left-aligned at the top, followed by a concise set of key values (calories, macronutrients, etc.) arranged in a simple grid: for example, two or three columns on desktop, collapsing into one column on mobile. Each value pair (label and number) has 8–12px of vertical spacing from the next row, and columns have 24px horizontal separation, maintaining the layout’s open and breathable character. The section remains visually quieter than the hero or related recipes, serving as a reference card rather than a visual focal point. Another 40–48px of space below this component separates it from the related-recipes-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow card; it scrolls naturally and does not stick or overlay, acting as a compact informational stop between the main recipe content and the related content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-recipes-list occupies the final section of the recipe detail page, positioned below the nutrition-summary with about 48–64px of vertical spacing to signal a transition from the main recipe to discovery. It aligns with the main column, spanning its full width, and begins with a section heading such as 'More family favorites' or 'Related recipes' left-aligned at the column start. Below the heading, a set of related recipe cards is displayed in a low-density layout: on desktop, this might be a single horizontal row of 3–4 cards, each card taking up roughly one quarter to one third of the column width with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them; on smaller screens, the cards stack into a vertical list or become a horizontally scrollable row with snap behavior. Each card uses a small thumbnail image on top and a short title beneath, with ample padding inside each card, echoing the editorial card style from the mockup. The entire section has additional bottom margin (48–64px) before the page footer, giving the page a soft visual landing instead of an abrupt end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow section at the bottom of the main content; cards may horizontally scroll on smaller viewports but the section itself does not stick or overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When triggered, the error-and-empty-state-handler takes over the main content column, ensuring a clear, singular message. It is centered within the same 1100–1200px max-width column as the normal recipe content and vertically positioned around the center of the viewport. A concise heading (e.g., 'Recipe not found' or 'Something went wrong') appears centered or slightly left-aligned within the column, with supporting text below and any retry or navigation actions arranged in a small, well-spaced button group. All standard recipe components (header, meta, lists, etc.) are either hidden or visually de-emphasized beneath this state so the user is not confused by partial content. There is abundant white space above and below the error message, reflecting the calm editorial style rather than an aggressive alert. On mobile, this block simply fills the available width while maintaining generous padding from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces or overlays the normal content region within the main column in a static, centered position; it does not stick to the viewport but is presented such that it is immediately visible upon page load or error occurrence.

name: Activities
title: Activities
description: Browse, filter, sort, and page through family activities, then open individual activity details to add them to plans or saved lists.

core_features: Load available filter options (age groups, locations, cost types, duration ranges) using getActivityFilterOptions and render them as interactive filters.

List activities using listActivities with parameters derived from URL query string (ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, sortBy, page) and internal configuration (pageSize, tags if used).
Allow users to adjust filters and sorting; update the URL query string accordingly and re-fetch activities via listActivities.
Display activities as clickable cards with key information (title, ageGroup, location, costType, durationMinutes, summary) sourced from the Activity model returned by listActivities.
Navigate to activity_detail.html with the selected activity id (activity_detail.html?id={activity.id}) when a user selects an activity card.
Provide pagination controls that map to the page URL parameter and the listActivities page parameter, using totalCount returned from listActivities.

Support scanning and light multi-selection of activities within the results list so users can quickly open multiple activity details (e.g., sequentially or via multiple card clicks) to add them into plans or lists from the detail page.
Optionally forward contextual parameters (such as planner context if present in the URL) when constructing navigation links to planner.html, without hardcoding specific values.
user_workflows:
Arrive with preselected filters from another page:
1. User clicks an Activities-related link from index.html or planner.html that includes one or more of the URL parameters ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes.
2. On loading activities.html, the page reads existing query parameters and initializes its filter state from them.
3. The page calls getActivityFilterOptions with no parameters to retrieve the available filter values and user-facing labels.
4. The filter bar component renders controls using the returned options and sets their selected values according to the URL parameters.
5. The data loader component calls listActivities with ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, sortBy, page (if present) and an internally configured pageSize.
6. The results list displays the returned activities and shows pagination based on totalCount.
7. The user can refine filters or sorting; each change updates the URL query string and triggers listActivities again, updating the results.

Refine activities and open a specific activity detail:
1. User changes one or more filters (e.g., selects a different age group or location) in the filter bar.
2. The filter bar component updates the in-memory filter state, then updates the URL query string to reflect the new filter values while resetting page to the first page and preserving unrelated existing query parameters.
3. The data loader observes the changed URL state and calls listActivities with the updated parameters.
4. The results list re-renders with the new activities from the API response.
5. The user clicks an activity card or its action link.
6. The page navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activity.id}, passing the Activity.id value as the id URL parameter.

Scan and select multiple activities before using their detail pages to add to plans/lists:
1. User browses the results list; each card includes a selectable control (e.g., checkbox or toggle) to mark it as "selected" in the current view.
2. When the user selects or deselects a card, the selection toolbar updates its internal list of selected activity ids; this selection is kept in page state (not persisted in URL or storage).
3. The user can move through pages and continue selecting activities on each page; selection scope is defined by the implementation (e.g., either limited to the current page or maintained across pages if desired, but not stored in localStorage).
4. From the selection toolbar or by clicking individual cards, the user opens each selected activity detail in activity_detail.html (one by one or via multiple clicks).
5. In each activity_detail.html page, the user uses page-specific controls (outside the scope of this design) to add the activity to an ActivityList or PlannerPlan.
6. If contextual navigation to planner.html is available (e.g., from within the detail page), the user is redirected with an appropriate planId as defined in that page’s design.

Paginate and sort results:
1. User adjusts the sorting control (e.g., chooses a different sort order).
2. The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and resets page to the first page, preserving other query parameters; the data loader calls listActivities with the new sortBy.
3. The user navigates between pages via pagination controls.
4. Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and triggers listActivities with the corresponding page value.
5. The results list updates to show activities for the selected page, and pagination controls update their active state based on current page and totalCount.
interactions: Filter changes: When the user selects or clears a filter (ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes), the filter bar updates the corresponding URL parameters, clears the page parameter, and triggers a reload of the activities list via listActivities.

Sort changes: When the user changes the sort option, the sort control writes the selected sortBy value to the URL parameters, resets page to the initial page, and triggers listActivities with the new sortBy.

Pagination: When the user clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number), the pagination component updates the page URL parameter and calls listActivities with the updated page value.
Activity card click: When the user clicks an activity card (or its primary action) in the results list, the page navigates to activity_detail.html with id set to the clicked Activity.id.
Activity card keyboard selection: When the user focuses an activity card and activates it via keyboard (e.g., Enter or Space key), the same navigation to activity_detail.html?id={activity.id} occurs.

Selection toggle on cards: When the user toggles the selection control on an activity card, the card emits a selection change event with its Activity.id; the selection toolbar maintains a collection of selected ids and updates its own state (e.g., enabled/disabled actions, count display).

Selection toolbar actions: When the user chooses an action from the selection toolbar (e.g., to iterate through selected activities), the toolbar triggers navigation events to each activity_detail.html?id={activityId} as the user chooses them; selection state remains in memory for the current page session.

Contextual navigation to planner (if applicable): If an "Add to Planner" contextual link is provided (e.g., as part of page configuration or card actions), clicking it navigates to planner.html with planId set to a value determined by the existing context (not hardcoded in this design).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Activities page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for list state (filters, sorting, pagination). On initial load, the page reads the following parameters from the query string: ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, sortBy, page. If any of these parameters are present, their values are used directly as inputs when calling listActivities; if absent, the page uses its default internal state or configuration for those parameters but does not hardcode specific values in this specification.

When getActivityFilterOptions resolves, the filter bar maps each URL parameter value to one of the available options (e.g., matching the .value property of the returned ageGroups, locations, costTypes, and durationOptionsMinutes). If a URL parameter does not correspond to any option, the component can treat it as "no selection" or a fallback state without persisting invalid values.

Any user interaction that changes filters or sorting updates the URL query string using the browser history API (e.g., pushing or replacing state) so that the current filter/sort/pagination state is always reflected in the URL. Specifically: ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, and sortBy are set or cleared to match the current selections; page is reset when filters or sorting change, and updated when the user changes pages.

The listActivities interface is always called with parameters derived from the current URL state: ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, sortBy, and page are passed directly from the URL (or from the computed default state if the corresponding URL parameter is absent). tags and pageSize are provided by internal configuration or higher-level context, not hardcoded in this specification. totalCount from the listActivities response is used to compute the available pages and to validate that the current page parameter remains within a valid range; if the current page exceeds the maximum based on totalCount, the implementation may adjust the page parameter and update the URL accordingly.

Any unrecognized or additional query parameters present in the URL (for example, contextual parameters relevant to other pages) are preserved when the Activities page updates the URL during interactions, ensuring that external context is not lost. Selected activities (for multi-selection) are stored in in-memory component state only and are not persisted to the URL or any client-side storage.

components.functionality: Initializes filter controls using options returned from getActivityFilterOptions and current values from the URL (ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes). On user changes, it updates the URL query string to reflect the selected filters, resets the page parameter, and triggers a refresh of the activities list.

Manages the sortBy state for the activities list. Reads the initial sortBy value from the URL, exposes one or more sort options derived from application configuration, and updates the sortBy URL parameter upon user selection while resetting the page parameter and triggering listActivities.

Orchestrates data fetching for the Activities page. Watches the URL query string for changes to ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, sortBy, and page. Whenever these change (including on initial load), it calls listActivities with the current values plus internally configured tags and pageSize, then passes the returned activities and totalCount to the results list and pagination components.

Displays activities as a list or grid of cards using the Activity objects from listActivities. Each card renders key Activity fields (e.g., title, ageGroup, location, costType, durationMinutes, summary) and includes actions for viewing details and toggling selection. Card clicks or keyboard activation navigate to activity_detail.html?id={activity.id}. Selection state changes are emitted to the selection toolbar.

Renders pagination based on totalCount from listActivities and the current page and pageSize. When the user selects a page (next, previous, or specific), it updates the page URL parameter and triggers listActivities via the data loader.

Maintains and displays the set of selected activity ids based on events from the results list. Shows the count of selected activities and provides actions for users to work through selected items (e.g., by sequentially opening their detail pages). When an action is triggered, it navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activityId} for the relevant activity without modifying backend data directly.

If the page or a specific card is configured with a planner context (e.g., a known plan identifier from outside this specification), this component exposes an "Add to Planner" navigation action. When triggered, it navigates to planner.html with planId set to the contextual value, allowing planner.html to handle the actual scheduling logic.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, linear, top-to-bottom flow within the main content column, which is centered on the page and constrained to roughly 1100–1200px. Above this sequence, the global full-bleed hero and the full-width primary site navigation remain as in the mockup. Within the Activities page content, the order runs: page/tagline label and editorial intro (not part of this task), then the activities-filter-bar as the first interactive band, followed by the activities-sort-control aligned to the top-right of the results area, then the activities-results-list (with the activities-list-data-loader occupying the same space while loading), followed by the activities-pagination-controls at the bottom. The activities-selection-toolbar and activities-contextual-planner-link are layered contextually on top of this flow: the planner link is placed near the top of the content stack, while the selection toolbar appears fixed at the bottom of the viewport when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, linear, top-to-bottom flow within the main content column, which is centered on the page and constrained to roughly 1100–1200px. Above this sequence, the global full-bleed hero and the full-width primary site navigation remain as in the mockup. Within the Activities page content, the order runs: page/tagline label and editorial intro (not part of this task), then the activities-filter-bar as the first interactive band, followed by the activities-sort-control aligned to the top-right of the results area, then the activities-results-list (with the activities-list-data-loader occupying the same space while loading), followed by the activities-pagination-controls at the bottom. The activities-selection-toolbar and activities-contextual-planner-link are layered contextually on top of this flow: the planner link is placed near the top of the content stack, while the selection toolbar appears fixed at the bottom of the viewport when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main Activities content uses the central 60–70% of the viewport width, with wide left and right gutters (about 15–20% each) that stay mostly empty on desktop, echoing the mockup’s editorial feel. Vertically, the space from the bottom of the hero/navigation to the start of the filter bar is generous (about 32–40px), and each major block (filters band, results list, pagination, selection toolbar) is separated by 24–32px of white space. The filter bar is treated like the mockup’s navigation band: a strong, full-width strip inside the content column. The results list takes up most of the remaining vertical space, with pagination occupying a relatively small band at the very bottom. The selection toolbar, when visible, occupies a shallow horizontal strip at the bottom of the viewport, about 64–80px tall, constrained to the same content width and floating above the page content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low-density, editorial layout: large swaths of white space, single-column stacking of activity cards, and generous padding inside each control. The filter bar uses roomy chips and controls with 16–24px horizontal padding and 12–16px vertical padding, similar to the mockup’s navigation categories. The results list favors a calm reading experience: one card per row (or visually tall rows), 24–32px vertical spacing between items, and no tight grids. The sort control, pagination, and planner link are compact but spaced so they never feel crowded, maintaining at least 16–24px breathing room around them.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After users land on the Activities page and take in the hero and top navigation, their eye moves down to the small Activities label and any editorial lead-in, then immediately to the filter bar band, which echoes the mockup’s category navigation and becomes the primary interactive anchor. From there, the gaze naturally shifts to the upper-right where the sort control sits, then drops down into the vertical stack of activity cards. As users scroll, the list and pagination continue this downward flow. When items are selected, the fixed selection toolbar appears at the bottom of the viewport, creating a secondary horizontal focal area that gently pulls the eye down without overwhelming the main list. The contextual planner link is placed near the top of the content column so that planning remains visible but secondary to the act of browsing.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width hero and the warm, minimal top navigation, the Activities page content begins within a centered, 12-column grid that is constrained to about 1100–1200px wide, leaving wide, quiet gutters on both sides. A small, left-aligned label and brief editorial intro set the tone, followed by a full-width filter bar strip that spans the entire content column like a secondary navigation, with spacious, tap-friendly controls. Just beneath and aligned to the right edge of this same column, a subtle sort control anchors the top of the results zone. The activity cards then flow downward in a single, generous column, each card nearly as wide as the content area and separated by comfortable white space, creating an easy, magazine-like browsing experience. At the bottom of the list, centered pagination controls appear with ample breathing room above and below. When the user selects activities, a slim, flat selection toolbar docks itself at the bottom of the viewport, perfectly aligned with the content column, while a contextual link to the planner near the top of the page reinforces how browsing ties into planning without adding visual clutter. The overall effect is a clean, calm, family-friendly layout that channels the mockup’s editorial hero and navigation patterns into a focused browsing and planning experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-filter-bar sits near the top of the Activities content area, directly below the editorial intro and label, occupying the full width of the central content column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport, centered). It appears as a distinct horizontal band with a white or very light background, echoing the mockup’s navigation bar: about 64px tall by default, expanding vertically if filters wrap to multiple rows. Internally, filter chips and controls are arranged from left to right, with 16–24px padding on the left and right edges and 12–16px top and bottom padding, ensuring generous spacing and no visual crowding. On smaller screens, the content can wrap to a second row or horizontally scroll within this band, maintaining the feeling of a soft, category-style navigation. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical white space above and below the bar, clearly separating it from both the editorial teaser above and the sort/results block below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll, spanning the central grid; contents may wrap or horizontally scroll within the constrained content width on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-sort-control is positioned immediately below the filter bar, within the same central content column, but aligned to the right edge to create a subtle tension and a natural visual step-down. Horizontally, it occupies roughly the rightmost 25–35% of the content column, leaving open white space to its left, which keeps the layout light and airy. Vertically, it sits in a slim band about 40–48px tall, with about 8–16px of vertical space above it (separating it from the filter bar) and about 16–24px below it before the first activity card. The control itself can be a compact dropdown or segmented toggle, but with 8–12px internal padding and clear labeling in line with the bold, clean typography of the mockup. On narrow viewports, this control can center-align above the first card, maintaining a consistent top-of-results position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing with the page content; right-aligned within the central grid on desktop, center-aligned on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-list-data-loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the activities-results-list, effectively taking over the results area while data is loading. It is centered within the main content column and matches its width, beginning immediately below the sort control with about 16–24px of vertical separation. Vertically, it extends down the page to show a stack of placeholder cards or a large central loading indicator, maintaining the single-column, editorial rhythm: each skeleton card is nearly full-width, with 24–32px of white space between them to mirror the eventual card spacing. The loader does not introduce new chrome or separate bands; it feels like a temporary, low-contrast wash over where the results will appear, preserving the calm atmosphere and avoiding visual jitter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and transient; it appears in place of the results list area and scrolls naturally with the page while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-results-list begins directly beneath the sort control with approximately 16–24px of vertical whitespace, and stretches across the full width of the central content column (aligned to the 12-column grid, about 1100–1200px max). Activities are presented as a single vertical column of large, editorial-style cards: each card is nearly full-width within the content column, with 24–32px of vertical spacing between cards to maintain a relaxed reading rhythm. The list extends downward as far as needed, pushing pagination toward the bottom of the viewport. The left and right edges of the cards align cleanly with the filter bar and sort control, reinforcing a strong, centered column. On smaller screens, cards maintain full-width behavior relative to the viewport, with spacing scaled down proportionally but still aligned to the 8px spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content region; cards stack vertically and the list grows downward, driving page height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the results list, centered horizontally within the content column and aligned to the same left/right margins as the cards. There is a generous 24–32px gap above the pagination row to clearly mark the end of the current batch of results, and 32–40px of white space below before the page footer or next section. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal strip—previous/next and page numbers—occupying roughly the middle 40–60% of the content width, leaving soft white space to either side. Buttons and page numbers follow the minimal, flat treatment with 8–12px internal padding and clear but unobtrusive typography, ensuring they are visible but do not overpower the list above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and positioned at the bottom of the results content; it scrolls out of view as the user moves up the page and reappears when they scroll down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-selection-toolbar appears only when one or more activities are selected, as a slim, flat bar anchored to the bottom of the viewport, visually echoing a quiet utility strip rather than a heavy panel. Horizontally, the toolbar is centered and constrained to the same width as the main content column, aligning its left and right edges with the cards and filter bar, while leaving the outer gutters untouched. It is approximately 64–80px tall, with 16–20px of padding top and bottom, and uses a subtle background tint or accent color that contrasts gently with the main page background while remaining in the warm, family-friendly palette. Within the bar, the selection count is left-aligned, and primary actions (e.g., add to plan, save list) are right-aligned, creating a clear visual balance. A small 8–16px gap separates the toolbar from the bottom of the viewport if desired, but no heavy shadows are used—at most a fine top border—to remain consistent with the minimal chrome of the mockup. When visible, it overlaps the lower portion of the content and pagination, which remain scrollable underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport while active, constrained to the central grid width; appears and disappears contextually based on selection state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities-contextual-planner-link is positioned near the top of the Activities content area, just above or to the right of the filter bar, so it is visible early in the vertical flow without competing for attention. On desktop, it can be right-aligned within the content column in the same horizontal row as a small section label (e.g., 'Activities'), occupying a narrow slice (about 20–25% of the column width) and leaving generous whitespace around it. Vertically, it sits 16–24px above the filter bar, acting like a subtle, text-based call-to-action such as 'Open Family Planner' or 'View My Plans.' The styling is understated—simple text link or ghost button with 8–12px padding and no heavy borders—so that it reads as a convenient pathway rather than a primary focal element. On smaller screens, it can shift to center-align above the filter bar, maintaining separation with 16px of vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element; it scrolls with the page and remains near the top of the Activities content stack.

name: Activity Detail
title: Activity Detail
description: Displays full information for a single activity and lets the user save it to activity lists or open it in the Planner, as well as browse related activities.

core_features: Load and display a single activity’s full details (title, summary, description, age group, location, cost, duration, materials, instructions, tags) using getActivityDetail based on the URL parameter id.
Display related activities (from getActivityDetail.relatedActivities) and allow navigation to their own Activity Detail pages via URL parameters.
Allow the user to add the current activity to an existing activity list or create a new list and add it in one step, using getActivityListsOverview and saveActivityToList.
After successfully adding to a list, provide navigation to the Saved Lists page (saved_lists.html) with appropriate URL parameters (listType and listId).
Provide an Add to Planner action that navigates to planner.html with the current activity context and optional planId from the URL, so scheduling can be completed in the Planner.
Support back navigation to the Activities listing (activities.html) or previous page via a Back link and browser back button.
user_workflows: View activity details: 1) User arrives on activity_detail.html?id={activityId}. 2) Page reads the id parameter and calls getActivityDetail(activityId=id). 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the page displays all activity fields and relatedActivities. 5) If getActivityDetail fails or returns no activity, an error message is shown instead of the detail content.

Save activity to an existing list: 1) On the Activity Detail page, user clicks the Save to list button in the actions bar. 2) The page opens a dialog and calls getActivityListsOverview if list data has not been loaded yet in this session. 3) The dialog lists all existing activity lists (with name and itemCount). 4) User selects one list and clicks Add. 5) The page calls saveActivityToList with activityId set to the current activity id and listId set to the selected list’s id (listName left empty). 6) On success, the dialog shows a confirmation and updates its in-memory list counts using the returned list and listItemCount or by reloading getActivityListsOverview if desired. 7) User can click View list in the confirmation, which navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=activity_list&listId={returned list.id}.

Create a new list and save activity into it: 1) User clicks Save to list. 2) In the dialog, user chooses the Create new list option. 3) User enters a list name. 4) User clicks Create and add. 5) The page calls saveActivityToList with activityId set to the current activity id and listName set to the entered name (listId left empty). 6) On success, the API returns the new ActivityList and ActivityListItem; the dialog updates its list overview (either by appending the new list from the response or by re-calling getActivityListsOverview). 7) The dialog shows a success message and offers a View list link that navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=activity_list&listId={new list.id}.

Add activity to Planner: 1) User is viewing an activity on activity_detail.html?id={activityId}. Optionally, the URL may also include planId={planId} when opened from planner.html. 2) User clicks the Add to Planner button. 3) The page constructs a navigation URL to planner.html that includes the current activityId as a query parameter (for example, planner.html?addActivityId={activityId}) and, if present in the current URL, the planId (planner.html?planId={planId}&addActivityId={activityId}). 4) The browser navigates to planner.html, where the Planner page (using its own interfaces) allows the user to choose the specific day and completes scheduling of this activity within the selected plan.

Navigate to related activities: 1) In the Related activities section, user sees a list of relatedActivities returned from getActivityDetail. 2) User clicks on a related activity card or title. 3) The browser navigates to activity_detail.html?id={relatedActivityId}. 4) The Activity Detail page re-runs getActivityDetail with the new id and updates all displayed information accordingly.

Back to activities or previous context: 1) User clicks the Back to activities link in the actions bar. 2) If the browser history has a previous page, the link may use a simple browser back action; alternatively, it can explicitly navigate to activities.html (without additional parameters) depending on implementation. 3) User returns to the activities listing or previous page. If the user wants to return to a specific saved list or planner context, they instead use the View list or Add to Planner navigation actions which carry specific URL parameters.
interactions: On initial page load or whenever the id URL parameter changes, call getActivityDetail(activityId=id) and update the Activity Detail and Related Activities components based on the response.
Clicking the Save to list button opens the Add to Activity List dialog and triggers getActivityListsOverview if list data is not yet loaded or is flagged as stale.
Within the Add to Activity List dialog, selecting an existing list and submitting calls saveActivityToList with the currently selected listId and the current activityId.

Within the Add to Activity List dialog, selecting the Create new list option and submitting calls saveActivityToList with listName set from user input and activityId set to the current activity; listId is omitted.

On successful saveActivityToList response, the dialog displays a success state, updates the list overview (either locally from the response or via a fresh getActivityListsOverview call), and exposes a View list action.
Clicking the View list action after a successful save navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=activity_list&listId={returned list.id}.
Clicking the Add to Planner button constructs a planner.html URL including the current activity id (and planId if present in the current URL) and navigates the browser to that URL.
Clicking any related activity item navigates to activity_detail.html?id={relatedActivityId}, causing the page to reload its detail view via getActivityDetail.
Clicking the Back to activities link navigates back to the previous page or directly to activities.html, depending on implementation decisions for history handling.
If getActivityDetail or getActivityListsOverview results in an error, the user can click a Retry action in the error state to re-call the corresponding interface.
If saveActivityToList returns an error (success=false), the dialog displays the message from the response and allows the user to adjust their selection or retry the submission.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state of the Activity Detail page is driven by URL parameters and interface responses. The required URL parameter id identifies which Activity to load; on every change of id in the query string (activity_detail.html?id={activityId}), the page calls getActivityDetail(activityId=id) and refreshes all bound components. If id is missing or invalid, the page shows an error and does not call the interface. An optional URL parameter planId can be included when the page is opened from planner.html to preserve planner context; when present, it is used as a context value that is passed back to planner.html when the user clicks Add to Planner (for example, planner.html?planId={planId}&addActivityId={activityId}). The Add to Planner action always includes the current activity id in the navigation URL so the Planner page knows which activity to schedule. When the user chooses View list after saving to a list, navigation to saved_lists.html includes listType=activity_list and listId set to the list.id from the saveActivityToList response, allowing the Saved Lists page to display the correct list. All transient UI states on the Activity Detail page (such as loading, error, dialog open/closed, selection within the Add to Activity List dialog, and display of confirmation messages) are managed in memory by the page and are not stored in URL parameters or localStorage; however, any cross-page context required by other pages (such as planId or listId) is propagated via the defined URL parameters.

components.functionality: Reads the id parameter from the page URL, calls getActivityDetail(activityId=id), manages loading and error states, and provides the loaded activity and relatedActivities data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered on page load to read the id URL parameter and call getActivityDetail.
onIdChange: Triggered when the id URL parameter changes (for example, after navigation to a related activity), re-calling getActivityDetail with the new id.
onRetryFetch: Triggered when the user clicks a Retry button in an error state, re-calling getActivityDetail with the current id.

components.functionality: Displays the primary fields of the current activity, such as title, summary, description, ageGroup/minAge/maxAge, location, costType/costAmount, durationMinutes, and tags. This component is read-only and updates whenever the bound Activity changes.
Shows the materials array of the current activity as a list of required materials or supplies, updating automatically when the bound Activity changes.
Renders the instructions array of the current activity as an ordered list of steps. Supports basic step-level interactions such as expanding/collapsing long steps if implemented.
event_handlers: onStepToggle: Optional handler if steps can be expanded/collapsed; toggles the visibility state for a given step.

components.functionality: Provides key actions for the current activity: Back to activities, Save to list, and Add to Planner. It coordinates opening the Add to Activity List dialog and constructing navigation URLs to other pages using URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClickBack: Navigates the user back to the previous page (using browser history) or to activities.html if no suitable history entry exists.
onClickOpenListDialog: Opens the add-to-activity-list dialog component, optionally triggering loading of lists via getActivityListsOverview if not already loaded.

onClickAddToPlanner: Reads the current Activity.id and any planId from the URL, then builds and navigates to planner.html with appropriate query parameters (for example, planner.html?addActivityId={activityId} or planner.html?planId={planId}&addActivityId={activityId}).

components.functionality: Allows the user to add the current activity to an existing activity list or to create a new list and add it. When opened, it loads all available activity lists via getActivityListsOverview (if not already loaded), displays them with item counts, and provides an option to create a new list by name. On submit, it calls saveActivityToList with either listId or listName and then shows a success or error state.
event_handlers: onOpen: Triggered when the dialog is opened; checks if activity lists have been loaded. If not, calls getActivityListsOverview to retrieve all lists and their itemCount values.
onSelectExistingList: Updates the dialog’s internal state to record the chosen existing listId when the user selects a list from the overview.
onToggleCreateNewList: Switches the dialog between 'use existing list' and 'create new list' modes; when in create mode, existing list selection is cleared.
onChangeNewListName: Updates the dialog’s internal state with the text entered by the user for a new list name.

onSubmitSaveToList: Validates that either an existing listId is selected or a new listName is provided (but not both). Then calls saveActivityToList with activityId bound to the current Activity.id and either listId or listName set from the dialog state. Handles loading and disabled states during the API call.

onSaveSuccess: Handles a successful saveActivityToList response (success=true) by updating the list overview in memory (using the returned list and listItemCount or optionally re-calling getActivityListsOverview), displaying a success confirmation with the returned message, and exposing the View list action.
onSaveError: Handles a failed saveActivityToList response (success=false) by showing the returned message and allowing the user to change their selection or retry.
onClickViewList: Navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=activity_list&listId={list.id from saveActivityToList response} so the user can see the full list that now includes the activity.
onClose: Closes the dialog and resets transient form fields such as selected list and new list name, without altering the underlying Activity Detail view.

components.functionality: Displays the relatedActivities returned from getActivityDetail as a list of activity summaries. Each item links to the corresponding Activity Detail page via the id URL parameter.
event_handlers: onClickRelatedActivity: Reads the clicked related activity’s id and navigates the browser to activity_detail.html?id={relatedActivityId}, which in turn triggers the activity-detail-loader to call getActivityDetail for the new id.

components.functionality: Shows transient success or error messages related to actions on the Activity Detail page, primarily for add-to-list operations (using the message returned by saveActivityToList) and data-load errors (for getActivityDetail and getActivityListsOverview).
event_handlers: onDismiss: Clears the currently displayed notification from view when the user closes it.

onRetryAction: When a notification corresponds to a recoverable error (e.g., list loading or activity loading), triggers the appropriate retry handler such as activity-detail-loader.onRetryFetch or add-to-activity-list-dialog.onOpen.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic for constructing the Planner navigation URL when the user chooses to add the current activity to the Planner. It ensures that the current Activity.id and any planner context (planId) present in the Activity Detail URL are passed through to planner.html as URL parameters.
data_binding: Optional planId from the current page URL
event_handlers: onClick: Reads Activity.id from the bound data and any planId query parameter from the current URL. Constructs a URL for planner.html that includes addActivityId={Activity.id} and, if available, planId={planId}. Navigates the browser to this URL so the Planner page can complete day and plan assignment.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, stacked structure: a full-width hero-style activity header at the very top (activity-main-info), immediately followed by a full-width action/navigation bar (activity-actions-bar). Below that, the main content is a single centered column containing, in order, a slim notification/status region (activity-status-messages when present), the two main editorial blocks for the activity content (activity-instructions-steps as the primary feature block and activity-materials-list as a supporting block), then a generous gap leading into a wide, editorial-feeling related content section (related-activities-list). The loader and dialog appear as overlays centered over this base stack.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, stacked structure: a full-width hero-style activity header at the very top (activity-main-info), immediately followed by a full-width action/navigation bar (activity-actions-bar). Below that, the main content is a single centered column containing, in order, a slim notification/status region (activity-status-messages when present), the two main editorial blocks for the activity content (activity-instructions-steps as the primary feature block and activity-materials-list as a supporting block), then a generous gap leading into a wide, editorial-feeling related content section (related-activities-list). The loader and dialog appear as overlays centered over this base stack.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero-style activity header occupies roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport height with a large activity image and title. The action bar beneath it takes about 8–10% of vertical space on first load and is visually compact but horizontally expansive. The main content column beneath (instructions, materials, related activities) sits within a constrained 1100–1200px width, centered, taking about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop with 15–20% gutters on each side. Vertical gaps between major blocks (hero to actions bar, actions bar to content, between instructions, materials, and related activities) use generous 24–40px spacing, aligned to the 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains low-density and editorial: few elements per fold, lots of white space, and clear separation between blocks. Each major component has comfortable internal padding (around 16–24px), and text is allowed to breathe, with short line lengths and ample margins. Only one major reading block (the instructions feature area) dominates the first content view below the hero and actions bar, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on single, strong focal content rather than many small cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is first pulled to the large hero image and activity title in the top center. From there it moves slightly down to any subtitle or meta details within the hero. Next it flows to the full-width actions bar below, scanning left-to-right across primary actions like saving, adding to planner, or opening the list dialog. As the user scrolls, attention moves into the main content column, where a small label-like header at the top of the instructions section mirrors the mockup’s subtle tagline line and leads into the large instructions block as the new focal anchor. Below that, the eye gently transitions to the more compact materials list and finally settles on the wide related-activities section. Notifications and status messages appear just beneath the actions bar, briefly intercepting attention without disrupting the vertical reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The Activity Detail page feels like a clean, modern editorial article for a family lifestyle magazine. A full-width hero band spans the top of the screen with a large, warm activity photo stretching edge-to-edge and a bold, centrally aligned activity title and subtitle layered over it within the central 1100–1200px content width. Directly beneath this hero sits a slim, white, full-width action bar that echoes a navigation strip, with key actions laid out horizontally and ample padding. Scrolling down, the layout narrows into a single centered column with generous left and right gutters, creating a calm reading lane. A small, understated label-like heading at the start of the instructions area leads into a large, full-bleed (within the column) instructions block that visually acts like the featured article card—broad, spacious, and highly readable. Below, the materials list appears as a secondary but clearly separated card, followed by a wide, low-density related-activities section that visually resembles a set of editorial cards or tiles. Throughout, vertical spacing between sections is generous, the chrome is minimal and flat, and there are no sidebars, giving the page a relaxed, family-friendly feel. Overlays like the loader and the add-to-activity-list dialog sit centered above this base layout, dimming the background while preserving the clean editorial character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the activity-detail-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily replaces interaction with the underlying page. It covers the entire browser window from edge to edge with a soft, semi-transparent white or very light overlay, preserving the warm editorial tone without feeling heavy. At the exact center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically—sits a simple, modern loading indicator (spinner or subtle animation) accompanied by a short loading label. The loader content is constrained to a relatively small central area, roughly 25–30% of the viewport width, leaving plenty of surrounding empty space to maintain the calm, minimal aesthetic. Once data is loaded, this overlay fades out completely, revealing the hero header at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-screen overlay positioned fixed to the viewport, centered content; blocks interaction with underlying components and scroll while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-main-info component occupies the hero region at the very top of the page. It spans the full width of the viewport from left edge to right edge, but its core content is aligned to a centered content container about 1100–1200px wide. A large activity image fills this hero area horizontally and takes up roughly 30–35% of the initial viewport height on desktop, scaling responsively. Over this image, in the center of the content container, the activity title is displayed in large, bold type with an editorial feel, centered or slightly above center vertically. A smaller subtitle or key meta information (such as age range or duration) appears just below the title with generous vertical spacing (16–24px) and softer weight. Internal padding around text respects the 16–24px scale from the image edges or any color overlay, ensuring the text does not touch the edges. The overall effect is a calm, magazine-style hero that immediately communicates what the activity is and sets the warm, family-oriented tone for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page content; scrolls away as the user moves down, without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the hero activity-main-info, the activity-actions-bar forms a full-width horizontal band that echoes the navigation bar from the mockup. It stretches from the left edge of the viewport to the right, with a flat white background and no visible shadows, and is visually separated from the hero by about 24px of vertical whitespace. Its core content is again constrained to the centered 1100–1200px column. Within this bar, key actions such as "Save to List," "Favorite," and the "Add to Planner" entry point are laid out in a left-to-right row, each with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and clear hit areas. On desktop, the actions may wrap into two lines if necessary, similar to the multi-row navigation in the mockup, but they retain ample spacing so the bar never feels crowded. The height of the bar on initial load occupies roughly 8–10% of the viewport height, corresponding to about 56–80px high. The overall treatment is light, with primary actions using a slightly stronger weight or accent color for emphasis while remaining visually quiet to preserve the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned directly under the hero; as the user scrolls, the bar becomes sticky, pinning to the top of the viewport so key actions remain accessible while the main content scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-status-messages component appears just below the activity-actions-bar within the same central content column. It sits inside the 1100–1200px centered width and does not span fully edge-to-edge, maintaining the editorial column structure. When present, a single slim message bar or card (for success, error, or info) is displayed, occupying nearly the full width of the content column but with comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (8–16px). It is visually separated from the actions bar above by around 16–24px of whitespace and from the main content below by another 16–24px, so it reads as its own distinct, unobtrusive strip. The styling is subtle—flat background with a gentle tint and clear text—ensuring it catches the eye right after the action bar without overwhelming the hero or the main content. Multiple messages, if needed, stack vertically with consistent spacing, but in general the layout favors a single, concise message at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the vertical flow beneath the (optionally sticky) actions bar; scrolls with the main content and does not remain fixed once it leaves the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-instructions-steps component forms the primary editorial content block in the main column, analogous to the feature article card in the mockup. It appears immediately below any status messages, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. The component is centered within the 1100–1200px main content width and stretches nearly full width within that column, with generous internal padding (24–32px) on all sides. At the top-left of this block, a small, subtle label (such as "How to do this activity" or "Instructions") is rendered in a lighter weight or smaller uppercase style, mimicking the "2026 Family Treasure" line from the mockup; it aligns flush left with the main text below and sits about 8–12px above the first step heading or body text. Below this label, the steps are presented in a vertically ordered list with ample spacing (16–24px) between steps, ensuring readability and a low-density feel. On larger screens, the instructions block visually occupies most of the initial main content viewport beneath the actions bar, acting as the new focal anchor as the user scrolls. The background remains flat (white or a very soft tint) with no visible shadows to align with the minimal chrome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the central column, growing vertically with content; scrolls naturally without sticky behavior and anchors the main reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activity-materials-list component is placed directly after the instructions block in the vertical stack, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px to keep the sections distinct but visually related. It shares the same centered 1100–1200px width as the instructions, and its card-like body also stretches nearly full width inside that column with internal padding around 24px. The materials list is slightly more compact than the instructions, occupying less vertical height on initial view, which reinforces its role as supporting information. A clear, left-aligned heading (for example, "Materials" or "What you’ll need") sits at the top with 8–16px of spacing above the list items. The individual materials entries are spaced with 8–16px of vertical padding between rows, maintaining a light, easy-to-scan list. This block visually feels like a secondary editorial card stacked beneath the main article, consistent with the calm, editorial blog layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block, full width within the central column; scrolls normally with no fixed behavior and adapts its height to the number of materials.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-activities-list appears as the final major section in the main content column, following the materials list. It is separated from the materials by a larger vertical margin—around 40–64px—to mark a clear transition from the current activity details to exploratory browsing. The section still honors the 1100–1200px centered width but may visually feel wider because multiple related activity cards or rows are shown. At the top left of this section, a heading like "Related activities" is set in a slightly larger, bold type, echoing an editorial section title. Below, related items are arranged in a low-density grid or single-column list, depending on screen size, with generous horizontal and vertical spacing between cards (16–24px). Each card maintains the minimal, flat look with ample white space and small imagery. The overall section height can extend beyond the initial viewport, encouraging scrolling, but at any given fold the content still feels airy and not crowded. This section acts as a calm, magazine-style "more to explore" area at the bottom of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and scrollable as part of the main content; cards within may wrap responsively but the section itself remains within the central column and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When invoked, the add-to-activity-list-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay above the entire page, similar in spirit to a magazine-style inset panel. A semi-transparent backdrop covers the full viewport, subtly dimming the hero, action bar, and content beneath while preserving their visibility. The dialog itself is a clean, rectangular card positioned at the center of the viewport (both horizontally and vertically), with a width around 40–50% of the viewport on desktop, but never exceeding the main 1100–1200px content width. It uses generous internal padding (24–32px) and aligns its form fields in a single column with clear spacing (16–24px) between elements, maintaining the overall low-density look. The title of the dialog is left-aligned at the top, with the form inputs and any list selection controls stacked beneath. Action buttons (e.g., "Save" and "Cancel") are aligned along the bottom, typically right-aligned, with comfortable spacing. The dialog’s edges are crisp and flat, with no heavy shadows—if any elevation is used, it is minimal and very soft, to remain consistent with the minimal chrome aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Modal overlay fixed to the viewport, centered on both axes; blocks interaction with all underlying components until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-planner-link lives inside the activity-actions-bar as one of the key, horizontally arranged controls. It typically appears on the right half of the actions bar (for example, aligned to the right or near other planning-related controls), balancing the more generic actions (like saving or favoriting) on the left. The link is styled as a prominent text-based control with a subtle icon, maintaining the flat, minimal look—no heavy buttons, but slightly stronger weight or accent color so that during a left-to-right scan of the bar, the eye pauses on it. It shares the same vertical height and padding as other actions, with 16–24px of horizontal padding around its clickable area. On narrower screens, if the actions wrap into two lines, the add-to-planner-link remains clearly visible and maintains at least 8–12px of vertical spacing from adjacent controls to avoid crowding. This placement ensures that as users scan the bar after absorbing the hero, they naturally discover the planner option as part of the main action set.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the horizontally flowing actions bar; moves with the bar when it becomes sticky so it stays visible at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls through the activity details.

name: Search & Articles
title: Articles Search
description: Search and browse articles with keyword, category, and other filters, then open individual articles for detailed reading.

core_features: Run keyword searches across all articles using the searchArticles interface.
Filter results by category, level, age group, publish date range, session length range, and minimum comment count.
Sort results by relevance or other supported sort options via the sortBy parameter.
Paginate through results using the page parameter and pageSize passed to searchArticles.
Display article result cards showing title, summary, category, publish date, age group, session length, comment count, and other available metadata from the Article model.
Navigate to article_detail.html with the selected article id when a result card or title is clicked.
user_workflows: From any page, the user types a keyword into the global header search, submits, and arrives on search.html with the query parameter set; the page reads URL parameters, calls searchArticles, shows matching article cards, and allows navigation to article_detail.html.

The user lands on search.html from the header navigation without a query; the page shows an empty or helper state until the user enters a keyword or applies filters, then searchArticles is called and results appear.

The user refines results by selecting one or more filters (category, level, ageGroup, date range, session length range, minCommentCount); each change updates the URL parameters, resets page to 1, calls searchArticles with the new parameters, and refreshes the list.
The user changes the sort option (e.g., relevance, newest); the sortBy URL parameter is updated, page is reset to 1, searchArticles is called, and results are re-rendered in the new order.

The user moves between pages of results using pagination controls; clicking a page number or next/previous updates the page URL parameter, calls searchArticles with the same filters and new page value, and shows the corresponding set of articles.

The user clicks an article title or card in the results list; the browser navigates to article_detail.html with the article id in the URL parameters, enabling reading, bookmarking, and commenting on that specific article.
interactions: Typing in the search field and submitting the search updates the query parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers searchArticles.
Clicking a filter control (category, level, ageGroup, date pickers, numeric ranges) updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchArticles call.
Clicking a 'clear filter' or 'reset filters' control removes specific parameters or all filter-related parameters from the URL and re-calls searchArticles.
Changing the sort dropdown updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchArticles.
Using pagination controls updates the page URL parameter and triggers searchArticles with the current query and filters.
Clicking an article result card or title navigates to article_detail.html?id={article.id} using the Article id from the current search results.
When the browser’s back/forward buttons are used, the controller component detects URL parameter changes and re-runs searchArticles to keep the results in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: On page load, the controller reads URL parameters (query, category, level, ageGroup, minPublishDate, maxPublishDate, minSessionLengthMinutes, maxSessionLengthMinutes, minCommentCount, sortBy, page). These values are passed directly into searchArticles with an additional pageSize value from a configurable source. If a parameter is missing, it is passed as undefined or a configuration-derived default, allowing the backend to apply its own defaults (for example, default sort or page number). After searchArticles resolves, the returned articles array and totalCount are stored in page state and passed down to the results list, summary, and pagination components. When the user interacts with search inputs, filters, sorting, or pagination, the relevant URL parameters are updated (either via full navigation or history API), and page is set to 1 for any change that alters the result set except pure pagination. Each URL change triggers the controller to re-read parameters and call searchArticles again so that the UI state (visible filters, search term, sort option, current page, and results list) always matches the current URL. Navigation to article_detail.html is performed by constructing a URL with id set to the Article.id from the selected result card.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes the page, reads URL parameters, calls the searchArticles interface, manages loading/error states, and provides the resulting Article data and totalCount to child components.
event_handlers: // Called on initial page load; reads URL params and calls searchArticles.
// Called when the browser URL query string changes (including back/forward); re-reads parameters and re-calls searchArticles.
// Invoked by child components (search form, filters, sort, pagination) when search-related parameters change; updates URL accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides a search input and submit button for entering or editing the keyword query that drives article search. It reflects the current query from URL state and triggers new searches when submitted.
event_handlers: // Updates internal form state and optionally performs debounced suggestions (if implemented outside this spec) without calling searchArticles directly.
// Prevents default form submission, updates the query parameter in the URL (and removes page to reset it to the default), then notifies the controller to re-run searchArticles.
// Clears the search input, removes the query parameter from the URL, resets page to default, and triggers a new search via the controller.

components.functionality: Displays filter controls for category, level, ageGroup, publish date range, session length range, and minimum comment count. It reads current filter values from the URL-backed state and allows users to adjust them to refine results.
event_handlers: // Updates the category URL parameter based on the selected category, removes page, and notifies the controller to call searchArticles.
// Updates the level URL parameter and re-triggers search via the controller.
// Updates the ageGroup URL parameter and re-triggers search via the controller.
// Updates minPublishDate and maxPublishDate URL parameters (using ISO-formatted values) and re-triggers search.
// Updates minSessionLengthMinutes and/or maxSessionLengthMinutes URL parameters and re-triggers search.
// Updates the minCommentCount URL parameter and re-triggers search.

// Removes all filter-related URL parameters (category, level, ageGroup, minPublishDate, maxPublishDate, minSessionLengthMinutes, maxSessionLengthMinutes, minCommentCount), resets page, and notifies the controller to run a search with only query and sortBy if present.

components.functionality: Shows a summary of currently applied filters (for example, selected category or date range) as removable chips or labels. It provides quick actions to remove individual filters or clear all filters.
event_handlers: // When a single filter chip is cleared, removes the corresponding URL parameter (e.g., category or minPublishDate/maxPublishDate) and triggers a search via the controller.
// Removes all filter-related URL parameters while preserving query and sortBy, resets page, and triggers search via the controller.

components.functionality: Provides a dropdown or buttons to choose the sort order for search results (e.g., relevance, newest, most_commented). It reflects the current sortBy value from URL state.
event_handlers: // Updates the sortBy URL parameter to the newly selected sort option, removes page, and notifies the controller to re-run searchArticles with the new sort order.

components.functionality: Displays summary information about the current search, such as the number of results returned from searchArticles, the current query, and the active page. It reacts dynamically to updates from the controller.

Renders the list of article result cards based on the articles array returned from searchArticles. Each card exposes the Article metadata needed for scanning (title, summary, category, publishDate, ageGroup, sessionLengthMinutes, commentCount, tags) and is clickable to open the article detail page.
event_handlers: // Receives the selected Article.id and navigates the browser to article_detail.html?id={Article.id} using standard URL navigation.
// Called by the controller whenever new results arrive from searchArticles, updating the rendered list.

components.functionality: Represents a single article within the results list. It displays the article’s key fields from the Article model and emits events when the user interacts (clicking the card or specific links).
event_handlers: // Emits the Article.id to the parent article-results-list, which triggers navigation to article_detail.html via onArticleClick.
// Supports keyboard activation (e.g., Enter/Space) for accessibility, triggering the same behavior as onClick.

components.functionality: Shows pagination UI based on totalCount from searchArticles and the current page and pageSize values. Allows navigation to specific pages and next/previous pages.
event_handlers: // When a user selects a different page, updates the page URL parameter while keeping query, filters, and sortBy unchanged, then notifies the controller to re-run searchArticles.
// Optional: if pageSize is exposed to the user, updates a pageSize parameter source consistent with searchArticles usage and resets page to its default, then triggers a new search.
