components.functionality: Listens to loading and error flags from the controller (which wraps calls to searchArticles) and conditionally displays loading indicators or error messages instead of or alongside the results list.
event_handlers: // Triggered when the controller starts or finishes a call to searchArticles; toggles the loading state display.
// Triggered when the controller encounters an error from searchArticles; updates URL-independent state to show an error message and may offer a retry option.
// Calls back into the controller to re-run searchArticles with the current URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: All search and article components sit within the main centered content column that appears below the global hero and navigation from the mockup. Inside this column, content follows a simple, linear top-to-bottom flow: search control at the top, then filters (summary + sidebar trigger), then sorting + results summary, followed by the vertical list of article cards, with pagination at the bottom. The filter-sidebar is treated as a contextual, slide-out or expandable panel attached to this main column (not a persistent left rail) so the page preserves the mockup’s single-column editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All search and article components sit within the main centered content column that appears below the global hero and navigation from the mockup. Inside this column, content follows a simple, linear top-to-bottom flow: search control at the top, then filters (summary + sidebar trigger), then sorting + results summary, followed by the vertical list of article cards, with pagination at the bottom. The filter-sidebar is treated as a contextual, slide-out or expandable panel attached to this main column (not a persistent left rail) so the page preserves the mockup’s single-column editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the main content column for this page occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 15–20% whitespace gutters on either side. Vertically, the search-and-filters block (search form + active filters + optional filter panel + sort/results summary row) takes up about the upper 25–30% of the available content area below the global nav, leaving the remaining 70–75% to the article list and pagination. Major sections (search block, filter block, list block, pagination) are separated with 24–40px vertical spacing, aligned to the 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout remains low-density and editorial: one main column of information, generous white space around and between components, and no crowded multi-column grids. Each article card is given enough vertical padding and separation to feel like an individual editorial feature. Controls like filters and sort appear visually lightweight (chips, concise text labels) with sufficient padding (16–24px) so the interface feels calm and breathable, matching the lifestyle-magazine tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the centered search bar near the top of the content column, then moves slightly downward to the active-filters bar where applied filters are clearly summarized. From there, a subtle left-right scan occurs across a row that pairs sort control on the right with results summary on the left. Next, the eye moves down into the vertical stack of article cards, each with strong imagery and headings acting as focal anchors, ending at the pagination controls. The filter-sidebar, when opened, slides in from the left edge of the content column or expands beneath the filters bar, catching attention without disrupting the primary top-to-bottom reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the large photographic hero and the full-width navigation bar from the main site frame, a single, centered content column emerges, occupying about two-thirds of the screen and framed by generous white gutters. At the top of this column sits a spacious search area with a wide, centered search field that echoes the editorial headline scale: clean, bold label, and comfortable padding. Just beneath, a slim active-filters bar stretches across the column, left-aligned text and filter chips arranged in an airy row. A compact horizontal strip follows, pairing the results summary on the left with a minimal sort control aligned to the right, creating a subtle balancing bar. Below this, the page opens into a vertical cascade of article cards, each spanning the full width of the content column, with large imagery and clear headlines, separated by consistent white space. At the bottom, a centered pagination row anchors the end of the results, with controls spaced generously so they feel tappable and relaxed. When users open the advanced filters, a light, panel-like filter-sidebar appears attached to the left side of this column (or expands just under the filters bar), still contained within the main content width, preserving the mockup’s single-column, magazine-like flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-controller is an invisible structural wrapper that aligns all other search-related components inside the main centered content column. It begins roughly 40–56px below the global navigation bar, matching the generous section spacing seen between hero, nav, and content in the mockup. Horizontally, it spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered on the page with wide left and right gutters. Vertically, it organizes its child components in a strict top-to-bottom stack: search-query-form at the top, followed by active-filters-bar, then the row containing results-summary and sort-control, then the article-results-list with article-result-card items, and finally the pagination-controls at the bottom. Loading-and-error-state content is also housed within this wrapper, replacing or overlaying the list area when active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard block-level container within the overall page flow, controlling maximum width and vertical spacing for all enclosed components but not itself visible; it scrolls with the page and aligns to the center grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-query-form is placed at the very top of the search-results-controller, functioning as the visual entry point into the Search & Articles page. It is centered horizontally within the content column, with the main search input spanning roughly 70–80% of that column’s width (about 50–55% of the full viewport on desktop). The form sits on a white background with at least 24–32px of clear space above and below it, echoing the breathing room between major content blocks in the mockup. The text field has generous internal padding (around 16px) and a modest height, visually reminiscent of a clean editorial search bar rather than a compact utility control. Any accompanying label or helper text sits directly above the input, left-aligned but still centered within the overall column, with a subtle hierarchy that positions the search field as the dominant element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the main content column; on narrower screens it stretches closer to 90–100% of the column width while maintaining centered alignment and consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar behaves more like a contextual, panel-style filter area than a permanent side column, in order to respect the mockup’s single-column editorial layout. By default, it is collapsed or hidden, with its presence indicated by a prominent 'Filters' button or chip within the active-filters-bar. When opened on desktop, the panel appears attached to the left edge of the main content column, overlaying the article-results-list area but still confined within the column’s width (not extending into the outer page gutters). It occupies roughly 35–40% of the content column’s width, leaving the remaining 60–65% dimmed or pushed slightly to the right, with a soft but flat separation (e.g., a contrasting background color rather than heavy shadows). Vertically, it extends from just below the active-filters-bar down towards the mid-screen, with internal padding of around 24px and 24–32px spacing between filter groups, creating a calm, form-like panel. On mobile and small tablets, this same panel appears as a full-width slide-up or slide-over drawer from the bottom or side, covering the article-results-list area while maintaining generous internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden by default and revealed as an overlay or slide-out attached to the main content column. It scrolls independently if its content exceeds the viewport height and can be dismissed to return focus to the article list; it never appears as a fixed, persistent left sidebar occupying its own page column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-bar sits immediately below the search-query-form, acting as a slim but important horizontal band that summarizes the current state of filtering. It spans the full width of the content column and is left-aligned within that column, with 16–24px of vertical padding and 16–24px of top margin from the search form. Applied filters appear as small, pill-shaped chips or tags aligned from left to right, wrapping onto a second line if needed, reflecting the mockup’s pattern of horizontally flowing labels (as seen in its navigation section). The text label (e.g., 'Active filters') is subtle and placed at the far left, with an optional 'Clear all' link or a 'Filters' button aligned toward the right end of the bar. The bar maintains a light, minimal appearance—white background or a very soft tint—with enough spacing above and below to feel distinct yet unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the normal flow, always visible when filters are active; when there are no filters, it can collapse to a minimal single-line row or hide entirely, closing the gap so that the results-summary row shifts immediately beneath the search-query-form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control appears in the first row beneath the active-filters-bar, horizontally paired with the results-summary. This row stretches across the full width of the content column, with the sort-control visually anchored to the right edge while the results-summary anchors to the left. The sort-control itself is compact—a text label like 'Sort by:' followed by a dropdown or segmented control—occupying roughly 20–25% of the column width. It is vertically centered within a row that has about 16–24px of top and bottom padding, mirroring the comfortable spacing of the nav items in the mockup. The overall effect is a light, unobtrusive control that provides structure along the top of the results without dominating the view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal flow, right-aligned within its row. On narrow screens, it stacks beneath the results-summary, left-aligned with a small (8–16px) vertical gap, preserving a top-to-bottom scanning pattern when horizontal space is limited.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary sits on the left side of the same horizontal row as the sort-control, directly beneath the active-filters-bar. It is left-aligned with the content column’s text baseline, taking up about 50–60% of the row’s width. The summary (e.g., '24 articles matching “sleep routines”') appears in a single line of body text, styled clearly but not as a headline, supporting the editorial tone without competing with article titles. There is about 16–24px of vertical padding above and below this row, and a 16–24px gap between this row and the first article card below, giving the impression of a small, calm status line separating controls from content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally above the article-results-list. On small screens, when the row stacks, it appears above the sort-control and remains left-aligned, ensuring it is read first before the user chooses a sort option.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-results-list occupies the largest portion of the page below the search and filter controls, forming a vertical stack of article-result-card components. It begins approximately 24–32px below the row containing the results-summary and sort-control. The list spans the full width of the content column, with each card separated by a consistent vertical gap of 24–32px, echoing the spacing between major content blocks in the mockup. There is no multi-column tiling; instead, the list maintains a single-column, editorial feel where each article card reads like its own feature. The overall list area extends down the page, taking up the majority of the scrollable vertical space before pagination appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level container that scrolls with the page. When the loading-and-error-state is active, this region is temporarily replaced or visually overlaid by loading or error messaging, while the surrounding search and filter controls remain visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-result-card is a full-width card within the content column, visually inspired by the mockup’s large '100 BEST...' editorial feature but scaled for a list context. The card typically includes a rectangular image at the top spanning the full card width, followed by the article category label, headline, short description, and meta information (e.g., reading time). The card’s background is white (or a very light tone), with internal padding of about 24px on all sides and a vertical spacing system that ensures 8–16px between textual elements. The image height is roughly 35–45% of the viewport height on larger screens for visually rich articles, but can be shorter for smaller items; the text area below should feel open rather than densely packed. Cards sit against the page background with no heavy shadows—only subtle borders or color shifts—to stay aligned with the flat, minimal chrome of the mockup. Overall, each card feels like a mini editorial layout, allowing one article to be scanned comfortably at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully responsive within the main content column; the card width adjusts with the column, while internal layout adapts (e.g., stacking meta info below the description on smaller screens) but remains vertically oriented. No sticky or floating behavior; each card scrolls naturally with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the search-results-controller, directly beneath the last article-result-card, with a generous top margin of about 32–40px to clearly separate navigation from content. They are horizontally centered within the content column, aligning with the overall editorial symmetry. The controls themselves—page numbers, next/previous arrows, or 'Load more' button—are spaced with at least 8–16px between individual elements, maintaining touch-friendly sizing and the relaxed feel of the mockup’s navigation items. The overall pagination row has about 16–24px of vertical padding above and below, forming a clean visual endpoint for the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the normal flow at the bottom of the page segment. On desktop, numbers are displayed in a single line; on smaller screens, the design may simplify (e.g., previous/next only or a large 'Load more' button) while remaining centered. It scrolls with the page and becomes visible as users reach the end of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-state occupies the same vertical region as the article-results-list but does so temporarily during data fetching or when an error occurs. When loading, a centered spinner or skeleton list appears within the content column, roughly mid-way down the viewport, with ample white space around it so it feels light and unobtrusive. When an error occurs (e.g., 'No results found' or network error), a message card appears centered within the column, approximately the width of a typical article-result-card but slightly shorter, with 24px padding and clear, friendly text. This message card sits about 32–40px below the results-summary row, preserving the familiar vertical rhythm. Any retry button or alternative actions are stacked beneath the message text in a simple vertical arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or overlays the article-results-list area while keeping the search-query-form, active-filters-bar, and sort/results controls visible above. It scrolls with the page and is fully contained within the main content column; once results are successfully loaded, it yields the space back to the article-results-list.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a full wellbeing or parenting article, with options to bookmark it into reading lists and read or add comments.

core_features: Load and display full article details (title, author, publish date, category, optional level and age group, tags, image, and content) using getArticleDetail based on the article ID in the URL.
Display a live comment count for the article using getArticleComments, keeping this count consistent with the value shown in search results.
Show existing approved comments for the article and keep them updated after a new comment is added, using getArticleComments.
Provide a bookmark control that lets the user save the article to an existing or new reading list, using getReadingListsOverview and saveArticleToReadingList.
Provide a comment form where a user can enter their name and comment text and submit it to the article using addCommentToArticle.
Allow navigation back to the article list (e.g., search results or homepage) and forward to a specific reading list after bookmarking, using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Open and read an article:
1. User arrives on article_detail.html with a required id URL parameter (article_detail.html?id={articleId}).
2. The page controller reads the id parameter and calls getArticleDetail(articleId).
3. In parallel, the page controller calls getArticleComments(articleId) to get comments and the current commentCount.
4. The article header and content components render the article data when getArticleDetail returns.
5. The comments section renders the comment count and list once getArticleComments returns.

Bookmark an article into a reading list:
1. While reading an article, the user clicks the bookmark control (e.g., a button labeled 'Add to Reading List').
2. If reading lists have not yet been loaded, the page controller calls getReadingListsOverview().
3. A selection UI is shown (e.g., a dropdown or modal) listing available reading lists from getReadingListsOverview.
4. The user either selects an existing list or opts to create/use a default list. The bookmark control then calls saveArticleToReadingList with articleId and either a chosen readingListId or a readingListName (when creating or using a default list).
5. When saveArticleToReadingList returns success, the bookmark UI updates to reflect that the article is saved (e.g., toggled state or a confirmation message) and shows an action to 'View Reading List'.
6. If the user clicks 'View Reading List', the page navigates to saved_lists.html with URL parameters listType=reading_list and listId set to the returned readingList.id from saveArticleToReadingList.

Read comments and jump to the comments section:
1. The article header shows a comment count badge using the commentCount from getArticleComments.
2. The user clicks the comment count badge or a 'View comments' link.
3. The page scrolls/focuses to the comments section component.
4. The comments list component displays all comments returned by getArticleComments, ordered according to the API response.

Add a new comment to an article:
1. In the comments section, the user fills in the comment form: a required name field and a required comment text field.
2. The user submits the form, triggering a call to addCommentToArticle with articleId (from URL), authorName (from the form), and text (from the form).
3. While waiting for the response, the form shows a submitting state to prevent duplicate submissions.
4. On success, the page controller can either: (a) immediately append the returned comment object to the comments list if the API indicates it is approved, or (b) show a message that the comment was received and may appear after moderation if isApproved is false or not present.
5. The local comment count displayed on the page is incremented to stay consistent with search results, or the page controller can re-call getArticleComments(articleId) to resync comments and commentCount.
6. On error, an error message is displayed and the comment is not added to the list.

Navigate back to articles and between related articles:
1. The page may receive an optional from URL parameter indicating the source page (e.g., from=search or from=index).
2. The article header shows a 'Back to Articles' link. When clicked, it uses the from parameter (if present) to decide the destination (e.g., search.html or index.html); if no from parameter exists, it can fall back to a generic articles list page or rely on the browser back button.
3. The related articles list (from getArticleDetail.relatedArticles) shows links to other article_detail.html pages, each with its own id parameter.
4. When the user clicks a related article, the URL updates with the new id, triggering the page controller to re-run getArticleDetail and getArticleComments for the new article.
interactions:
Page load / URL change:
- Trigger: The page loads or the id URL parameter changes (e.g., via navigation to a related article).
- Action: The page controller calls getArticleDetail(articleId) and getArticleComments(articleId). When data is received, it updates bound components (header, content, comments, related articles).

Click 'Back to Articles' link:
- Trigger: User clicks a back navigation link in the header.
- Action: Navigate to search.html, index.html, or another configured list page based on an optional from URL parameter or default behavior.

Click bookmark / 'Add to Reading List' control:
- Trigger: User clicks the bookmark control in the article header or near the article content.
- Action: If reading lists are not yet loaded, call getReadingListsOverview(). Then open the reading list selection UI. When the user confirms a list, call saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId and the selected readingListId or a readingListName. Show success or error status based on the API response.

Click 'View Reading List' after bookmarking:
- Trigger: After a successful saveArticleToReadingList call, the user clicks 'View Reading List'.
- Action: Navigate to saved_lists.html with listType=reading_list and listId set to the readingList.id returned by saveArticleToReadingList.

Click comment count badge / 'View comments':
- Trigger: User clicks the comment count badge in the article header.
- Action: Scroll to the comments section and focus on it. Optionally update the URL with a section=comments parameter so reloading the page returns directly to comments.

Submit comment form:
- Trigger: User submits the comment form with name and text filled.
- Action: Front-end validation ensures both fields are non-empty. If valid, call addCommentToArticle(articleId, authorName, text). On success, update the comments list and comment count (by either appending the new comment or re-calling getArticleComments). Show success or error feedback. Clear the form if appropriate.

Click on a related article:
- Trigger: User clicks a related article link in the related articles component.
- Action: Navigate to article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId} (and optionally preserve or set a from parameter). The page controller then reloads article and comments for the new id.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is driven by URL parameters, without using localStorage. The required id query parameter (article_detail.html?id={articleId}) defines which Article is displayed. On initial load and whenever the id parameter changes, the page controller calls getArticleDetail(articleId) and getArticleComments(articleId) to refresh all bound components. If id is missing or invalid, the page should show an appropriate error state and avoid calling the interfaces.

Optional URL parameters can further refine behavior:
- from: Indicates the source context (e.g., search, index, saved_lists). The 'Back to Articles' link uses this value to decide where to navigate. Changing from affects only navigation targets, not data fetching.
- section: When section=comments is present, after loading article and comments the page automatically scrolls to the comments section so the user can immediately read or add comments.

The comment count displayed on this page is kept in sync with the value used in search results by always taking the authoritative commentCount from getArticleComments. After adding a comment via addCommentToArticle, the page can either optimistically increment the commentCount in memory or re-fetch via getArticleComments(articleId) to ensure full consistency. Bookmarking state is not persisted via URL; instead, when saveArticleToReadingList succeeds, any visual indication that the article is saved is handled in in-memory component state, while navigation to saved_lists.html uses the readingList.id returned from the API as a URL parameter (listId).

components.functionality: Central orchestrator that reads URL parameters, loads article and comments, coordinates bookmark and comment actions, and passes data and handlers to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read id and optional parameters (from, section) from the URL, call getArticleDetail(articleId) and getArticleComments(articleId). When both responses arrive, update child components with article, relatedArticles, comments, and commentCount.
onUrlChange: When the id parameter changes (e.g., navigation to a related article), repeat the data loading process and reset component states dependent on the current article.

onBookmarkRequested: Triggered by the bookmark control when the user wants to save the article. Ensures reading lists are loaded (via getReadingListsOverview if needed), then passes lists to the bookmark control for selection.

onBookmarkConfirm: Receives the selected readingListId or readingListName from the bookmark control and calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId, readingListName). On success, updates bookmark-related UI state and exposes the returned readingList.id and listItemCount to relevant components.

onCommentSubmit: Receives authorName and text from the comment form and calls addCommentToArticle(articleId, authorName, text). On success, either appends the returned Comment to the local comments collection (if appropriate) and increments commentCount, or triggers a refresh via getArticleComments(articleId). On error, passes error information back to the comment form.
onCommentsRefreshRequest: Optionally re-calls getArticleComments(articleId) (e.g., after posting a comment or after a manual refresh action in the comments section).
onNavigateToReadingList: Uses the ReadingList returned by saveArticleToReadingList to construct a URL to saved_lists.html?listType=reading_list&listId={readingList.id} and triggers navigation.
onBackNavigate: Uses an optional from parameter to decide whether to navigate to search.html, index.html, or another articles list page, or falls back to browser history.

components.functionality: Displays article title, author, publish date, category, optional level and age group, tags, a comment count badge, a back navigation link, and the primary bookmark control entry point.
event_handlers: onClickBackLink: Calls the page controller's onBackNavigate to return to the previous article listing context.
onClickCommentCount: Requests the page controller to focus or scroll to the comments section and optionally updates the URL to section=comments.
onClickTag: Navigates to a search or filter page with the clicked tag in the URL (e.g., search.html?tag={tag}), without additional API calls from this page.
onClickBookmark: Triggers onBookmarkRequested in the page controller to start the bookmark flow (e.g., open the bookmark control / modal).

components.functionality: Renders the main body of the article, including featured image (if available), summary (if available), and full content.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Optional handler to notify the page controller that content has rendered, which can be used to accurately scroll to sections (e.g., comments) if section=comments is set in the URL.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related articles returned by getArticleDetail and allows the user to navigate to another article detail page.
event_handlers: onClickRelatedArticle: Navigates to article_detail.html?id={relatedArticle.id}&from={currentFrom}, which triggers the page controller's onUrlChange and reloads article and comment data.

components.functionality: Manages the flow to add the current article to a reading list, including loading available reading lists, letting the user choose or name a list, calling the save interface, and offering navigation to the reading list.
event_handlers: onOpen: Invoked when the bookmark button in the header or content is clicked. Requests the page controller to ensure reading lists are loaded via getReadingListsOverview, then displays the selection UI.
onSelectReadingList: Captures the user's chosen readingListId from the selection UI and passes it to the page controller's onBookmarkConfirm.

onNewReadingListNameChange: Captures user input for a new reading list name when the user opts to create or use a named list, and passes the value to onBookmarkConfirm as readingListName when the user confirms.

onConfirmSave: Calls the page controller's onBookmarkConfirm with articleId and either readingListId or readingListName, which triggers saveArticleToReadingList. Shows loading and then success or error state based on the response.
onClickViewReadingList: After a successful save, triggers onNavigateToReadingList in the page controller to navigate to saved_lists.html with the appropriate listType and listId.

components.functionality: Wrapper component that groups the comments header (with count), the comments list, and the comment form. Manages focusing, scrolling, and refresh requests.
event_handlers: onFocusRequest: Called by the page controller when section=comments is present or when the comment count badge is clicked; scrolls and focuses the comments section.

onRefreshRequested: Optional handler (e.g., pull-to-refresh or button) that requests the page controller to call onCommentsRefreshRequest, which re-invokes getArticleComments(articleId) and updates the section.

components.functionality: Displays the list of approved comments for the article, including authorName, text, and createdAt values, in the order provided by getArticleComments.
event_handlers: onCommentsUpdated: Called by the page controller when a new set of comments is loaded (initial load or refresh) so the list can re-render.

components.functionality: Captures a user's name and comment text for the current article and submits them to the backend as a new comment.
data_binding: Comment (single, from addCommentToArticle.comment on success, if used for optimistic update)
event_handlers: onChangeName: Updates the local form state with the user's typed name.
onChangeText: Updates the local form state with the user's typed comment text.

onSubmit: Validates required fields (authorName and text). If valid, calls the page controller's onCommentSubmit with articleId, authorName, and text, which in turn invokes addCommentToArticle. Disables the form during submission and shows success or error feedback depending on the API response.

onSubmitSuccess: Invoked by the page controller when addCommentToArticle returns success. Clears the form fields, and either triggers an optimistic update (append the returned Comment to the list) or requests a comments refresh.
onSubmitError: Invoked by the page controller when addCommentToArticle returns an error, displaying an error message to the user.

components.functionality: Displays transient success or error messages for bookmark and comment actions (e.g., comment submission success or bookmark failure).
event_handlers: onShowNotification: Triggered by the page controller when an action (bookmark or comment submission) completes with a result that includes a message or error.
onDismissNotification: Allows the user to manually dismiss a visible notification, clearing the current status from view while retaining the underlying data state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s stacked, editorial structure: a full-width article hero at the top (article-header), followed immediately by a flat, full-width utility bar (bookmark-control) that visually echoes the navigation bar in the mockup. Below this, the main article body (article-content) sits as a centered text column. After the body, related-articles-list appears as an editorial section, followed by a clearly separated comments-section containing the comment-list and comment-form. The notification-banner, when present, sits above the article hero as a slim status band. All components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom flow with no sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s stacked, editorial structure: a full-width article hero at the top (article-header), followed immediately by a flat, full-width utility bar (bookmark-control) that visually echoes the navigation bar in the mockup. Below this, the main article body (article-content) sits as a centered text column. After the body, related-articles-list appears as an editorial section, followed by a clearly separated comments-section containing the comment-list and comment-form. The notification-banner, when present, sits above the article hero as a slim status band. All components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom flow with no sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The article hero at the top occupies roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport height, mirroring the mockup’s hero dominance. The bookmark-control bar beneath it takes about 8–10% of the viewport height (around 64–72px). The remaining vertical space is given to the article-content, with related-articles-list and comments-section extending below the fold. Horizontally, all primary content sits within a centered column constrained to around 1100–1200px max width, leaving 15–20% of the viewport on each side as whitespace gutters on large screens. Vertical gaps between major sections follow the 8px base grid (typically 32–48px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, calm editorial feel: generous margins and line spacing, one major focal element per section, and ample whitespace around each block. The hero shows just the article’s main image and key text; the utility bar is clean with a small set of actions; the article body is a comfortable reading column with high line-height and clear paragraph spacing. Related articles are presented in a small number of large, card-like items rather than many small tiles. Comments are stacked cleanly with clear separation, avoiding visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the article hero headline and image, then moves slightly down to any subtitle/metadata. From there, it naturally flows into the bookmark-control bar where key actions live, scanning left-to-right. Next, attention drops to the small introductory label at the top of the article-content (echoing the ‘2026 Family Treasure’ line), then continues down through the main text. As the article ends, the ‘Related Articles’ heading and cards become the new focal anchor, guiding the user onward. Finally, the eye moves into the comments heading, then down through the comment-list and ends at the comment-form, inviting interaction. The sticky behavior of the bookmark-control keeps a subtle horizontal anchor at the top as users scroll the text.

layout.overall_description: The page reads like a modern, family-lifestyle magazine article laid out on the web. Below the global site navigation, an optional, slim notification band stretches across the width of the content area, followed by a large, photographic article hero that fills roughly the top third of the screen. The headline and supporting text sit on top of this image in a clear, editorial composition. Directly beneath the hero, a flat, white utility bar holds the bookmark control, spanning the full width of the central column and later becoming sticky as you scroll. Below this bar, the content tightens into a comfortable reading column centered on the page, with a small, muted label at the top and flowing paragraphs underneath. After the article text, a ‘Related Articles’ section presents a few large, image-forward cards aligned to the same central column. Finally, the comments section, also aligned to this column, appears with a clear heading, a vertical stack of well-spaced comments, and a simple, inviting comment form at the bottom. Throughout, wide horizontal gutters and generous vertical spacing keep the layout airy, warm, and easy to read, echoing the original mockup’s editorial, family-focused tone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-page-controller acts as the structural frame for the entire view. It defines a full-viewport-height canvas with a centered content column constrained to approximately 1100–1200px max width on desktop. On large screens, this column sits in the middle of the viewport with roughly 15–20% horizontal whitespace gutters on each side; on smaller screens, it expands to nearly full width with 16–20px side padding. All page components—notification-banner (when present), article-header, bookmark-control, article-content, related-articles-list, and comments-section—are stacked vertically within this column, each separated by 32–48px of vertical space. The controller follows an implicit 12-column grid within the max-width area, but exposes only large, flat sections rather than visible grid lines or card chrome.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal full-page scroll container, anchoring the centered grid and vertical stacking order for all child components; it does not scroll independently of the viewport and has no sticky behavior itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner appears only when needed as a slim, full-width band directly below the global site navigation and directly above the article hero (article-header). Visually, its background color subtly stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, while its text content is constrained to the same central 1100–1200px column as the rest of the page. The banner is approximately 48–56px tall with 16px vertical padding, and its message text is left-aligned within the column, with 24px padding from the left edge. There is a modest 16–24px gap between the bottom of the site nav and the top of this banner, and another 16–24px gap between the bottom of the banner and the top edge of the article-header, preserving the airy feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears at the top of the scroll stack and moves away as the user scrolls; it does not stick to the viewport and does not overlap the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header is the dominant hero block at the top of the main content area, positioned directly below the notification-banner (if present) and otherwise directly under the global site nav. It spans the full width of the central content column, with its photographic background image extending edge-to-edge within that column. On desktop, it consumes about 30–35% of the initial viewport height, typically between 320–520px tall depending on image aspect ratio. The image is covered by a subtle overlay to ensure text legibility. Inside, a safe-content zone with 24–32px padding from the left, right, and bottom edges houses the article’s elements: a small, muted category or tag line near the top-left; the main headline set in large, bold type anchored left-of-center and toward the lower half of the hero; and an optional subtitle or short description directly beneath the headline. Article metadata (author, date, read time) sits in a smaller row either above the headline or just below it, aligned left. The hero’s bottom edge is clean and flat, with 24–32px of white space separating it from the bookmark-control bar beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero section that participates in normal document flow. The background image scales responsively with viewport width while preserving its aspect ratio, cropping as needed to keep the focal area behind the headline.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-control sits immediately below the article-header as a full-width, flat utility bar that visually echoes the navigation bar from the mockup. It occupies the entire width of the central column and is approximately 64–72px tall, with 16–20px vertical padding. The bar has a white background with a subtle bottom border or hairline divider, and no heavy shadows. Within this bar, the primary bookmark button (e.g., an icon plus label like ‘Save to reading list’) is left-aligned, starting about 24px from the left edge of the column. Any secondary controls (such as a dropdown of reading lists or a subtle share link) appear to the right of the main bookmark button, forming a compact horizontal group with 16px spacing between items. The right side of the bar remains mostly open space, reinforcing the low-density feel. On narrow screens, controls may center or wrap to a second line while remaining within the same flat bar. There is roughly 24–32px of white space between the bottom of this bar and the start of the article-content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned directly under the hero in normal flow; after the user scrolls past the hero, this bar becomes sticky and pins to the top of the viewport, maintaining its width and alignment with the central column while the article-content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content block begins just below the bookmark-control bar, separated by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace. It forms the primary reading column, centered within the page and narrower than the full container: on desktop, the text column occupies around 60–70% of the overall viewport width (typically 680–760px within the 1100–1200px content area), leaving comfortable inner gutters to the left and right. The first line within this block is a small, understated label (such as a section tag or year, mirroring the ‘2026 Family Treasure’ style) aligned to the left, with 8–12px spacing beneath it. Directly below, the lead paragraph begins, set with generous line-height and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs to maintain a relaxed reading rhythm. Inline images, pull quotes, and subheadings are full-width only relative to the article text column, not the full page, and each is given adequate vertical breathing room (24–32px) to stand out. The entire article-content area has 24–32px of padding at the top and bottom (via spacing to neighbors) and concludes with a slightly larger bottom margin (40–48px) before the related-articles-list section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a single, continuous vertical column that scrolls normally with the page; no internal scroll containers or fixed elements inside this component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list appears directly beneath the end of the article body, separated by approximately 40–48px of whitespace to clearly signal a new section. It aligns to the same central content column as the article-content. At the top of this block, a small, all-caps label such as ‘Related’ or ‘You might also like’ is left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing below it, followed by a slightly larger, bold subheading like ‘More for your family’ that anchors the eye. Below the heading, related articles are displayed as a small set of large, editorial cards. On desktop, these cards can be arranged in a two-column grid: each card takes about 48% of the content column width with a 24px gutter between them, and rows are separated by 32px vertically. Each card contains a full-width thumbnail image at the top, followed by the article title and a short teaser, echoing the magazine-style feature card from the mockup but at a smaller scale. On tablets and mobile, the layout collapses into a single column where cards stack vertically. The block ends with 48–64px of vertical spacing before the comments-section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that scrolls with the page; cards inside wrap responsively from two columns to one based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments-section is anchored below the related-articles-list with a generous 48–64px top margin, signaling a shift from editorial reading to community interaction. It spans the same width as the article-content (the central reading column) to feel contained and conversational. The section starts with a clear ‘Comments’ heading aligned left, set in a bold but not oversized style, with a lighter comment count or subtitle next to or just below it. Optionally, the entire comments-area can sit on a subtle, slightly tinted background or be separated from the preceding content by a thin top border to visually segment it without feeling heavy. Inside this container, the comment-list appears immediately below the heading, followed by the comment-form at the bottom. Inner padding of 24–32px between the section edge and its contents ensures breathing room. The bottom of the section has a 48–64px margin before the site footer, maintaining consistency with the overall spacious layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal section in the page’s vertical flow; no independent scrolling or sticky behavior. The entire comments block scrolls together with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Nested inside the comments-section, the comment-list sits directly below the ‘Comments’ heading, separated by around 24–32px of vertical space. It uses the full width of the comments-section’s reading column but maintains an airy feel by giving each comment its own card-like block. Each comment item spans the width of the column and includes 16–24px of internal padding, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between successive comments. The top row of each comment aligns the avatar and author name on the left, with a small timestamp floated or aligned to the right on the same line. The comment text appears beneath this row, full-width, with comfortable line-height and clear separation from other comments. If there are no comments, the list area displays a single centered helper message (e.g., ‘Be the first to comment’) with generous padding above and below, so the section still feels intentional and balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls as part of the overall page; there is no internal scroll or pagination container. The list simply grows downward as more comments are added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-form is placed at the bottom of the comments-section, separated from the last comment in the comment-list by approximately 32–40px to clearly indicate a new interaction area. It spans the full width of the comments reading column. The form is presented as a clean, flat block with a subtle top border or light background contrast, and 24–32px of internal padding on all sides to maintain the editorial spaciousness. Input fields (such as a large comment textarea and any required name/email fields) are stacked vertically and left-aligned, with 16px spacing between each field. The primary action button (e.g., ‘Post comment’) sits at the bottom of the form, aligned to the right on desktop and expanding to full width or centered on smaller screens, with 16–24px of space above it. The bottom of the form has 48–64px of whitespace before the page footer, giving the conclusion of the page a relaxed, uncramped finish.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow and scrolls with the rest of the page; no sticky or floating behavior. It remains anchored as the final element within the comments-section.

name: Shop
title: Shop
description: Browse and refine family-focused products by category, search term, filters, and sorting, then open product detail pages.

core_features: Load available product categories and allow switching between them using getShopCategories.
Apply an in-category search term that updates the product list via the query URL parameter and listProducts.
Display filter controls (price, rating, tags) using getProductFilterOptions and apply selected filters through listProducts, including maxPrice and minRating URL parameters.
Provide sort options (e.g., by price or rating) that update the product listing using the sortBy URL parameter and listProducts.
Support paginated browsing of products using the page URL parameter and listProducts.
Render products as selectable cards; clicking a card navigates to product_detail.html with the product id in the URL.
user_workflows: Browse by category only: 1) User opens shop.html (via header or direct URL). 2) Page reads URL parameters (category, query, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, page). 3) shop-page-controller calls getShopCategories. 4) If category is present in URL and matches a category from getShopCategories, that category is selected; otherwise the first category from getShopCategories is selected. 5) shop-page-controller calls listProducts with selected category and any other URL parameters. 6) Product-grid displays returned products. 7) User can click a different category; category-navigation updates the category URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a refreshed listProducts call.

Search within a category: 1) User types a term into category-search-form while a category is selected. 2) On submit, category-search-form updates the query URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and preserves current category and other filter parameters (maxPrice, minRating, sortBy). 3) shop-page-controller re-reads URL parameters and calls listProducts with category, query, and current filters. 4) product-grid updates with matching products.

Filter by price and rating: 1) On initial load, shop-page-controller calls getProductFilterOptions. 2) filter-panel renders price and rating options from the response and pre-selects options based on current URL parameters maxPrice and minRating where applicable. 3) User adjusts max price and/or minimum rating. 4) On filter apply, filter-panel updates maxPrice and/or minRating in the URL, clears page to the first page, keeps category, query, and sortBy values, and triggers listProducts. 5) shop-page-controller calls listProducts using updated parameters; product-grid refreshes.

Filter by tags (non-URL state): 1) getProductFilterOptions provides tagOptions. 2) filter-panel displays tagOptions as selectable controls. 3) User selects or deselects tags; filter-panel maintains the selected tag values in component state (not in URL). 4) On apply, filter-panel invokes a callback to shop-page-controller with selected tags. 5) shop-page-controller calls listProducts including the tags parameter along with category, query, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and page from URL. 6) UI indicates active tag filters within the filter-panel.

Sort and paginate: 1) sort-and-pagination-bar reads the current sortBy and page from the URL when rendered. 2) User changes the sort order; sort-and-pagination-bar updates sortBy in the URL, resets page to the first page, and preserves category, query, maxPrice, and minRating, then triggers listProducts. 3) User navigates between pages using pagination controls; sort-and-pagination-bar updates the page URL parameter and preserves all other URL parameters. 4) shop-page-controller calls listProducts with the updated page and existing filters/sorting, and product-grid updates.

Open a product detail: 1) User clicks on a product card in product-grid. 2) product-grid (or product-card) reads the clicked product id and initiates navigation to product_detail.html?id=<product.id>. 3) The product detail page uses the id parameter to load and display full details (outside the scope of this page).
interactions: Category selection: Clicking a category in category-navigation sets the selected category, updates the category URL parameter, clears the page parameter to the first page, and triggers a listProducts call through shop-page-controller.

Search form submission: Submitting category-search-form reads the input value, updates the query URL parameter, resets the page URL parameter to the first page, keeps current category and filters, and triggers listProducts.
Clear search: Using a clear action within category-search-form removes the query URL parameter, resets the page URL parameter to the first page, and triggers listProducts with an empty search term.

Adjusting max price: Changing the price control in filter-panel updates a pending maxPrice value in component state; on apply, the component writes maxPrice to the URL (or removes it if unset), resets the page URL parameter to the first page, and triggers listProducts through shop-page-controller.

Adjusting minimum rating: Changing the rating selection in filter-panel updates a pending minRating value in component state; on apply, the component writes minRating to the URL (or removes it if unset), resets the page URL parameter to the first page, and triggers listProducts.

Selecting tags: Selecting or deselecting a tag option updates the tag selection state within filter-panel; on apply, the selected tags are passed to shop-page-controller, which calls listProducts with those tags while leaving URL parameters unchanged for tag state.

Changing sort order: Changing sort selection in sort-and-pagination-bar updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers listProducts. The selected sort option is derived from the current sortBy URL parameter on render.

Paginating results: Clicking a page number or next/previous in sort-and-pagination-bar updates the page URL parameter and triggers listProducts. Existing category, query, maxPrice, minRating, and sortBy parameters remain unchanged.
Product card click: Clicking a product in product-grid navigates via link to product_detail.html?id=<product.id>, using the id from the Product entity.

Initial load and URL changes: On any initial page load or URL parameter change (through navigation), shop-page-controller re-parses the URL parameters, updates internal state for selected category, search term, filters, sort, and page, and calls listProducts accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters and API responses. On load, shop-page-controller reads category, query, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and page from the URL. It then calls getShopCategories to obtain available categories and determines the selected category: if category from the URL matches a category value from getShopCategories, it is used; otherwise the first category from the getShopCategories result is selected and reflected back into the URL by navigation. shop-page-controller also calls getProductFilterOptions on load to populate filter-panel with available priceRanges, ratingOptions, and tagOptions. The selected max price and minimum rating are derived from maxPrice and minRating URL parameters; when absent, filter-panel displays no pre-applied numeric threshold and shop-page-controller passes undefined or equivalent values for those parameters to listProducts. Tag selections are maintained in component state only and are passed to listProducts via the tags parameter without being encoded as URL parameters. The current search term is taken from query in the URL and bound to category-search-form; changing it updates the query parameter. The sortBy URL parameter represents the current sorting mode; if missing, shop-page-controller may pass an undefined or default sortBy value to listProducts based on application configuration without hardcoding any specific sort label here. The page URL parameter indicates the current result page; if page is missing or invalid, shop-page-controller treats it as the first page and may update the URL accordingly. After resolving effective values for category, query, maxPrice, minRating, sortBy, and page, and combining them with any tag selections, shop-page-controller calls listProducts with category, query, minPrice (if needed by internal logic or based on chosen priceRanges), maxPrice, minRating, tags, sortBy, page, and a pageSize defined by layout or global configuration. When the user interacts with filters, search, sorting, or pagination, the interacted component updates the relevant URL parameters (except tags, which remain internal), resets page to the first page when appropriate, and notifies shop-page-controller. Any navigation that changes the URL (either by pushing new history entries or using anchor links) causes a re-evaluation of URL parameters and triggers a new listProducts call, ensuring the view is always consistent with the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state for the Shop page. On load or URL change, it parses URL parameters, determines the active category, search term, filters, sort, and page, and orchestrates calls to getShopCategories, getProductFilterOptions, and listProducts. It passes the results and effective parameters down to child components and receives filter/sort/search changes back from them to update the URL and refresh data.

Displays the list of available product categories returned by getShopCategories and allows the user to switch the active category. Highlights the selected category based on the category URL parameter or the default determined by shop-page-controller.

Provides an input field to search within the currently selected category. The input value is initialized from the query URL parameter. On submit or clear, it updates the query URL parameter (adding or removing it), resets the page parameter to the first page, and notifies shop-page-controller to re-run listProducts.

Renders filter controls using the data from getProductFilterOptions. Displays price range helpers and allows the user to select a maximum price, shows rating options for minimum rating, and lists available tags as selectable options. Pre-populates max price and minimum rating based on maxPrice and minRating URL parameters. Maintains local state for pending filter selections and selected tags. When the user applies filters, it updates maxPrice and minRating in the URL, resets the page URL parameter to the first page, and passes selected tags to shop-page-controller for inclusion in the next listProducts call.

Displays sorting controls and pagination controls for the current product result set. The selected sort option is tied to the sortBy URL parameter, and the current page is tied to the page URL parameter. It also uses the totalCount returned by listProducts, along with the current page size, to compute available pages. Changing the sort selection updates sortBy in the URL and resets page to the first page. Pagination controls update the page URL parameter while keeping other URL parameters intact.

Displays the list of products returned by listProducts as a grid or list of product cards. Handles empty states when no products are returned. Clicking on a product card navigates to the product detail page using the product id in the URL.

Represents a single product within product-grid. Receives a Product entity and displays key information such as image, name, price, rating, and any family-focused indicators provided in the Product data. Clicking the card triggers navigation to product_detail.html with the id parameter set to the product id.

Listens to the results from listProducts and displays an informative message in product-grid when no products are returned for the current category, search, filters, and sorting. Offers an action to clear search and/or filters by resetting related URL parameters (query, maxPrice, minRating, page) and notifying shop-page-controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stacking of large, clearly separated sections. A full-width hero area at the very top (owned by the shop-page-controller) introduces the Shop with a large image and central headline. Directly beneath, a full-width white bar houses the category navigation tabs. Below this, the main content is constrained to a centered column (~1100–1200px) where, in order, the search form, filter panel, sort/pagination bar, and finally the product grid are stacked as distinct blocks. The sort-and-pagination bar plays a similar role to the small section label in the mockup, while the product grid—especially the first row of product cards—acts as the large editorial feature content in the main column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stacking of large, clearly separated sections. A full-width hero area at the very top (owned by the shop-page-controller) introduces the Shop with a large image and central headline. Directly beneath, a full-width white bar houses the category navigation tabs. Below this, the main content is constrained to a centered column (~1100–1200px) where, in order, the search form, filter panel, sort/pagination bar, and finally the product grid are stacked as distinct blocks. The sort-and-pagination bar plays a similar role to the small section label in the mockup, while the product grid—especially the first row of product cards—acts as the large editorial feature content in the main column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top ~30–35% of the initial viewport is given to the hero section with a large lifestyle photograph and Shop masthead. The category-navigation bar directly below it occupies roughly 8–10% of the viewport height as a calm, full-width white strip. The remaining space introduces the main content column, where the search form and filter panel together take about 10–15% of the vertical viewport, the sort-and-pagination bar a shallow 5% or less, and the rest is devoted to the product grid. Horizontally, all substantive content below the hero is centered within a fixed-width column (about 60–70% of the viewport width) with generous gutters (15–20% of viewport width) left and right, mirroring the mockup’s editorial column. Vertical gaps between these blocks are generous (24–40px) and aligned to an 8px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately low-density and airy. Each section—hero, category navigation, search, filters, controls, and grid—is given its own clear band with ample whitespace above and below, avoiding visual crowding. Navigation tabs are spaced with comfortable horizontal padding, the search field is wide and unobstructed, and filter controls are exposed in a single simple band or a lightweight collapsible panel rather than a dense sidebar. The product grid favors moderate product count per view (e.g., 2–3 rows visible above the fold) over packing in many items, with generous gaps between cards. Typography is large and legible, and there are no visually heavy borders or shadows, maintaining a calm, magazine-like browsing experience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the full-width hero image and centered Shop headline, then to a concise supporting line. From there, the gaze naturally drops to the category-navigation bar and scans left-to-right across tabs, identifying key product families. Below that, attention is pulled to the centered search form, then flows down to the filter panel, which allows refinement. Next, the narrow sort-and-pagination bar—styled like a subtle section label with result count and sort control—acts as a transitional line that leads directly into the product grid. The first row of product cards becomes the new focal anchor as the user scrolls, encouraging exploration downward through the grid. Throughout, the layout reinforces a top-down narrative: brand and purpose → high-level structure (categories) → targeted tools (search/filters/sort) → detailed content (products).

layout.overall_description: At the very top of the Shop page, a full-width hero spans edge-to-edge across the viewport, showing a warm, family-oriented photograph. Centered in this hero, a bold but friendly Shop masthead and a brief line of copy introduce the idea of curated family wellbeing products. As you scroll slightly, the hero gives way to a crisp, white category bar that stretches full width: a thin horizontal band containing neatly spaced category tabs that can wrap or scroll if there are many. Below this, the layout tightens into a centered content column about 1100–1200px wide, surrounded by generous left and right whitespace. Within this column, a wide search field sits near the top, centered and flanked by breathing room above and below. Directly beneath, a full-width filter panel forms another clean, lightly outlined band of controls, feeling like a single calm block rather than a busy sidebar. Next, a slim control bar containing result count, sort options, and pagination acts like an understated section label that anchors the start of browsing. Under this, the product grid fills the main column: rows of spacious product cards with large imagery and airy text, separated by ample vertical and horizontal gaps. The overall impression is of an editorial shopping experience—linear, calm, and easy to scan—guiding the user from inspiration to discovery to detailed exploration without clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-page-controller defines the full page scaffolding and hosts both the full-width hero and the centered main content column. At the very top, it allocates the upper ~30–35% of the initial viewport to a hero section that spans 100% of the browser width and height of this region. This hero contains a large, warm lifestyle photograph that bleeds edge-to-edge. Over this image, centered both horizontally and vertically within the hero, sits the Shop headline (e.g., 'Family Shop') in a bold, editorial typeface, with a shorter subtitle directly beneath it; both are center-aligned and occupy roughly 40–60% of the hero’s width, leaving generous margins around the text. Directly below the hero, the controller places a full-width white strip used by the category-navigation bar. Beneath that, the main content area is constrained to a fixed-width, center-aligned column approximately 1100–1200px wide, with equal gutters on left and right (roughly 15–20% of viewport width on large screens). All subsequent components—the search form, filter panel, sort-and-pagination bar, product grid, and empty state—are stacked vertically inside this column, each separated by 24–40px of vertical whitespace. The controller enforces the 8px-based spacing and keeps the background a clean, neutral white to match the minimal, editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the static, scrollable frame for the entire page. The hero area scrolls away normally; the main content column remains centered and fluid within the viewport width, with responsive adjustments but no fixed or overlapping behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-navigation component sits immediately below the hero, occupying a full-width, horizontal bar that stretches edge-to-edge across the browser window. This bar is about 56–72px tall (roughly 8–10% of the visible viewport height) and has a solid white background, clearly distinct from the hero image above. Inside, the navigation tabs are horizontally aligned within the same central content width as the main column; they are centered overall, with left and right padding matching the content column margins. Each tab is presented as a text label with generous horizontal padding (16–24px per side) and vertical padding (8–12px), arranged in a single row on desktop. If there are too many tabs to fit comfortably, they can horizontally scroll or wrap into a second row, but the entire component remains a compact band. Some priority categories may appear slightly bolder or in an accent color to punctuate the left-to-right scan, echoing the mockup’s subtle emphasis treatment. There is at least 16–24px of vertical space between this bar and the hero above, and between this bar and the search form below, maintaining clear separation between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This bar is positioned statically below the hero but may adopt a sticky behavior on desktop: once scrolled past the hero, it can stick to the top of the viewport, providing persistent access to categories while the rest of the page scrolls underneath. It does not overlap the hero; it only becomes sticky after the hero has scrolled out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category-search-form is placed inside the centered main content column, directly beneath the category-navigation bar, with approximately 32px of vertical whitespace separating them. It is aligned to the center of the column and spans about 60–70% of the column width, leaving generous left and right whitespace inside the column. The primary search input is a single, wide text field with rounded or softly squared corners, about 48–56px tall, reinforcing the minimal, modern look. If there is a search button, it is integrated to the right of the field as a compact, inline action rather than a bulky block. The entire search form sits as a distinct band within the column, but it does not have a heavy card outline—just enough margin (24px above, 24px below) so it reads as its own element. On larger screens, the search can be center-aligned; on smaller screens, it stretches closer to 100% of column width while maintaining consistent vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Statically positioned within the content flow. It expands and contracts horizontally with the main content column but does not stick or float; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-panel appears directly below the search form, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the main content column (matching the 1100–1200px constraint) but maintains a light, understated appearance: think of a low, horizontal band or a slim card rather than a heavy sidebar. Internally, the panel has around 16–24px of padding on all sides. At a minimum, it features a left-aligned 'Filters' label or summary text, with key filter controls (e.g., dropdowns or chips for price, age range, category refinements) laid out horizontally next to it where space allows, and wrapping to additional lines if necessary. On desktop, filters are primarily arranged in a single row or two shallow rows, preserving the sense of a horizontal panel. On smaller screens, they wrap neatly, still contained within the same block. There is no persistent left-hand sidebar structure; instead, the filter-panel lives in the main vertical flow as its own clear section above the product grid. If advanced filters exist, a 'More filters' trigger can expand the panel downward, increasing its height but still staying within the same centered column and separated from the product grid by a consistent 24–32px margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level section within the column. It may support a collapsible/expandable interaction, where expanding it pushes the content below downward, but it is not fixed to the side and does not overlay the product grid. It scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-and-pagination-bar is placed immediately beneath the filter-panel, with about 24px of vertical space separating them. It is relatively shallow—around 48–56px tall—and spans the full width of the main content column. Visually, it resembles a refined, understated section label: a thin horizontal band where the left side shows a small, left-aligned line of text (for example, '48 items • For families in 2026') echoing the mockup’s '2026 Family Treasure' label, while the right side contains compact controls such as a sort dropdown and possibly a view toggle. Pagination controls can appear on the far right or below this bar at the bottom of the grid, but the key idea is that this bar visually introduces the product listing section. The typography here is smaller and less bold than headlines, but slightly accented to guide the eye down into the product grid. There is clear, consistent whitespace above (24px) and below (24–32px) so the bar reads as a distinct but lightweight divider between filters and products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Statically positioned, it scrolls away as the user moves down the product grid. On very long lists, a duplicate instance or pagination controls may appear again at the bottom, but the bar itself does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid sits directly below the sort-and-pagination-bar and occupies the remainder of the main content area as the primary visual anchor once the user starts scrolling. It spans the entire width of the centered column. On large desktop screens, products are arranged in a 3-column grid with equal-width columns and consistent gutters—horizontal gaps of about 24–32px between cards and vertical spacing of 32–40px between rows. On very wide screens, this can flex to 4 columns while maintaining ample card width and whitespace; on tablet and mobile, it gracefully collapses to 2 and then 1 column while preserving comfortable spacing. The first row of product cards appears fully within the initial viewport below the fold of the hero, acting as a new focal point as users scroll down. The grid does not touch the browser edges; it always stays within the central column and maintains the editorial sense of a neatly laid-out gallery of stories/products. A generous margin (at least 48–64px) at the bottom of the grid provides a clean endpoint before any footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The grid is a standard responsive grid that expands vertically as more products are loaded or paginated. It reflows seamlessly at responsive breakpoints but does not have any sticky or fixed behavior. The grid area becomes the host region for the empty-state-message when no products are available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card occupies a single cell in the product-grid. Within its column, the card spans the full available column width, with a light background and either no visible border or a very subtle 1px line in a soft neutral tone. Internal padding is generous—typically 16–24px on all sides. At the top of the card, a large product image spans the full card width, with an aspect ratio around 4:3 or square, giving it a photographic, editorial feel. Below the image, the product title is left-aligned, set in a strong but friendly typeface, and allowed to wrap to two lines without feeling cramped. Under the title, supporting metadata (price, brief description, tags like 'Eco-friendly') appear in smaller, lighter text, with 8–12px vertical spacing between elements. Actions (e.g., 'View details' or an add-to-cart icon) are placed toward the bottom right or as a subtle underlined link under the description, but kept visually minimal to preserve the clean, magazine-like aesthetic. Vertical spacing within the card adheres to the 8px scale, with 8–16px between text blocks and 16–24px between the image and the title. The card’s overall height remains moderate, ensuring that 2–3 rows of cards are visible without feeling dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Product cards are fully clickable containers within the grid and respond to hover with subtle cues (e.g., a soft border color change or slight elevation implied by a border, not a heavy shadow). They do not overlap each other and resize proportionally with the grid columns at different breakpoints, maintaining consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears in place of the product-grid’s cards when no products match the current search, filters, or category. It is positioned within the same centered main content column and vertically anchored where the first row of products would normally appear. The message content is horizontally centered and constrained to a narrower width (approximately 480–600px) to keep it readable and prominent. There is generous vertical padding above and below—at least 64px—so it feels like a calm, intentional section rather than a small error note. The text is center-aligned and may include a small, soft illustration or icon above a clear headline (e.g., 'No treasures found') and a short explanatory line beneath, with spacing of 8–16px between elements. Optional action buttons (e.g., 'Clear filters') appear centered below, separated by 24px from the text. The overall style mirrors the editorial tone: minimal, friendly, and non-alarming, with plenty of white space surrounding the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state replaces the visual space that would be occupied by the product-grid’s cards and scrolls like a normal block. It is not fixed or overlayed; when filters are changed and products become available, it disappears and the standard grid layout reappears in its place.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information about a single product, allows saving it to custom product lists, and shows related products for easy comparison.

core_features: Load and display full product details (name, image, category, price, rating, description, features) using getProductDetail(productId) based on the URL parameter id.
Expose a clear product permalink (product_detail.html?id={productId}) so users can open multiple product detail pages in separate tabs or windows for comparison via standard browser actions.

Allow users to add the current product to an existing or new product list using getProductListsOverview() to fetch available lists and saveProductToList(productId, listId, listName) to persist the selection.
After saving a product to a list, optionally navigate the user to the Saved Lists page (saved_lists.html) with URL parameters listType=product_list and listId set to the target list.
Display related products from getProductDetail(productId).relatedProducts and allow navigation to their detail pages (product_detail.html?id={relatedProductId}) in the same tab or a new tab.
Provide a "Back to Shop" navigation that links to shop.html with the category URL parameter derived from the current product’s category (shop.html?category={product.category}).
