Handle loading and error states for data retrieval so users understand when the product is being loaded, cannot be found, or when saving to a list fails.
user_workflows: 1. View a product from the Shop: (a) User clicks a product in shop.html; (b) Browser navigates to product_detail.html?id={selectedProductId}; (c) On page init, product_detail.html reads id from the query string and calls getProductDetail(productId=id); (d) Page displays product information and relatedProducts; (e) User can click the Back to Shop link, which sends them to shop.html?category={product.category}.

2. Open multiple product detail pages for comparison: (a) User is on shop.html or saved_lists.html; (b) For each interesting product, user opens product_detail.html?id={productId} using native browser behavior (e.g., middle-click, context menu 'Open link in new tab'); (c) Each tab independently reads its own id parameter and calls getProductDetail for that specific product; (d) User can switch between tabs to compare details and related products.

3. Add product to an existing list: (a) On product_detail.html, user clicks the 'Save to list' action; (b) The page opens the Add-to-List modal, which calls getProductListsOverview(); (c) The modal lists all existing product lists returned; (d) User selects one list and submits; (e) The page calls saveProductToList(productId=currentProduct.id, listId=selectedListId, listName=null); (f) If success is true, a success message is shown with the updated listItemCount; (g) User may click 'View list' in the confirmation, which navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=product_list&listId={returnedList.id}.

4. Create a new list and add the product: (a) User clicks 'Save to list' on product_detail.html; (b) Add-to-List modal opens and fetches existing lists with getProductListsOverview(); (c) User chooses the option to create a new list and enters a list name; (d) On submit, page calls saveProductToList(productId=currentProduct.id, listId=null, listName={userEnteredName}); (e) On success, the backend creates the list and item, and returns list and item; (f) The modal shows a success state, including the new list’s name and listItemCount; (g) If the user clicks 'View list', navigate to saved_lists.html?listType=product_list&listId={list.id}.

5. View a related product from a product detail page: (a) On product_detail.html, the user scrolls to the related products section; (b) Each related product tile links to product_detail.html?id={relatedProductId}; (c) When the user clicks a tile, the browser navigates to that URL (same tab), or the user may open in a new tab using native browser behavior; (d) The new product detail page reads its own id, calls getProductDetail for that product, and displays its own details and related products.
interactions: Click Back to Shop link to navigate to shop.html?category={currentProduct.category}.
Click browser Back button to return to the previously visited page (shop.html, saved_lists.html, or any other origin), relying on browser history.
Click the 'Save to list' button to open the Add-to-List modal and trigger getProductListsOverview().
Select an existing list in the Add-to-List modal and click the 'Add' or 'Save' button to call saveProductToList with productId and listId.
Choose 'Create new list' in the Add-to-List modal, type a list name into a text input, and click 'Create and add' to call saveProductToList with productId and listName.
Click 'View list' in the post-save confirmation state to navigate to saved_lists.html with listType and listId parameters derived from the saveProductToList response.
Click a related product tile or title to navigate to another product detail page with its own id in the query string.
Use native browser interactions (e.g., open link in new tab) on any product link to view multiple product_detail pages side by side.

Interact with pagination or scrolling (if present) in the related products section to see more relatedProducts without leaving the current product (client-side behavior only; no additional interfaces).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the URL query parameter id. On every page load or when the id parameter changes (e.g., via navigation to another product_detail.html?id=...), the page reads id and invokes getProductDetail(productId=id). The returned product object becomes the single source of truth for all product-specific UI: title, category, imageUrl, price, rating, description, and features, as well as for generating the Back to Shop link (shop.html?category={product.category}). The relatedProducts array from getProductDetail is used to populate the related products grid; each item’s id is used in its own product_detail.html?id=... link. The 'Back to Shop' link is stateless beyond using product.category as the category parameter; the browser Back button behavior is handled by the browser history stack and does not require additional state management. When the user opens the Add-to-List modal, the page calls getProductListsOverview() without URL parameter changes; this list data is kept in component state only for the lifetime of the open modal. Saving a product to a list uses saveProductToList(productId=currentProduct.id, listId or listName) and does not alter the current page URL. After a successful save, if the user chooses 'View list', the page navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=product_list&listId={list.id} using values from the saveProductToList response. No use of localStorage or other persistent client storage is required; all cross-page navigation and state transfer happen via URL parameters and API responses within the allowed interfaces.

components.functionality: On page initialization and whenever the URL query parameter id changes, this component reads id from the URL, calls getProductDetail(productId=id), manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting product and relatedProducts data to child components.

Displays the product’s primary identifying information, such as name and basic category context, using the product passed from product-detail-loader. It may also render a simple breadcrumb or category label based on product.category for context.

Shows the product image or images associated with imageUrl from the product object. If multiple images are supported by the backend via a structured imageUrl field or associated metadata, this component can display them as a gallery; otherwise, it displays the single imageUrl.

Displays the product’s price, currency, rating, and ratingCount using the product data. It renders these values read-only and may include formatted text (e.g., rating as stars) strictly based on the numeric fields without hardcoding any product-specific values.

Shows the product description text and any additional narrative content using product.description. If description is absent, it can omit itself or show a generic placeholder message driven by configuration rather than any hardcoded product text.
Renders the product.features array as an ordered or unordered list of key bullet points. It is purely data-driven from product.features and hides itself when features is not provided or is empty.

Provides a dedicated 'Back to Shop' link that navigates to shop.html with a category query parameter derived dynamically from the currently loaded product.category (shop.html?category={product.category}). It does not modify browser history beyond standard navigation and works alongside the browser’s native Back button.

Renders the primary call-to-action that lets users save the current product to a list. When clicked, it opens the Add-to-List modal and triggers retrieval of product lists via getProductListsOverview().

On open, calls getProductListsOverview() to fetch all existing product lists for the current user and displays them in a selectable list, along with an option to create a new list. On submit, if an existing list is selected, it calls saveProductToList(productId=currentProduct.id, listId=selectedListId, listName=null). If the user chooses to create a new list, it calls saveProductToList(productId=currentProduct.id, listId=null, listName={userEnteredName}). It then shows feedback based on the returned success and message fields, and exposes a 'View list' action that navigates to saved_lists.html?listType=product_list&listId={list.id} when clicked.

Embedded within or associated with the Add-to-List modal, this panel displays the set of product lists returned from getProductListsOverview(), including each list’s name and itemCount. It is read-only and updates whenever getProductListsOverview() is re-run.

Displays the relatedProducts array from getProductDetail as a grid of product tiles. Each tile uses the related product’s id to construct a link to product_detail.html?id={relatedProductId}. Users can click a tile to navigate in the same tab, or use native browser actions to open it in a new tab or window for comparison.

Shows transient or persistent messages related to loading status and save actions, such as errors from getProductDetail or saveProductToList, and confirmations when a product has been added to a list. It consumes message and success values from interface responses and does not use any hardcoded product-specific text.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A full-width hero band at the top combines the product’s primary imagery and title (media gallery + header) in a visually dominant area. Directly below, a full-width meta/action bar echoes the mockup’s navigation bar, housing the back link, price/rating, and primary "add to list" action arranged horizontally within the centered column. Under this, the main content column presents the product narrative in stacked sections: description, features, product lists overview, and finally a related products grid. Auxiliary feedback (notification banner) appears just beneath the action bar, and overlays (loader, modal) sit above all content when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A full-width hero band at the top combines the product’s primary imagery and title (media gallery + header) in a visually dominant area. Directly below, a full-width meta/action bar echoes the mockup’s navigation bar, housing the back link, price/rating, and primary "add to list" action arranged horizontally within the centered column. Under this, the main content column presents the product narrative in stacked sections: description, features, product lists overview, and finally a related products grid. Auxiliary feedback (notification banner) appears just beneath the action bar, and overlays (loader, modal) sit above all content when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the hero (media + header) occupies roughly the top 30–35% of the viewport height, stretching full width. The meta/action bar beneath it takes about 8–10% of the vertical space. The main content column below uses the remaining height and continues down the page, with each section separated by 24–40px of vertical whitespace. Horizontally, the central content column is constrained to ~1100–1200px and visually reads as 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 15–20% whitespace gutters on either side on larger screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps an airy, editorial feel: only a few major components are visible at a time, with generous spacing between them. Typography is allowed to breathe, lists and grids are not overly dense, and no section feels crowded. The meta/action bar items have comfortable gaps, text sections have 16–24px internal padding, and vertical rhythms follow multiples of the 8px base grid, maintaining a calm, low-density reading and browsing experience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the large hero image and the overlaid product title, then moves slightly downward to any supporting subtext in the header. From there, the eye naturally drops to the meta/action bar, scanning left-to-right from the back link, through the price and rating, to the primary "add to product list" button, mirroring the mockup’s category navigation flow. Below that, attention is guided down to a small, left-aligned label line at the top of the description, then into the fuller product story and feature list. The flow then continues to the product lists overview and finally settles on the related-products grid as the last major visual anchor, completing a clean, vertically guided journey.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page reads like a modern, editorial feature in a family lifestyle magazine. At the very top, a full-width hero band dominated by the product’s main image stretches edge-to-edge, with the content column centered within it. The product name and key header text are overlaid near the center of this hero, creating a strong focal point against the warm, photographic backdrop. Directly beneath, a white, full-width meta/action bar mirrors a navigation strip: inside the centered column, the back-to-shop link sits at the far left, the price and rating cluster just right of center, and the primary add-to-list button anchors the far right, all arranged on a single horizontal line with generous breathing room. If a notification is present, a slim banner appears just below this bar, before the storytelling content starts. The main content area then unfolds in a single, centered column: a small, understated label line introduces the product narrative, followed by an open, text-led description block and a softly highlighted features list. Further down, a clean overview panel summarizes the user’s product lists in a calm, dashboard-like card. The page concludes with a broad related-products grid, aligning product cards neatly within the same central column and leaving generous side gutters. When needed, a full-screen loader or a centered add-to-list modal gently overlays this structure, always preserving the underlying minimalist, flat aesthetic without heavy chrome or shadows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay above all other elements. It covers the entire screen from top to bottom and edge to edge with a semi-transparent, warm-tinted white layer, subtly dimming the hero and content beneath. At the exact center of the viewport, a compact spinner or progress indicator is displayed, optionally accompanied by a short status line. The overlay aligns to the same centered grid as the page but is not constrained by the content column, ensuring that regardless of scroll position, the loader always fills the screen until data is ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport as a full-screen overlay; it does not scroll with the page and disappears with a fade or soft transition once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product header is placed inside the top hero band, overlaid on the product-media-gallery. Within the full-width hero, the main content column is centered, and the header sits within the middle portion of that column, roughly spanning 50–60% of the column width. It is horizontally centered in the column and vertically placed around the mid-height of the hero band, with at least 64px of space from the top edge of the hero to keep it balanced. The product title uses bold, large type and is center-aligned, with any subtitle or secondary meta text directly below it, separated by about 8–12px. The text sits against the image with sufficient contrast, potentially supported by a subtle gradient behind the text area, but the overall look remains flat and minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Statically positioned within the hero container and layered above the media using absolute positioning; it scrolls away naturally with the hero band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The media gallery forms the first and most dominant content block on the page, acting as the hero. It spans the full browser width, from the very left edge to the right, directly beneath any global site masthead. Its height is set to approximately 30–35% of the viewport, with a minimum height of around 360px to preserve impact on shorter screens. The primary product or lifestyle image fills this area, cropping gracefully to maintain visual focus. The centered content column sits on top of this image, hosting the overlaid product-header. Optional thumbnail images or secondary media controls can be aligned along the bottom of the hero within the content column, in a tight horizontal strip with about 16px of spacing above the bottom edge, keeping them visually subordinate to the main image. As the user scrolls, the entire hero band (media plus header) moves up and out of view in a single, smooth block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level, full-width section at the top of the content; it is static in the document flow, not sticky, and defines the hero region for the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price and rating block lives inside a full-width white meta/action bar that sits directly below the hero band, visually echoing a navigation strip. This bar runs edge-to-edge horizontally but contains a centered content column constrained to about 1100–1200px. Inside this column, the price-rating-block is placed slightly left of center, occupying approximately 40% of the column width. The price typically appears on the top line in a prominent but clean type, with the rating (stars and count) directly below or beside it, separated by 8–12px. Vertically, the block is centered within a bar that is roughly 80–96px tall, leaving comfortable space above and below the text. To its left is the back-to-shop link near the column edge, and to its right is the add-to-product-list-button, establishing a left-to-right meta/action flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the meta/action bar, aligned using the central grid; on smaller viewports, it may stack above or below adjacent elements while remaining within the same bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section begins in the main content column directly below the meta/action bar (and below any notification banner, if present). It is separated from the bar by about 32–40px of vertical whitespace, reinforcing the distinction between meta/actions and content. The section aligns to the centered column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. At the top of the section, flush left, is a small, understated label line (e.g., the product category, collection, or "Product Story") set in a lighter weight or small caps, visually echoing the mockup’s narrow "2026 Family Treasure" label. About 16px below this label, the main description text flows as one or more paragraphs in a single column. The section has a flat white background, with 24px of internal padding at the top and bottom, and 16–24px between paragraphs, giving the copy a relaxed, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked block within the main content column, expanding vertically based on text length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product features list is placed immediately beneath the description section, separated by a vertical gap of around 24px to maintain hierarchy while clearly associating it with the description. It lives within the same centered content column and spans its full width. The block appears as a lightly differentiated card: a very subtle warm-tinted or off-white background distinguishes it from the pure white page, but there are no borders or shadows, preserving the flat aesthetic. Inside, there is approximately 24px of padding on all sides. A small, left-aligned heading like "Key Features" or "What We Love" appears at the top, followed by a vertical list of bullet points. Each feature item is left-aligned, with about 12–16px of vertical spacing between items, making the list feel scannable without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow; it does not float or stick, simply expands as features are added.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-shop link resides in the meta/action bar that sits directly below the hero. Within the centered content column, it is aligned to the far left, becoming the first element in the horizontal row of controls. The link is text-based, optionally with a left-pointing chevron icon, and is given modest horizontal padding (about 8–12px from the column edge) so it does not feel cramped. It is vertically centered within the bar’s 80–96px height, matching the vertical alignment of the price-rating-block and add-to-product-list-button. Its styling is understated but clearly interactive, serving as a gentle pathway back to the broader shop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the meta/action bar, aligned to the left within the column; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-product-list button shares the meta/action bar with the back-to-shop link and price-rating-block. It is positioned at the far right of the centered content column, creating a strong endpoint in the left-to-right scan across the bar. The button has a medium footprint, with around 16px of horizontal padding and 8–12px of vertical padding inside, and sits on the same horizontal baseline as the price text to create a balanced row. It is surrounded by at least 16px of whitespace from neighboring elements, so it stands out as the key interactive control without feeling oversized. On smaller screens, when the bar content stacks, the button drops below the price-rating-block but remains left-aligned to that block, keeping the hierarchy clear while adapting to the narrower layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow; it opens the add-to-product-list-modal when activated but does not change position itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When invoked, the add-to-product-list modal appears centered over the page content, dimming everything behind it with a semi-transparent overlay that covers the full viewport. The modal panel itself is a clean, flat rectangle with softly rounded corners, sized to roughly 480–600px wide on desktop (about 40–50% of the viewport width) and constrained in height so that its top and bottom sit at least 64px away from the viewport edges. Inside, content is arranged in a single vertical column: a title at the top, the selection/creation form for product lists in the middle, and action buttons aligned to the right at the bottom. The internal padding is generous (around 24px on all sides), and vertical spacing between form controls follows the 8–16px grid, making the modal feel spacious and calm rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay, centered horizontally and vertically in the viewport; if content exceeds the available height, the modal body scrolls internally while the overlay and header/footer remain fixed within the modal frame.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-lists-overview panel sits in the main content column below the features list and above the related-products-grid. It is separated from the features list by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace, signaling a shift from product information to the user’s saved content. The panel spans the full width of the centered column, maintaining the same side gutters as the description and features. It appears as a flat, card-like section with a white or very slightly tinted background and about 24px of internal padding. At the top, a concise heading like "My Lists" is left-aligned, followed by a vertically stacked set of rows or tiles, each representing a product list, with 12–16px spacing between them. Any list-level actions (e.g., view, remove) are subtly aligned to the right within each row. The layout remains single-column on all screen sizes, simply expanding vertically as more lists are added.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow; it grows taller as more data is shown, pushing subsequent content downward while staying within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-products grid forms the final major block of the page, visually anchoring the lower portion of the layout. It begins around 40px below the product-lists-overview-panel, aligned within the same centered content column. At the top of the section, a left-aligned heading such as "Related Products" introduces the grid. Below this heading, product cards are arranged in a responsive grid that respects the 12-column system: on large screens, three cards occupy a row, each spanning roughly 3–4 grid columns, with consistent horizontal and vertical gaps of about 24px between cards. On medium screens, the grid collapses to two cards per row, and on small screens to a single column of stacked cards, while still maintaining generous spacing. The section’s height is determined by the number of related items, but the airy card spacing and the persistent side gutters preserve the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the main content column; it extends downward as more related items are present and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner appears within the main content flow as a slim, horizontally oriented bar. It is placed directly beneath the meta/action bar and above the product-description-section, separated from the meta/action bar by about 8–16px of vertical space so it reads as a distinct layer of feedback. The banner spans the full width of the centered content column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the description. It features a lightly tinted background color corresponding to the message type (success, info, warning, error) while remaining flat and shadow-free. Inside, there is around 16–20px of vertical padding and similar horizontal padding at the sides. The message text is left-aligned and may wrap to multiple lines, with a small dismiss (close) icon aligned to the right. When shown, it pushes the content below down; when dismissed or hidden, the gap closes and the description section moves up to sit closer to the meta/action bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow; it may animate in and out (e.g., slide or fade), but once present it scrolls with the rest of the content and does not remain fixed.

name: Planner
title: Planner
description: Plan and manage family activities and recipes by creating named plans, filtering activities and recipes, assigning them to days of the week, and viewing the schedule by day.

core_features: Display an overview of all existing planner plans and allow creation of new plans using getPlannerPlansOverview and createPlannerPlan.
Open a specific planner plan by planId and show its metadata and items using getPlannerPlanDetail.

Provide an Activities editing view for the current plan where users can filter activities (via getActivityFilterOptions and listActivities) and add selected activities to specific days using addItemToPlannerPlan.
Provide a Recipes editing view for the current plan where users can filter recipes (via getRecipeFilterOptions and listRecipes) and add selected recipes to specific days using addItemToPlannerPlan.
Provide a schedule view that summarizes the current plan by day of week, showing all assigned activities and recipes with resolved details using getPlannerPlanSchedule.
Allow removal of individual activities or recipes from a plan (from any view that shows plan items) using removeItemFromPlannerPlan and then refreshing plan data.
Enable navigation to detailed activity and recipe pages (activity_detail.html and recipe_detail.html) from items listed inside the plan, passing the appropriate id parameter in the URL.
Enable navigation to the broader Activities and Recipes pages (activities.html and recipes.html) from within the planner, passing preferred filter parameters based on the current planner filters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Open Planner and select or create a plan: (1) User navigates to planner.html (without planId). (2) Page controller calls getPlannerPlansOverview to load available plans. (3) User either clicks an existing plan in the overview, or opens the 'Create new plan' form. (4) If creating, user submits name and optional description/dates; the component calls createPlannerPlan. (5) On success, the page updates the URL to planner.html?planId={newPlan.id}&view=activities (or another default edit view) and reloads the relevant plan data. (6) If selecting an existing plan, the page updates or reads the existing planId parameter and loads the plan into the chosen view.

Workflow 2 – Edit a plan via Activities view and add activities to days: (1) User is on planner.html with planId set and view=activities in the URL. (2) The page calls getPlannerPlanDetail(planId) to confirm the plan context and may call getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) to show a compact schedule summary. (3) Activities filter panel calls getActivityFilterOptions to populate filter dropdowns and then calls listActivities with the current filter values and pagination parameters derived from URL. (4) User adjusts filters; the component updates activity-specific query parameters in the URL and re-calls listActivities. (5) From the results list, user clicks an 'Add to plan' action for a chosen activity, selects a dayOfWeek in a day-selection control, optionally adds notes, then confirms. (6) Component calls addItemToPlannerPlan with itemType='activity', the selected activityId, planId, selected dayOfWeek, and notes. (7) On success, the page re-calls getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) (and/or getPlannerPlanDetail(planId)) so the UI reflects the updated plan contents.

Workflow 3 – Edit a plan via Recipes view and add recipes to days: (1) User is on planner.html with planId set and view=recipes in the URL. (2) The page calls getPlannerPlanDetail(planId) and may call getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) to show summary. (3) Recipes filter panel calls getRecipeFilterOptions to populate filter controls, and then calls listRecipes with current filter values and pagination from URL. (4) User refines recipe filters; the component updates recipe-specific query parameters in the URL and re-calls listRecipes. (5) In the recipe results list, user clicks an 'Add to plan' action, selects a dayOfWeek, optionally enters notes, and confirms. (6) Component calls addItemToPlannerPlan with itemType='recipe', the selected recipeId, planId, selected dayOfWeek, and notes. (7) On success, the schedule or summary components refresh via getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId).

Workflow 4 – Review schedule and remove items: (1) User navigates to planner.html with planId set and view=schedule in the URL, or switches to the Schedule view via the tab switcher which updates the view parameter. (2) The page calls getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) to retrieve items grouped by day, with resolved Activity or Recipe details. (3) For each item, the schedule view offers actions: view details (navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activityId} or recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId}) and remove from plan. (4) If user chooses to remove, component calls removeItemFromPlannerPlan(planItemId). (5) On success, component re-calls getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) to refresh the daily listing. (6) User can switch back to activities or recipes views; the view parameter is updated and the corresponding data is fetched.

Workflow 5 – Navigate to broader Activities or Recipes pages from Planner: (1) User is in Activities or Recipes view within Planner, with certain filters active. (2) User clicks 'Browse more activities' or 'Browse more recipes'. (3) Planner constructs a URL to activities.html or recipes.html, mapping relevant current filter values to outgoing query parameters (e.g., ageGroup and location to corresponding parameters for activities.html, mealType to recipes.html). (4) Browser navigates to the new page; Planner does not handle data loading after that navigation.

Workflow 6 – Deep link into Planner from other pages: (1) External pages (index.html, recipes.html, activities.html, saved_lists.html) link to planner.html with a planId parameter (and optionally view). (2) On load, Planner reads planId and view from the URL. (3) If planId is present, the page immediately calls getPlannerPlanDetail(planId) and getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) as needed for the requested view. (4) User sees the specified view of the given plan without needing to reselect it from the overview.
interactions: Selecting an existing plan in the plan overview updates the URL planId parameter and loads that plan via getPlannerPlanDetail (and, depending on view, getPlannerPlanSchedule).
Submitting the 'Create new plan' form triggers createPlannerPlan and, on success, updates planId and view in the URL and reloads the page state around the new plan.
Switching between Activities, Recipes, and Schedule views updates the view parameter in the URL and triggers the appropriate data fetching for that view.

Changing activity filters (ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, tags, sortBy, page) updates corresponding activity filter parameters in the URL and issues a new listActivities call with the updated parameters.

Changing recipe filters (mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxPrepTimeMinutes, maxTotalTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag, sortBy, page) updates corresponding recipe filter parameters in the URL and issues a new listRecipes call with the updated parameters.
Clicking pagination controls for the activities or recipes results updates the respective page parameter in the URL and re-calls listActivities or listRecipes.

Clicking 'Add to plan' on an activity or recipe opens a day-of-week selection and optional notes input; confirming triggers addItemToPlannerPlan and a subsequent refresh of schedule data via getPlannerPlanSchedule.
Clicking 'Remove' on a scheduled plan item (in schedule or summary) triggers removeItemFromPlannerPlan and then re-fetches the schedule via getPlannerPlanSchedule.
Clicking an activity title in the schedule or summary navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activityId}.
Clicking a recipe title in the schedule or summary navigates to recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId}.
Clicking 'Browse more activities' in the Activities view navigates to activities.html with ageGroup and location parameters derived from the current activity filters.
Clicking 'Browse more recipes' in the Recipes view navigates to recipes.html with a mealType parameter derived from the current recipe filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for its state. The primary parameters are planId (string) and view (string). If no planId is present, the Planner shows the plans overview and does not load plan-specific data. When planId is present, getPlannerPlanDetail(planId) is called to load core plan information; additional calls depend on the current view. The view parameter controls which main sub-view is active. Suggested values are 'activities', 'recipes', and 'schedule'. When view is 'activities', the Activities editing view is shown and the page calls getActivityFilterOptions and listActivities with filters derived from activity-specific URL parameters (e.g., actAgeGroup, actLocation, actCostType, actMaxDurationMinutes, actTags, actSortBy, actPage, actPageSize). When view is 'recipes', the Recipes editing view is shown and the page calls getRecipeFilterOptions and listRecipes with filters from recipe-specific URL parameters (e.g., recMealType, recMaxCookTimeMinutes, recMaxPrepTimeMinutes, recMaxTotalTimeMinutes, recMaxCaloriesPerServing, recMinRating, recServings, recHealthTag, recSortBy, recPage, recPageSize). When view is 'schedule', the page calls getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) and displays the schedule grouped by day. The tab switcher updates the view parameter and triggers re-rendering without persisting view state elsewhere. Activity and recipe filters, as well as their pagination and sorting settings, are always mirrored into and restored from the query string, allowing direct linking and back/forward navigation. After mutating operations (createPlannerPlan, addItemToPlannerPlan, removeItemFromPlannerPlan), the URL parameters remain unchanged except when a new plan is created (planId is updated); plan content is refreshed via follow-up calls to getPlannerPlanDetail and/or getPlannerPlanSchedule so that the UI stays in sync with server state.

components.functionality: Reads and interprets URL query parameters (planId, view, and filter-related parameters) on load and on every navigation event; determines which main sub-view to render; orchestrates data fetching for the active view by invoking the appropriate interfaces; updates the URL via history APIs when user interactions change state (plan selection, view changes, filters, pagination) without reloading the page.

When no planId is in the URL, calls getPlannerPlansOverview to display the list of existing PlannerPlan entities with itemCount; lets user select a plan to work on or open a plan creation form. On selection, it updates the URL with planId and a default view and delegates to the routing controller to load the selected plan.

Provides inputs for creating a new PlannerPlan (name, optional description, optional startDate and endDate). On submit, calls createPlannerPlan with the entered values. If the response indicates success, it updates the URL (planId to the returned plan.id and view to an edit view) and notifies the routing controller to reinitialize state for the new plan.

Displays current plan metadata (name, description, optional start/end dates) once a planId is active. It relies on data provided by getPlannerPlanDetail(planId). It may show basic stats (such as total item count per day) if provided in the detail or by an additional call to getPlannerPlanSchedule, but does not modify the plan as there is no update interface.

Renders tab controls for the three main views associated with the current plan: Activities, Recipes, and Schedule. The active tab is determined by the view parameter in the URL. When a tab is clicked, it updates the view parameter (e.g., view='activities', 'recipes', or 'schedule') and triggers the routing controller to fetch and render the corresponding data.

Visible when view='activities' and planId is present. On initialization, calls getActivityFilterOptions to populate filter controls such as ageGroup, location, costType, and duration options. When the user changes any filter or sorting or pagination setting, it updates the corresponding activity-related URL parameters and informs the routing controller so that listActivities is called with the new parameters.

Visible when view='activities'. Calls listActivities using ageGroup, location, costType, maxDurationMinutes, tags, sortBy, page, and pageSize values taken from URL state. Displays a paginated list of Activity items with an 'Add to plan' control for each. When 'Add to plan' is used, opens the day selection flow and then calls addItemToPlannerPlan with itemType='activity'. On success, triggers a refresh of schedule-related data.

Visible in the Activities view. Reads current activity-related filter settings (particularly ageGroup and location) from URL state and builds a navigation URL to activities.html with ageGroup and location query parameters. Clicking the link performs a client-side navigation to that URL.

Visible when view='recipes' and planId is present. On initialization, calls getRecipeFilterOptions to populate filter controls such as mealTypes, servingOptions, ratingOptions, cookTimeOptionsMinutes, and calorieOptionsPerServing. Whenever a filter, sorting, or pagination setting changes, it updates recipe-related URL parameters and notifies the routing controller so that listRecipes is called with the updated parameters.

Visible when view='recipes'. Calls listRecipes using mealType, maxCookTimeMinutes, maxPrepTimeMinutes, maxTotalTimeMinutes, maxCaloriesPerServing, minRating, servings, healthTag, sortBy, page, and pageSize from URL state. Shows a paginated list of Recipe items with an 'Add to plan' action per item. When the user completes the add flow, it calls addItemToPlannerPlan with itemType='recipe' and then refreshes schedule-related data.

Visible in the Recipes view. Reads the current recipe filter state, especially mealType, from URL parameters and constructs a navigation URL to recipes.html with the mealType parameter set. Clicking the link sends the user to that URL.

Shared component used by both activities and recipes results lists when the user chooses 'Add to plan'. It provides controls for selecting a dayOfWeek (based on PlannerPlanItem.dayOfWeek enum values) and entering optional notes. On confirmation, it returns the selected dayOfWeek and notes to the calling list component, which then executes addItemToPlannerPlan.

Visible when view='schedule' (and may also be embedded in a compact form in edit views). Calls getPlannerPlanSchedule(planId) to retrieve a structured list of days, each with items containing PlannerPlanItem plus resolved Activity or Recipe details. Renders items grouped by dayOfWeek. Each item offers actions to navigate to its detail page and to remove it from the plan.

Encapsulates actions available on each item within the schedule view. For a given PlannerPlanItem, if itemType is 'activity', clicking the item title navigates to activity_detail.html?id={activityId}; if 'recipe', it navigates to recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId}. The remove control calls removeItemFromPlannerPlan(planItemId) and, on success, notifies the schedule view to refresh data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The Planner page adopts the mockup’s stacked, editorial structure inside a centered 12-column content column (max-width ~1120px, 15–20% viewport gutters on desktop). At the top of the Planner content column sits a large hero-style current-plan header. Immediately beneath it is a compact plan-management strip containing the plans overview and the entry point to the create-plan form. Below that, a full-width tab bar functions like the mockup’s navigation band, switching between three main views: Schedule, Activities, and Recipes. Within each tab, content is arranged linearly from top to bottom: the Schedule tab shows a small, left-aligned schedule label line followed by a large schedule grid card; the Activities and Recipes tabs each show a two-column internal layout (left filter panel, right results list) contained within the central column. Overlays (day selection dialog and item actions) float above this main flow but are visually light to preserve the minimal, editorial aesthetic.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Planner page adopts the mockup’s stacked, editorial structure inside a centered 12-column content column (max-width ~1120px, 15–20% viewport gutters on desktop). At the top of the Planner content column sits a large hero-style current-plan header. Immediately beneath it is a compact plan-management strip containing the plans overview and the entry point to the create-plan form. Below that, a full-width tab bar functions like the mockup’s navigation band, switching between three main views: Schedule, Activities, and Recipes. Within each tab, content is arranged linearly from top to bottom: the Schedule tab shows a small, left-aligned schedule label line followed by a large schedule grid card; the Activities and Recipes tabs each show a two-column internal layout (left filter panel, right results list) contained within the central column. Overlays (day selection dialog and item actions) float above this main flow but are visually light to preserve the minimal, editorial aesthetic.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The current-plan hero header occupies roughly the top 25–30% of the initial viewport within the content column. The plan-management strip (plans overview and optional create-plan form) and the tab bar together take another 10–15% of vertical space at load. The remaining viewport height is dedicated to the active tab’s primary content: on Schedule, the schedule view card fills most of the remaining space (about 50–60% of viewport height), echoing the large feature card in the mockup; on Activities/Recipes, the filter panel occupies about 30–35% of the column width on the left and the results list occupies 65–70% on the right. Major sections (hero, plan controls, tabs, tab content) are separated by 24–40px vertical gaps, aligned to the 8px spacing scale. Internal padding for cards, panels, and the tab bar is typically 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a calm, low-density feel. Only one primary mode (Schedule, Activities, or Recipes) is visible at a time via tabs, preventing the page from feeling like a dashboard. Each major block (hero header, plan controls, tab bar, tab content) is given generous whitespace above and below. Within lists, only a handful of items are visible above the fold, with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. Filter panels and forms use clear grouping and comfortable padding, avoiding cramped controls. Overlays are compact and centered around the user’s focus, but with sufficient white space and minimal chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the large current-plan header at the top center/left of the column, mirroring the hero in the mockup. From there, the gaze moves slightly down to the plan-management strip where existing plans can be scanned horizontally and a new plan can be initiated. The eye then drops to the full-width tab bar, scanning left-to-right across the main Planner sections. On the Schedule tab, the eye proceeds downward to the small, left-aligned schedule label line (analogous to '2026 Family Treasure'), and then lands on the large schedule grid card, which becomes the main focal anchor as users scroll. On Activities/Recipes, the eye moves from the tab label down into the filter panel (left), then across to the results list (right), following a subtle Z-pattern. Contextual overlays (day selection, item actions) appear close to the user’s point of interaction, creating a tight local flow without disturbing the global vertical rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The Planner page sits within a spacious, center-aligned content column reminiscent of an editorial lifestyle site. At the top of this column, a large, soft-colored current-plan header spans the full column width, with generous padding and a prominent plan title, setting a calm, welcoming tone similar to a hero article card. Just beneath it, a low-profile plan-management strip lets users scan existing plans and start a new one without breaking the visual quiet. A full-width, flat tab bar follows, behaving like a warm, minimal navigation band that anchors the Planner’s main modes: Schedule, Activities, and Recipes. Only one mode is visible at a time. On the Schedule tab, a slim, left-aligned line labels the current day or week, and directly below it a broad schedule card fills the column, presenting the family’s week in a clean grid that echoes the large feature image card in the mockup. On the Activities and Recipes tabs, the content column internally splits into a left filter panel and a right results list, but the overall experience still feels like a single, editorial article column due to consistent margins, typography, and restrained chrome. Inline dialogs and item actions float lightly above the schedule or lists, close to the user’s focus, without adding heavy shadows or clutter. The result is a planner that feels more like a thoughtfully designed family magazine spread than a utilitarian tool, guiding the user smoothly from plan context, to section navigation, to focused planning tasks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and lives conceptually at the top of the Planner page hierarchy, synchronizing the URL with the active tab (Schedule, Activities, Recipes), the selected plan, and any filter state. It does not occupy any visible space in the layout and does not affect the visual grid directly; instead, it ensures that when the user switches tabs or plans, the correct content appears within the existing visual structure of the centered column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; no spatial behavior. It underpins routing and state but does not render UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans overview appears directly below the current-plan header, anchored to the left edge of the central content column. It occupies a horizontal strip about 60–70% of the column width on desktop, leaving comfortable whitespace to the right. Within this strip, existing plans are represented as a horizontal row of pill-like labels or compact cards, spaced 8–16px apart, with the current plan subtly emphasized. The strip sits approximately 24px below the bottom of the hero header and about 16–24px above the create-plan form (when expanded) or the tab bar (if the form is closed), maintaining a clean separation between the header and the navigation-like plan selection. The overall height of this strip is modest, roughly 56–72px, preserving the low-density editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow. On small screens, the row can horizontally scroll within the same strip, but it remains part of the stacked vertical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create-plan form is positioned immediately beneath the plans overview, aligned with the left edge of the content column and spanning the full column width. By default, it can appear in a collapsed state triggered by a 'Create new plan' affordance located at the right end of the plans overview strip. When expanded, it forms a light, card-like block with 24px internal padding on all sides and 24–32px vertical spacing above and below, extending the hero feel into a more functional area. The form’s height will typically be 25–35% of the column height in the viewport, depending on fields, but it remains visually airy, with distinct field groups separated by 16–24px. It is fully contained above the tab bar so that plan definition feels like a preparatory step before diving into the tabbed views.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding and collapsing in place and pushing surrounding content up or down. On mobile, it still spans the full width of the column and remains immediately above the tab bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan header is the visual hero of the Planner section and is placed at the very top of the Planner content column, directly under any global site masthead or page-level hero. It spans the full width of the central column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport width) with generous left and right margins relative to the viewport. Vertically, it occupies roughly 30% of the initial viewport height, creating a substantial but not overwhelming presence. The header appears as a full-bleed card within the column, often with a soft, solid background color or a subtle lifestyle image, with the plan title set in large, bold type, aligned center-left. Supporting text (e.g., date range, family name) sits below the title with comfortable 8–16px spacing, mirroring the headline/subtitle relationship in the mockup. There is at least 32px of whitespace below this header before the plans overview begins, reinforcing its status as a distinct, hero-like block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the Planner content column. As the user scrolls, it scrolls offscreen, handing visual focus to the tab bar and main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab switcher is positioned directly beneath the plan-management area (plans overview and optional create-plan form), spanning the full width of the central column and reading as a flat, full-width navigation bar. It sits roughly 24–32px below the component above it and 24px above the tab content. The bar has 12–16px of vertical padding and presents tab labels (e.g., Schedule, Activities, Recipes) horizontally, centered or left-aligned, with ample 24–32px horizontal spacing between them. If there are more tabs than can fit on one line, they wrap gracefully into two lines while maintaining generous padding, echoing the mockup’s multi-row navigation behavior. The active tab is indicated with an accent color and a subtle underline or weight change, not heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky relative to the viewport after the user scrolls past the hero: once the top of the tab bar reaches the top of the viewport, it remains fixed there (offset appropriately below any global header) so that section switching remains accessible while the user scrolls through tab content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Activities tab is active, the activities filter panel appears in the main tab content area as the left column of a two-column layout. This panel begins approximately 24px below the tab bar, aligned with the left edge of the central column, and extends vertically down the page. On desktop, it occupies about 30–35% of the content column width, leaving the remaining 65–70% for the results list on the right. The panel’s interior is card-like: a white background with 24px padding on all sides and clear grouping of filter controls with 16–24px vertical spacing. The top of the panel is aligned with the top of the activities results list, creating a neat horizontal baseline. There is ample whitespace (24–32px) below the bottom of the panel before any subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Activities tab content. On smaller screens, the panel stacks above the results list, maintaining the same 24px gap below the tab bar and preserving the top-to-bottom reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities results list sits to the right of the activities filter panel when the Activities tab is active. It begins at the same vertical position as the filter panel, about 24px below the tab bar, and aligns with the right edge of the central content column. On desktop, it occupies roughly 65–70% of the column width. The list is contained within a subtle card-like region or simply a clean white background with clear typographic hierarchy; individual activity rows are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing and may include a short description and primary action, but remain visually light. Above the fold, only a manageable number of activities (e.g., 4–6) are visible to preserve the low-density feel. The list extends downward, and there is an extra 16–24px gap below the final item before the 'Browse more' link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Activities tab. On smaller screens, it stacks below the filter panel and expands to full column width, maintaining consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The activities 'Browse more' link appears directly below the activities results list within the Activities tab. It is right-aligned to the results list column, sitting approximately 16–24px beneath the last visible activity item, and 32px above any subsequent content or the bottom of the viewport. The link is lightly styled—accent color text with minimal or no iconography—to maintain the clean editorial look, and occupies only a single line, preserving generous whitespace around it so it does not compete with the list items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it anchors the end of the results list and scrolls with the page. On small screens, it aligns with the full-width results list, remaining right-aligned within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Recipes tab is active, the recipes filter panel occupies the same visual role and space as the activities filter panel. It starts approximately 24px below the tab bar, aligned to the left edge of the central column, and forms the left column of a two-column layout. Its width is about 30–35% of the column, with the recipes results list filling the remaining width to the right. The panel is visually consistent with the activities filter: a light, card-like block with 24px internal padding and clearly separated filter groups spaced 16–24px apart. The top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the recipes results list, and there is at least 24–32px of whitespace below the panel before the end of the tab content or page bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Recipes tab. On mobile, it stacks above the recipes results list and spans the full width of the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipes results list sits to the right of the recipes filter panel when the Recipes tab is active, occupying approximately 65–70% of the central column’s width. It begins at the same vertical position as the filter panel, about 24px below the tab bar. Each recipe entry appears as a clean row or compact card with 16–24px vertical spacing between items, and may include a small thumbnail, title, and key metadata, but is kept visually simple to preserve the airy editorial aesthetic. The list extends downward with only a small number of recipes visible initially, preventing the view from feeling like a dense catalog. The bottom of the list is followed by a 16–24px gap before the 'Browse more' link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inside the Recipes tab content. On smaller screens, the list stacks beneath the filter panel and expands to full column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipes 'Browse more' link is positioned directly beneath the recipes results list, aligned to the right edge of the list column. It appears around 16–24px below the last recipe item, creating a clear visual endpoint for the list, and remains surrounded by whitespace so it reads as a gentle call to see more rather than a heavy button. It mirrors the styling and alignment of the activities 'Browse more' link, reinforcing consistency across tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling naturally with the Recipes tab content. On mobile, it aligns right within the full-width results column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-item day selection inline dialog appears as a lightweight overlay when the user chooses to add an activity or recipe to the schedule. Visually, it is centered over the relevant content area—either above the schedule grid or near the right edge above the results list—but always within the horizontal bounds of the central column so it feels connected to the Planner rather than the entire page. Its width is about 40–50% of the content column on desktop (never stretching full width), with 24–32px padding inside and ample whitespace around it. The dialog is minimal: a simple white surface, subtle border or hairline outline (no heavy shadow), and compact options for choosing a day/time. The background content remains visible but slightly deemphasized, either via a very light overlay tint or lowered contrast.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating overlay anchored to the Planner column, not fixed to the viewport edges. It scrolls with the underlying page if the user scrolls, but is intended to be used in-place. On mobile, it expands to about 80–90% of the column width and stays centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the Schedule tab is active, the schedule view occupies the primary content area beneath the tab bar. At the very top of this tab’s content, a slim, left-aligned text line—such as 'Week of May 4–10' or 'Today'—acts like the '2026 Family Treasure' label from the mockup. It sits about 24px below the tab bar and spans only the left portion of the column, with generous whitespace above and below (~8–16px) to keep it understated. Directly beneath this label, the main schedule card fills the full width of the central column, forming a large, contained block that echoes the feature article card in size and presence. The schedule grid itself occupies roughly 50–60% of the viewport height on initial load, with 24px internal padding around the edges and 16px spacing between days or time slots. The card rests on a clean, white or very light-tinted background, clearly separated from surrounding whitespace by its edges and a 32–40px gap below before any further content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the Schedule tab content. It scrolls as part of the page. On smaller screens, the grid may become more vertical and scrollable within the same card area, but the overall block remains full-width within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Schedule item actions are contextual controls that appear in close proximity to individual items inside the schedule view. For each scheduled activity or recipe, these actions either sit inline at the right edge of the item row/cell or appear as a compact, floating strip attached to the bottom or top edge of the selected item. They are visually restrained—small icons or text links with minimal chrome—ensuring the schedule grid remains clean and readable. Vertical and horizontal spacing around these controls (8–12px) keeps them from crowding the item text. When multiple items are visible, only the hovered or selected item’s actions are emphasized, preventing visual clutter across the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline/anchored within the schedule grid. They appear or fade in place as the user focuses on an item and do not affect overall layout flow significantly (no large reflows). On mobile, they may appear beneath the item content within the same cell, still aligned to the right where possible.

name: Tools
title: Tools
description: Lists all planning and budgeting tools available on the site, highlights recommended tools, and lets users open specific tools such as the Wellness Budget Planner.

core_features: Fetch and display the list of available tools using getToolsOverview, including each tool’s name, description, and recommendation flag.
Visually highlight tools marked as recommended, and optionally allow users to filter to recommended tools only.
Support different view modes (for example, list or grid) controlled via URL parameters so users can share or revisit a preferred layout.
Support deep links that focus on a specific tool (for example, wellness_budget_planner) using URL parameters, by scrolling it into view and/or visually emphasizing it.
Allow users to navigate from a tool entry to its dedicated tool page, including navigation to wellness_budget_planner.html when the clicked tool corresponds to the Wellness Budget Planner.
user_workflows: Browse all tools from main navigation: 1) User clicks the 'Tools' link in the global header and lands on tools.html. 2) On page load, the page controller reads any URL parameters (for example, view or filter). 3) The page calls getToolsOverview with no parameters. 4) When the response is returned, the controller stores the list of tools in page state. 5) The tools list component renders all tools, optionally grouped or marked as recommended based on the isRecommended property. 6) The user can change view mode or filters, which updates the URL parameters and re-renders the list client-side without new API calls.

View only recommended tools: 1) The user clicks a 'Recommended only' toggle or filter control. 2) The filter control updates the URL (for example, adding or removing a filter=recommended parameter) using history.replaceState or a full navigation. 3) The page controller reacts to the URL change, updates its internal filter state, and applies a client-side filter on the tools list based on isRecommended. 4) The tools list component re-renders to show only tools with isRecommended set to true.

Follow a deep link from an article to a specific tool: 1) A user clicks a deep link inside an article that points to tools.html with a toolKey parameter (for example, tools.html?toolKey=wellness_budget_planner). 2) On tools.html load, the page controller reads toolKey from the URL. 3) The page calls getToolsOverview. 4) After data is loaded, the controller searches the tools array for a tool whose key matches the toolKey URL parameter. 5) If found, the deep-link handler instructs the tools list to scroll that tool into view and mark it as focused/active (for example, via an internal selectedToolKey state). 6) The user can then optionally click the focused tool to navigate to its dedicated tool page.

Open a specific tool from the list: 1) The user scrolls through the tools list and clicks on a specific tool entry. 2) The tools list component emits a tool selection event with the tool’s key. 3) The page controller checks if the selected tool’s key matches a known navigation mapping (for example, key equals wellness_budget_planner). 4) If the key matches wellness_budget_planner, the browser navigates to wellness_budget_planner.html. 5) For other keys, the controller can navigate according to configured routes for that key (if such routes exist in the application’s routing configuration), or remain on the page if no route is defined.
interactions: On page load (tools.html): Triggered when the user visits via the header navigation or a deep link. The page controller reads URL parameters (such as view, filter, and toolKey) and calls getToolsOverview. While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown instead of the tools list.

Change view mode: The user clicks a control to switch between available view modes (for example, list or grid). This interaction updates the view URL parameter (for example, tools.html?view=list or tools.html?view=grid), updates internal state, and causes the tools list component to re-render in the new mode, without re-calling getToolsOverview.

Toggle recommended-only filter: The user interacts with a checkbox or toggle (for example, 'Show recommended tools only'). The filter component updates the filter URL parameter (for example, filter=recommended or filter=all). The controller updates the in-memory filter state and passes a filtered subset of tools (based on isRecommended) to the tools list component.

Deep-link focus to a specific tool: When tools.html is opened with a toolKey URL parameter, or when a user chooses a tool from a focus control, the controller sets an internal selectedToolKey state and instructs the tools list to scroll that specific tool into view and visually indicate its focus state. If the toolKey does not match any tool from getToolsOverview, the controller leaves the list un-focused.

Click on a tool card/row: When the user clicks a tool entry, the tool-card or tools-list component fires an onClick/onToolSelect event with the selected tool’s key. The page controller handles this: if key equals wellness_budget_planner, it performs a navigation to wellness_budget_planner.html; if the key maps to another route in the application’s routing configuration, it navigates accordingly; if there is no mapping, it can ignore the click or display a non-data-dependent message.

Retry after error: If the getToolsOverview call fails (network or server error), the page shows an error state with a 'Retry' action. When the user clicks retry, the controller calls getToolsOverview again without changing the URL parameters.

Keyboard and focus interaction: Users can move focus between tool items (for example, using Tab and arrow keys), and pressing Enter or Space on a focused tool item triggers the same handler as clicking, allowing keyboard navigation into wellness_budget_planner.html or other mapped tool pages.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page does not use localStorage; all restorable state is represented through URL parameters and in-memory state. On every load or navigation to tools.html, the page controller reads URL parameters and initializes the view accordingly. Example parameters: 1) view: Defines the layout mode for the tools list (for example, view=list or view=grid). If view is absent or invalid, the controller uses a default mode defined in code and does not override it with any stored value. 2) filter: Controls which subset of tools is shown. If filter=recommended, the controller sets its internal filter state to show only tools where isRecommended is true; otherwise, it shows all tools. 3) toolKey: When present (for example, tools.html?toolKey=wellness_budget_planner), the controller stores this value in selectedToolKey after loading data and attempts to locate a tool from getToolsOverview whose key matches this parameter. If such a tool exists, the tools list component is instructed to scroll to and highlight that tool. If it does not exist, no special focus is applied. The URL is the only persistence mechanism: whenever the user changes a view or filter setting via UI controls, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters (using navigation or history APIs) and then re-derives internal state from the updated URL. The getToolsOverview interface is always called without parameters; view, filter, and toolKey influence only client-side filtering, layout, and focus, not the API request.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading from getToolsOverview, interprets URL parameters into internal state (view mode, filter, selectedToolKey), orchestrates which tools are passed to child components, and handles navigation when a tool is selected.
Displays a loading indicator or message while the getToolsOverview request is in progress and hides itself once data has either loaded successfully or failed.
Shows an error message when getToolsOverview fails and provides a retry action that triggers the controller to re-fetch the tools list.
Allows the user to switch between available view modes (for example, list or grid) and synchronizes the chosen mode with the view URL parameter via the controller.

Provides controls (such as a toggle or checkbox) to limit the displayed tools to recommended ones only, and communicates the selected filter to the controller so it can update the filter URL parameter and filtered tool list.

Displays a subset of tools that are marked as recommended (isRecommended true), giving quick access to popular or suggested tools. It receives the full tools list from the controller and internally filters by isRecommended.

Shows the main list of tools according to the current view mode and filter state. It receives the full tools array and state flags (for example, view mode, whether to show only recommended) from the controller and renders a collection of tool-card components. It also applies a visual focus style to the tool whose key matches selectedToolKey when present.

Represents a single tool item with dynamic name, description, and recommended status as provided by getToolsOverview. When clicked or activated via keyboard, it emits a selection event containing the tool’s key back up to the tools-list and controller.

Displays a message or guidance when getToolsOverview returns an empty array or when the combination of filters applied by the controller results in no visible tools. It appears only when the filtered tools collection is empty.

Translates the toolKey URL parameter into a selectedToolKey state in the controller after getToolsOverview has loaded. It identifies the matching tool in the data set, and signals the tools-list component to scroll that tool into view and highlight it.

Receives tool selection events (tool keys) and performs page navigation. For the tool whose key is mapped to the Wellness Budget Planner (for example, key equals wellness_budget_planner), it navigates to wellness_budget_planner.html. For other keys, it uses the application’s routing configuration if available, or does nothing if no route is configured.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global full-width hero and site navigation sit at the very top (outside this page’s component set). Directly beneath the global nav, the Tools page content forms a single centered column. At the top of this column, a subtle page label and title (via the page controller) echo the '2026 Family Treasure' line from the mockup. Immediately below that, a large, editorial-style recommended strip acts as the hero card for tools. Under the hero strip, a horizontal control band contains view-toggle and filter controls. Below the controls, the main tools list fills the rest of the page, made up of spacious item cards. Loading, error, and empty states replace the recommended/list region as needed, while deeplink/navigation handlers operate invisibly in the background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: global full-width hero and site navigation sit at the very top (outside this page’s component set). Directly beneath the global nav, the Tools page content forms a single centered column. At the top of this column, a subtle page label and title (via the page controller) echo the '2026 Family Treasure' line from the mockup. Immediately below that, a large, editorial-style recommended strip acts as the hero card for tools. Under the hero strip, a horizontal control band contains view-toggle and filter controls. Below the controls, the main tools list fills the rest of the page, made up of spacious item cards. Loading, error, and empty states replace the recommended/list region as needed, while deeplink/navigation handlers operate invisibly in the background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column is constrained to ~1100–1200px and centered, occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 15–20% whitespace gutters on each side. Vertically, the area below the global nav begins with a 32–40px margin before the Tools label/title line. The recommended strip occupies roughly the top third of the main Tools content section, with generous 32–48px margins above and below. The control bar (view + filters) consumes a slim band (about 8–10% of the visible Tools section height) and sits just above the tools list. The tools list then takes up the remaining vertical space with cards stacked or arranged in a loose grid, and continues down the page as the user scrolls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density and editorial. Each major block (label/title, recommended strip, controls, list) is separated by 24–40px vertical gaps. Internal padding on cards and strips is ~24px on all sides, with navigation/controls using 12–16px padding per item. Tool cards are not tightly packed; even in a grid, each card has 24px gaps horizontally and vertically. Text lines are kept short within the central column, with plenty of white space around interactive elements to create a calm, magazine-like reading and browsing experience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves top-to-bottom: first the global hero and nav, then the small, left-aligned Tools label/title in the content column. From there, attention is pulled immediately to the broad recommended tools strip, which acts as a hero-style focal element similar to the '100 BEST…' card in the mockup. Below that, the eye naturally catches the horizontal line of view-toggle and filter controls, scanning left-to-right. This anchors the user in the structure of the tools list. Finally, attention moves down into the tools list itself, where each tool card can be scanned vertically. In loading or error scenarios, centered status or error content temporarily becomes the focal point inside the same content column, preserving the same vertical flow and alignment.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width hero and navigation, the Tools page content sits in a single, centered column with a maximum width of about 1100–1200px and wide, breathable gutters on both sides. A modest vertical gap from the nav (around 32–40px) introduces a slim, left-aligned line that quietly labels the section (for example, '2026 Tools & Planners'), setting an editorial tone similar to a magazine section heading. Just beneath this, a large, banner-like recommended tools strip spans the full width of the content column, with a soft background color, generous padding, and a bold headline that mirrors the hero-article treatment in the mockup—this is the main visual anchor when you arrive on the page. Below that strip, a clean, low-profile horizontal band houses the view-toggle and filter controls, aligned within the same column; these controls are roomy and uncluttered, letting the user choose how they see and narrow the tool list. The main tools list stretches down from this band, displaying tool cards in a comfortable list or grid layout, with ample white space between cards and around their content. Loading, error, and empty states all appear centered within this main content column, replacing the recommended strip and/or list as needed but always respecting the same margins and alignment, so the page retains its calm, structured editorial flow regardless of state. Invisible controllers and deeplink handlers sit behind this composition, orchestrating behavior without adding visual noise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller anchors the Tools section within the central content column that begins just below the global navigation. Visually, it manifests as a subtle header band at the top of the Tools content area. This band starts about 32–40px beneath the site nav and is left-aligned within the 1100–1200px content width. It contains a small, single-line label (e.g., '2026 Tools & Planners') in a lighter weight, and optionally a slightly larger page title just beneath it, echoing the '2026 Family Treasure' label from the mockup. The label line hugs the left edge of the content column, with 16–24px padding above and below, and a comfortable 24–32px gap below it before the recommended strip begins. The page controller does not introduce any heavy borders or backgrounds; it relies on whitespace and typography to delineate the start of the Tools section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in the normal vertical flow at the top of the Tools content column; it does not scroll independently or stick, but scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state occupies the same central column region normally used by the recommended strip and tools list. When active, it appears directly below the Tools label/title band, maintaining the same 24–32px top margin. Within the content column, a simple, unobtrusive loading indicator (e.g., spinner and short 'Loading tools…' text) is centered horizontally. Vertically, it sits in a generous block of whitespace, roughly 200–300px tall, so that it feels intentional and calm rather than cramped. The indicator itself keeps to a compact width (no more than 25–30% of the column), surrounded by breathing room on all sides. Other content like the list or recommended strip is hidden to avoid visual clutter, preserving a clean, editorial feel.
