component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown and hidden dynamically within the main content flow. It does not overlay the entire page; instead, it replaces the recommended strip and tools list region within the content column while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display appears in the central column where the tools list would ordinarily begin, just below the Tools label/title and in place of the recommended strip and list when errors prevent loading. It is centered horizontally within the 1100–1200px column, with a 24–32px vertical gap from any content above. The error message is presented in a medium-width block (around 40–50% of the column width) to keep line lengths readable, with 24px internal padding and ample white space around it. A succinct headline and body copy are stacked vertically with 8–16px gaps, and optionally a retry button sits beneath, aligned left within the error block. The error panel may use a light-tinted background or subtle border to distinguish it, but it remains visually flat and minimal, consistent with the mockup’s lack of heavy chrome. Below the error block, additional whitespace (32–48px) reinforces that this is a self-contained message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal flow, replacing the recommended strip and tools list area when triggered. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay or fix itself to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-toggle controls sit within a horizontal control band just above the tools list, inside the central content column. This band starts approximately 24–32px below the bottom edge of the recommended tools strip (or below the page header when no recommended strip is shown). It spans the full width of the column, with 16–24px vertical padding and no distinct background beyond the default page white, relying on spacing to separate it from neighboring sections. Within this band, the view-toggle controls are aligned toward the right side of the column, forming a small cluster of toggle buttons or icons (e.g., list vs grid) with 8–12px horizontal spacing between them and 8–12px padding inside each control. On narrower screens, they can wrap or stack beneath the filters, but on desktop they remain a compact group in the upper right of the tools list area, balancing the filters on the left. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this control band from the first row of tool cards below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow. It scrolls away as the user moves down the list. Optionally, in long lists, this band can become sticky within the viewport just under the global nav, but by default it behaves like a normal block element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls share the same horizontal control band as the view toggles, positioned just above the tools list within the central content column. They are left-aligned, starting at the left edge of the column, roughly 24–32px beneath the recommended tools strip. Filter chips, dropdowns, or input fields run from left to right, with each control having 8–12px internal padding and 8–16px gaps between controls. The overall height of this filter area matches the view-toggle area, forming a single, tidy row on larger screens where filters appear on the left and view toggles on the right. If there are many filters, they can wrap into a second line, but generous vertical spacing (8–12px between rows) keeps them airy and readable. The band itself is visually flat, relying on whitespace above and below (16–24px) rather than borders or shadows to set it apart from the recommended strip and the list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow, sharing the same band as the view toggles. It scrolls with the page; optionally, the combined filter/view bar can be configured to stick below the global nav when the user scrolls deeply into the list, but its default behavior is non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommended tools strip functions as a hero-style card within the Tools content, directly below the Tools label/title band from the page controller. It begins about 24–32px under the label line and spans the full width of the central content column (~1100–1200px), effectively appearing as a large, editorial feature card, similar in scale and prominence to the '100 BEST…' hero article in the mockup. The strip uses a soft, solid background color to distinguish it from the white page, with generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides). Inside, a bold, large-type headline announcing the recommended tools sits at the upper left, with a concise supporting description beneath it, and potentially a primary action button (e.g., 'Open Wellness Budget Planner') aligned left below the copy. Visual elements like small thumbnails or an illustration can appear to the right on larger screens, but they do not overpower the text. A 32–40px vertical margin separates the bottom of this strip from the filters/view controls band below, emphasizing its role as a featured, self-contained highlight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and integrated into the main vertical flow. It anchors the top of the Tools content section and scrolls off-screen as the user moves down to the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tools list occupies the main body of the Tools page, starting immediately below the filter/view controls band. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the last control from the first row of tools. Within the central content column, the list presents tool cards either as a single, comfortable column (list view) or as a loose, two-column grid (grid view) on desktop, depending on the active view-toggle state. In grid mode, each row contains two tool cards, each taking roughly 48–50% of the column width with a 24px horizontal gap between them, and 24–32px vertical gaps between rows. In list mode, each card spans nearly the full width of the column with 24–32px gaps between cards. The list extends down the page, with plentiful whitespace above, between, and below cards to maintain the calm editorial feel. There is no sidebar; the list remains centered and aligned with the rest of the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally below the controls and scrolls vertically with the page. It is replaced or visually suppressed when loading, error, or empty states are active for this data region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each tool card is a self-contained, flat panel within the tools list, designed as a mini editorial feature. In the central column, a card either fills nearly the full width (list view) or about half the width (grid view), always maintaining at least 24px of outer spacing from neighboring cards. Internally, the card has 24px padding on all sides. At the top left, a clear tool name is set in a strong, but not oversized, heading style, with a brief descriptive line beneath it in smaller text, separated by 4–8px. Any meta information (e.g., category, time to complete) appears in a smaller, lighter style above or below the main description, with 4–8px spacing. A primary call-to-action (e.g., 'Open Planner') sits aligned to the left at the bottom of the card, possibly paired with a subtle secondary link on the right. Cards maintain a white background with no or very subtle borders, relying on the surrounding whitespace and consistent padding to stand out; shadows are minimal or absent to honor the flat, editorial look. Vertical space between stacked cards is 24–32px, preserving the low-density reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained entirely within the tools-list region and participates in its layout logic (single column or grid). Cards do not have independent sticky or floating behavior; they scroll with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state display appears in the main list region when filters or data conditions result in no tools to show. It is positioned directly below the filter/view controls band, centered within the central content column. The empty-state panel has a moderate width (around 40–50% of the column), leaving generous margins on either side, and is surrounded by 32–48px of vertical whitespace to feel calm and intentional. Inside, a friendly headline and a short explanatory message are stacked with 8–16px spacing, and a clear action (e.g., 'Clear filters') is placed beneath, aligned left within the panel. The panel may use a very light tint or simple outline to differentiate it from the background without adding heavy chrome. No tool cards are shown when this state is active, so the empty-state block becomes the primary focal element in this area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the tools list area while visible, appearing in the same place in the vertical flow. It scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The deeplink handler does not render a visible element; instead, it governs behavior for linking directly into specific tools or filtered views. Conceptually, it is anchored to the Tools page as a whole and responds to URL parameters or state changes by adjusting which tools are highlighted, which filters are active, and which view mode is selected. Any visual impact it has—such as pre-opening a particular tool card or pre-filtering the list—is expressed through those existing visual components (filters, list, cards) without adding new UI chrome or occupying physical space on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and occupies no dedicated layout space. It operates in the background, synchronizing URL and application state for the existing components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation handler is also non-visual, managing transitions between the Tools page and specific tool experiences or other sections of the site. It resides logically at the page level, listening to interactions on tool cards and controls, and triggers route changes without rendering its own UI. The only visible effects are navigations and focus changes in existing components; it does not introduce new bars, buttons, or overlays.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, with no footprint in the spatial layout. It hooks into the app’s routing and history layers behind the scenes, leaving the editorial layout and white space uninterrupted.

name: Wellness Budget Planner
title: Wellness Budget Planner
description: Plan, view, and edit a monthly wellness budget by month and year, including category allocations, then save the configuration for future visits.

core_features: Read the target month and year from URL query parameters and load any existing wellness budget for that period using getWellnessBudgetForMonth.
Display and edit the total monthly wellness budget amount for the selected month and year.
Allow users to add, rename, reorder, and remove budget categories, assigning an amount to each category.
Continuously calculate and display the sum of category amounts and compare it with the total monthly budget to indicate over/under allocation.
Allow users to add optional notes for the selected month’s budget.
Validate user input (required fields, numeric values, non-negative amounts, and consistency between category totals and overall total) before saving.
Save the wellness budget and category allocations for the selected month and year using saveWellnessBudget, handling both creation and updates.
Allow users to switch between months and years via a selector that updates the URL parameters and reloads the appropriate budget data.
Provide navigation back to tools.html via a 'Back to Tools' interaction.
user_workflows:
Open planner for a given month/year:
1. User navigates to wellness_budget_planner.html with optional month and year query parameters.
2. On page load, the page-init logic reads month and year from the URL.
3. If either parameter is missing, the logic derives current month and year from the client date and updates the URL to include these values before continuing.
4. The page calls getWellnessBudgetForMonth with the resolved month and year.
5. If an existing budget is returned, the total amount, notes, and category list are populated; if not, empty inputs are shown for user entry while still bound to the selected month/year.

Create a new monthly wellness budget:
1. User confirms or selects the desired month and year in the month/year selector.
2. The total amount field is left empty initially; the user enters a numeric value.
3. The user adds one or more categories via the add-category action and enters a name and amount for each.
4. As the user types amounts, the summary panel updates the allocated total and indicates whether it matches, exceeds, or is below the overall total.
5. The user optionally enters notes for this month.
6. The user clicks the Save button; the save form validates all fields.
7. If validation passes, saveWellnessBudget is called with totalAmount, month, year, categories, and notes.
8. On a successful response, the page updates its in-memory budget state with the returned budget and categories and shows a success message.

Edit an existing monthly wellness budget:
1. User opens the planner for a month/year that already has a saved budget.
2. getWellnessBudgetForMonth returns an existing WellnessBudget and associated WellnessBudgetCategory records.
3. The total amount, notes, and category list are pre-filled from the response.
4. The user adjusts the total amount and/or any category names and amounts, or adds/removes categories.
5. The summary panel recalculates allocation and displays any mismatch with the total.
6. The user clicks Save; the form validates the updated data.
7. On passing validation, saveWellnessBudget is called again, overwriting the existing budget for that month/year.
8. On success, updated data from the response replaces the current state and the success status is displayed.

Switch between months and years:
1. User changes the month or year in the selector.
2. Before navigating, if there are unsaved changes, the page can prompt the user to confirm leaving without saving.
3. On confirmation, the selector updates the URL query parameters to the new month/year.
4. The page-init logic reacts to the URL change, calls getWellnessBudgetForMonth for the new month/year, and updates all bound fields.
5. The user can then view or edit the budget for the newly selected period.

Handle validation errors and save failures:
1. User attempts to save with missing total amount, missing category names, invalid numbers, or other validation issues.
2. The save form prevents the save call and surfaces inline error messages and/or a summary of validation issues.
3. If saveWellnessBudget returns success=false or an error message, the error is displayed in a notification area without clearing the user’s inputs.
4. The user corrects the issues and retries saving until success.

Return to tools:
1. User clicks the Back to Tools control.
2. If there are unsaved changes, the page may prompt for confirmation before navigating.
3. On confirmation (or if no unsaved changes), the browser navigates to tools.html without additional parameters.
interactions:
Month/year selection:
- User selects a month from a dropdown or similar control and/or changes the year via an input or dropdown.
- onChangeMonth/onChangeYear handlers update the planned target month/year in state.
- A navigation action updates the URL query string with the selected month and year and triggers a reload or soft navigation, which in turn triggers getWellnessBudgetForMonth.

Total budget input:
- User clicks into the total amount input and types a numeric value.
- onChange validates that the value is numeric and non-negative, updating the in-memory WellnessBudget.totalAmount.
- onBlur can trigger additional checks (e.g., ensuring that the total is not less than the sum of category allocations, or tagging a validation warning if so).

Category management:
- Add category: User clicks an 'Add Category' control. onAddCategory appends a new, empty category row (with generated id and position) to the in-memory categories array.
- Edit category name: User focuses the category name field and types a value. onUpdateCategoryName updates the corresponding WellnessBudgetCategory.name in state and clears any validation error for that field.
- Edit category amount: User types a numeric amount. onUpdateCategoryAmount updates amount, validates that it is numeric and non-negative, and triggers a recalculation of total allocated amount.
- Remove category: User clicks a remove control on a category row. onRemoveCategory removes the item from the array and normalizes positions.
- Reorder categories: User drags a category row or uses controls to move it up/down. onReorderCategories updates the positions and ordering in the state array.

Budget summary and validation feedback:
- Whenever totalAmount or any category amount changes, onRecalculate in the summary panel computes the sum of category amounts and the difference from totalAmount.
- If the allocation exceeds the total, the panel sets a validation status (e.g., over-allocated) that can disable the Save button until resolved.
- If the allocation is below the total, the panel can still allow saving but mark the budget as under-allocated.
- Validation states (field-level and overall) are propagated back to the save form component via onValidationStateChange.

Notes input:
- User types free-form text into the notes field for contextual information about the month’s budget.
- onChange updates WellnessBudget.notes in state.
- onBlur can mark the field as touched, influencing how validation messages are shown (e.g., minimum/maximum length if applicable).

Saving the budget:
- User clicks the Save button.
- onSubmit runs aggregated validation for totalAmount, month, year, categories, and notes.
- If any validation fails, submission is cancelled and errors are displayed; no call to saveWellnessBudget is made.
- If validation passes, onSubmit calls saveWellnessBudget with the current in-memory values and shows a loading state while awaiting the response.
- On success, onSaveSuccess updates the local WellnessBudget and WellnessBudgetCategory state from the returned objects, clears the unsaved-changes flag, and displays the message from the response.
- On failure, onSaveError displays the returned message (if present) and keeps the current inputs intact.

Unsaved changes and navigation:
- Any modification to total amount, categories, or notes sets an internal unsavedChanges flag.
- When the user attempts to change month/year or click Back to Tools, onBeforeNavigation checks unsavedChanges.
- If unsavedChanges is true, a confirmation step is presented; if the user confirms, navigation proceeds; otherwise, navigation is cancelled and the user remains on the page.

Back to Tools navigation:
- User clicks a Back to Tools link or button.
- onClick triggers navigation to tools.html, following unsaved-changes checks as described above.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is determined by the URL query parameters month and year. The canonical URL pattern is wellness_budget_planner.html?month=<month_key>&year=<year_number>. On initial load, the page-init component reads these parameters. If they are missing or invalid, it derives the current month and year from the client’s date and updates the browser URL to include them before proceeding. Once a valid month/year is available, getWellnessBudgetForMonth(month, year) is called. If a WellnessBudget is returned, it becomes the base state (totalAmount, notes, createdAt, updatedAt), and the associated WellnessBudgetCategory array becomes the editable category list, ordered by position. If getWellnessBudgetForMonth returns no budget (or budget is null), the page initializes an empty budget state bound to the selected month/year without persisting anything until the user saves. Changing the month or year via the selector updates the URL parameters and triggers the page-init logic again, which fetches the corresponding budget for the new period. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; any persisted data comes solely from saveWellnessBudget, and any previously saved data is read solely via getWellnessBudgetForMonth. In-memory state (current budget, category list, validation status, unsavedChanges flag, and transient messages) is cleared or reinitialized whenever the effective month/year (as defined by the URL parameters) changes.

components.functionality: Initializes the Wellness Budget Planner page by resolving the target month and year from URL parameters or the current date, updating the URL if needed, and fetching any existing budget data for that period using getWellnessBudgetForMonth. It manages loading and error states and exposes the current WellnessBudget and WellnessBudgetCategory list to other components as shared page state.

Provides controls for selecting the target month and year whose budget is being viewed or edited. It reflects the currently active month/year from page state and, when changed by the user, updates the URL query parameters and triggers the page-init logic to load the corresponding budget.

Captures and displays the total wellness budget amount for the selected month and year. It supports numeric input, basic validation (required, non-negative), and notifies dependent components when the value changes so they can recalculate allocation summaries.

Manages the collection of budget categories for the current month. It displays existing categories from the loaded budget, allows adding new categories, editing names and amounts, deleting categories, and reordering them. It maintains an in-memory representation of the WellnessBudgetCategory array and keeps positions consistent.

Calculates and displays summary information about the current budget, including the total monthly amount, the sum of all category amounts, and the difference between them. It determines whether the budget is balanced, under-allocated, or over-allocated and exposes this as validation state that can enable or disable saving.

Captures optional free-text notes associated with the wellness budget for the selected month and year. It binds directly to the WellnessBudget.notes field and participates in unsaved-changes tracking.

Wraps the editable budget fields and coordinates validation and saving. On submission, it validates totalAmount, month, year, categories, and notes, and if valid calls saveWellnessBudget. It then processes the response to update page state, manage success/error messages, and clear the unsaved-changes flag.

Displays non-field-specific messages such as loading status, save success messages from saveWellnessBudget, and errors from either getWellnessBudgetForMonth or saveWellnessBudget. It subscribes to events from the page-init and save form components.
Provides a control to navigate back to tools.html from the planner. It checks for unsaved changes before navigating and, if necessary, prompts the user to confirm leaving the page without saving.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s linear, stacked structure: a full-width hero at the very top (using the page-init as a photographic, editorial masthead for the Wellness Budget Planner), followed immediately by a full-width, white month/year control bar that visually echoes the navigation bar in the mockup. Below this, the main content sits in a centered column: a small, left-aligned section label line introduces the budget editor area, followed by one large, visually unified 'feature card' that contains the core budget tools (total budget, category editor, summary, notes, and save). The back navigation link and status messages are integrated subtly near the top of the main column so they don’t break the hero–nav–feature sequence.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s linear, stacked structure: a full-width hero at the very top (using the page-init as a photographic, editorial masthead for the Wellness Budget Planner), followed immediately by a full-width, white month/year control bar that visually echoes the navigation bar in the mockup. Below this, the main content sits in a centered column: a small, left-aligned section label line introduces the budget editor area, followed by one large, visually unified 'feature card' that contains the core budget tools (total budget, category editor, summary, notes, and save). The back navigation link and status messages are integrated subtly near the top of the main column so they don’t break the hero–nav–feature sequence.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport height, stretching edge-to-edge with its content constrained to the central 1100–1200px grid. The month/year selector bar sits directly beneath the hero, about 8–10% of vertical space with 16–20px vertical padding. Below that, generous vertical spacing (24–40px) separates the selector from the main content column. The main budget editor feature section spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, centered with 15–20% gutters on larger screens. Inside the editor, the total budget input and category list take most of the width, while the summary panel may share horizontal space on large screens but collapses into the vertical flow on smaller screens.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a calm, low-density editorial feel: large paddings (16–24px) inside sections, sizable margins (24–40px) between stacked components, and no visually crowded areas. Form fields are grouped into clear, breathing sections rather than tightly packed controls. The summary panel, notes, and save area are visually separated with whitespace and subtle dividers rather than heavy boxes, preserving the light, magazine-like aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the centered hero title and subtitle in the photographic masthead, then drops slightly to the month/year selector bar that functions like a site section nav. From there, the eye moves down into the main column to a small left-aligned label indicating the current budget context (e.g., month and year), then to the prominent total budget input. The flow continues down through the category list editor and across to the summary panel on larger screens, before moving downward again to notes and the save area. Status and error messages appear just above the main editor to be noticed without disrupting the editorial flow. The back-to-tools link is a quiet tertiary element at the top-left of the main content region, discoverable but not competing with the hero or primary tools.

layout.overall_description: The Wellness Budget Planner page opens with a full-bleed hero image that sets a warm, family-oriented tone, with the title 'Wellness Budget Planner' and a short, centered subtitle overlaid in large, editorial type. Immediately beneath this, a full-width white bar stretches across the page, containing the month/year controls aligned within a centered 12-column grid, reminiscent of a navigation bar of categories. As the user scrolls slightly, they arrive at the main content column, which is visibly narrower than the full viewport and centered with generous side gutters. At the top-left of this column, a discreet 'Back to tools' text link and any status or error messaging sit above a small, single-line section label indicating the current planning period. Below that, a large, unified budget editor 'feature section' occupies the width of the column: the total monthly budget input is presented prominently first, followed by a spacious list of editable categories on the left and a compact summary panel aligned to the right on larger screens but stacking naturally on mobile. Beneath these, a wide notes area invites reflective input, and finally a horizontally-aligned save bar closes the feature section. Overall, the page feels like a modern, editorial article that happens to be interactive—a calm, vertical flow with clear landmarks and ample whitespace guiding users from understanding to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component forms a full-width hero masthead at the very top of the page, spanning edge-to-edge across the viewport and occupying roughly 30–35% of the initial viewport height on desktop. A large photographic background (family, home, or wellness imagery) fills this area. Within this hero, content is constrained to a centered column about 1100–1200px wide. The main title 'Wellness Budget Planner' is centered both horizontally and vertically (or slightly above center for a more editorial feel), set in large, bold typography. Directly beneath the title, a single-line or two-line subtitle in a smaller, lighter weight is center-aligned, describing what the planner does. There is generous vertical spacing (about 24–32px) between the top of the viewport and the masthead text, and between the title and subtitle, emphasizing the spacious, editorial tone. The hero fades directly into the month-year selector below without a hard border, possibly with a very subtle bottom shadow line or a soft gradient to indicate the transition, but overall staying visually flat and minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block at the top of the document flow; scales responsively in height with min and max height constraints to preserve readability of the overlaid text on smaller and larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month-year-selector sits immediately below the hero, functioning as a full-width control bar similar to the navigation bar in the mockup. It uses a white background that contrasts against the hero image above and stretches edge-to-edge horizontally. Inside, content is aligned to the same central 1100–1200px grid as the hero. The bar has comfortable vertical padding (approximately 16–20px top and bottom) and a subtle bottom border or hairline rule to separate it from the content below. The month and year controls (dropdowns, segmented controls, or input fields) are laid out horizontally, left-aligned within the content column, with at least 16px spacing between them. On larger screens, optional quick-step controls such as 'Previous month' / 'Next month' or preset chips can align to the right side of the bar, echoing the right-aligned items in a navigation row; on smaller screens, these elements wrap into a second row or become horizontally scrollable chips, maintaining generous padding around each item. The overall effect is a clean, nav-like band that clearly communicates the current planning period and invites users to change it before they move into the main editor.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section that scrolls with the page; on tablet and mobile it increases in height to accommodate wrapping or horizontal-scrolling controls while preserving clear touch targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'navigation-back-to-tools' link is positioned at the top-left of the main content column, just below the month-year selector bar and aligned to the same left edge as the column content. There is a modest vertical gap of about 24px between the bottom of the month-year bar and this link, so it feels visually separated but still part of the upper meta layer of the page. The link is set in a smaller, unobtrusive text style (e.g., regular weight, slightly muted color), possibly accompanied by a simple left arrow icon. It does not span the full width—rather, it occupies only the space of the text, leaving generous whitespace to its right. Below this breadcrumb-like link, there is another 16–24px of vertical spacing before any labels or the main budget editor feature card begin. This placement keeps the back link easily discoverable but clearly secondary to the hero and month/year controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the main column; scrolls naturally with the page and maintains its relative position beneath the month/year selector on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status-and-error-messages panel is located in the main content column directly beneath the 'Back to tools' link and above the primary budget editor section. It spans the full width of the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport, max 1100–1200px) but is visually shallow, using a single-line or short multi-line message with 12–16px of padding on all sides. The panel uses a tinted background (e.g., soft green for success, subtle warm red or amber for errors) and a thin border or left accent bar to differentiate it from the white page background, consistent with a minimal editorial alert style. There is approximately 16px of vertical spacing above and below the panel so it doesn’t touch either the back link or the section label/editor card, preserving the spacious, low-density feel. When no status or error is present, this panel collapses entirely, allowing the hero–selector–editor flow to feel continuous.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main column that appears and disappears in the normal document flow based on state; no fixed or sticky behavior, to avoid disrupting the smooth vertical reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The total-budget-input is the first major interactive element within the main budget editor feature section, which itself sits inside the centered content column. It is positioned near the top of this section, following a small left-aligned label line that indicates the current planning period (e.g., 'Monthly budget for March 2026'), echoing the '2026 Family Treasure' label in the mockup. The total budget field is given strong visual emphasis: its label appears in a medium-weight type, left-aligned, with generous spacing (8–12px) above the input. The input control spans roughly 50–60% of the width of the main column on desktop, with ample padding inside (12–16px) and around it (at least 16px margin above and below), and may be accompanied by a right-aligned hint or small helper text, keeping within the same row but not crowding the field. On smaller screens, the input naturally expands to nearly full width of the column. The effect is that the total budget feels like the primary entry point into the feature section, much like a strong headline inside a feature card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element within the main editor section; participates in responsive behavior by increasing to nearly full width on narrow viewports while keeping its label stacked above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-category-list-editor sits directly below the total budget input within the same main feature section, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full available width of the central content column on mobile and tablet, and on larger screens it primarily anchors the left two-thirds of the feature section, leaving room for the summary panel to align on the right. Each category row is displayed with clear horizontal alignment—category name, allocation amount, and any controls—using a consistent grid and at least 8px vertical spacing between rows to maintain a light, scannable feel. The entire list editor is wrapped in subtle internal padding (16–24px) at the section level, with a soft boundary such as a light divider at the top and/or bottom, but without heavy borders or shadows. Conceptually, this block reads as the main 'body copy' of the feature card: dense with functionality but visually airy, allowing users to skim and edit categories without feeling overwhelmed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that grows vertically as categories are added; on wide screens it shares a horizontal row with the summary panel, occupying the left portion of a two-column layout inside the feature section. On narrow screens it stacks above the summary panel in a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-summary-panel is visually tied to the budget-category-list-editor as its counterpart and is placed to the right of the category list on larger screens, within the bounds of the same main feature section. On desktop, it occupies roughly the right one-third of the content column width, aligned to the top of the category editor so that its header lines up horizontally with the top of the category list. It is designed as a compact, card-like panel with a light, solid background (slightly tinted relative to the page white) and internal padding of about 16–24px. The summary numbers and indicators are arranged vertically with clear typographic hierarchy, leaving ample space between lines (8–12px), and a modest vertical gap (16–24px) separates this panel from the notes section below. On tablet and mobile, the summary panel falls beneath the category list, still spanning the full width of the central column, but maintains its internal padding and distinct background so it reads as a discrete, digestible summary card. This echoes the 'feature card' effect from the mockup, making the summary the visual anchor users scan after editing categories.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main feature section; on large screens it behaves like a right-hand, non-sticky column aligned with the category list, while on smaller screens it naturally stacks below in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notes-input sits below the combined category editor and summary panel area, serving as the reflective, editorial-style element of the feature section. There is a clear vertical separation—approximately 32px—between the bottom of the category/summary area and the notes component, ensuring it feels like a new sub-section. The notes label is left-aligned, with a subtle, calm tone in the typography, and the text area itself spans nearly the full width of the content column (minus standard horizontal padding), with a height sufficient to invite more than just a single word (e.g., 3–5 lines visible). Internal padding within the textarea follows the 12–16px guideline, and external vertical spacing (16–24px above and below) keeps it from touching adjacent components. The visual treatment stays minimal and flat—no heavy borders, just a clean outline and perhaps a slightly softer background than pure white—to align with the editorial, journaling feel appropriate for family and wellness notes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width element within the main column that expands vertically as users type more content; remains in the normal document flow on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-save-form is the closing section of the main feature area, positioned directly beneath the notes input with 24–32px of vertical space separating the two. It spans the width of the central content column but uses only as much vertical space as needed for its controls. Primary actions (such as 'Save budget') are right-aligned within the column, with secondary actions (like 'Cancel' or 'Reset') to the left of the primary button, forming a compact horizontal control group. On smaller screens, these buttons stack vertically with at least 8–12px between them to maintain comfortable tap targets. The section may have a very subtle top border or faint divider to signal the end of the editor, but it otherwise blends into the white background to maintain the minimal chrome aesthetic. Generous whitespace (24–40px) is left below this form before the end of the page or any footer elements, echoing the breathing room seen between major sections in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the main feature section; scrolls with the page and reflows from a right-aligned horizontal button row on desktop to a stacked layout on narrow screens.

name: Newsletter Preferences
title: Newsletter Preferences
description: Manage newsletter topics, delivery schedule, and the email address used for receiving the family lifestyle and wellbeing newsletter.

core_features: Fetch and display the current newsletter preferences for the single user by calling getNewsletterPreferences when the page loads.
Provide a form where the user can review and edit their newsletter settings: frequency, optional day of week, optional time of day, selected topics, and email address.
Allow the user to enable or disable newsletter delivery by changing the frequency field (including a "none" option to effectively unsubscribe).
Allow the user to select specific newsletter topics via checkboxes, mapped to the topics array in the NewsletterPreference model.
Allow the user to set or change the delivery schedule (frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay) within the constraints of the NewsletterPreference schema.
Allow the user to update the email address that will receive the newsletter, bound to the email field in NewsletterPreference.
Validate the form client-side (e.g., required email, required topics when frequency is not "none", required frequency) before submitting.
On form submission, call updateNewsletterPreferences with the current form values (frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, topics, email) and handle success or error responses.
Display success or error feedback after saving preferences, using both the response from updateNewsletterPreferences and optional URL parameters.
Provide navigation back to the home page (index.html) via a "Back to Home" control or site logo, optionally using a returnTo URL parameter.
user_workflows: Workflow: Load and review existing newsletter preferences. Steps: 1) User opens newsletter_preferences.html via footer link or direct URL. 2) On page initialization, the page calls getNewsletterPreferences with no parameters. 3) While loading, the form is disabled and a loading state is shown. 4) When getNewsletterPreferences returns, the form fields (frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, topics, email) are populated from preferences, and the form becomes editable. 5) If an error occurs while loading, an error message is shown and the form remains disabled until the user retries.

Workflow: Update topics, schedule, and email. Steps: 1) With the form loaded, the user edits one or more fields: checks or unchecks topics checkboxes, changes frequency, adjusts dayOfWeek and timeOfDay if relevant, and/or edits the email address. 2) The form tracks changes in component state and validates inputs on each field change (e.g., email format, required selections). 3) The user clicks the Save button to submit changes. 4) The page runs final validation; if invalid, inline errors are displayed and the updateNewsletterPreferences call is not made. 5) If valid, the page calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the current form values. 6) On success, the page updates the in-memory NewsletterPreference state from the returned preferences object, shows a success message, and may update the URL (e.g., ?status=success). 7) On error, the page shows an error message, keeps the user’s unsaved changes in the form, and allows retry.

Workflow: Disable all newsletters (unsubscribe). Steps: 1) User opens the preferences page and waits for getNewsletterPreferences to load. 2) User sets frequency to the value that represents no delivery (the "none" enum value in NewsletterPreference.frequency). 3) When frequency is set to "none", the topics and schedule fields are logically ignored or visually disabled while remaining bound to state. 4) User clicks Save. 5) Page validates that frequency is set and email is valid; topics may be allowed to be empty because delivery is disabled. 6) Page calls updateNewsletterPreferences with frequency set to "none" and the current values for the other fields. 7) On success, a confirmation message indicates that newsletters are turned off for that email address.

Workflow: Change delivery schedule but keep topics the same. Steps: 1) User opens the page and waits for preferences to load. 2) User adjusts frequency, dayOfWeek, and/or timeOfDay while leaving topics and email unchanged. 3) The form updates its internal state on each change. 4) User clicks Save; validation ensures that required schedule fields are provided based on the selected frequency (for example, dayOfWeek required when frequency is weekly). 5) Page calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the new schedule values and existing topics/email. 6) On success, the new schedule is confirmed and reflected in the form state.

Workflow: Change the newsletter email address. Steps: 1) User opens the page. 2) In the email input, the user replaces the current email with a new address. 3) Optional: user verifies other settings. 4) User clicks Save. 5) Page validates the email format and other required fields and then calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the new email and existing preferences. 6) On success, the page shows a confirmation message and updates the displayed email field to match the saved preferences.

Workflow: Navigate back to home after updating. Steps: 1) After a successful save (as indicated by the updateNewsletterPreferences response), the page displays a success message. 2) User clicks the "Back to Home" control or the site logo. 3) The page reads an optional returnTo URL parameter; if present and valid, it navigates there; otherwise it navigates to index.html. 4) No additional API calls are made solely for navigation.
interactions: On page load, a lifecycle event triggers getNewsletterPreferences; the result populates a shared NewsletterPreference state object used by all form components.

Changing the frequency field updates the NewsletterPreference.frequency value in component state and conditionally enables or disables dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, and topics fields according to business rules (e.g., topics required unless frequency is "none").
Changing the dayOfWeek field updates NewsletterPreference.dayOfWeek in state; the input is only validated when the selected frequency requires a dayOfWeek value.
Changing the timeOfDay field updates NewsletterPreference.timeOfDay in state; the input is validated as an HH:MM string consistent with the backend expectations.

Checking or unchecking a topic checkbox updates the topics array in NewsletterPreference.topics; the value for each checkbox corresponds to one of the allowed topic keys defined in the NewsletterPreference model.
Editing the email field updates NewsletterPreference.email in state and triggers email format validation (e.g., must contain an '@' and a domain).

Clicking the Save button triggers form validation; if valid, it calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the current state values for frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, topics, and email; the UI reflects loading while awaiting the response.

If updateNewsletterPreferences returns success: true, the UI shows a success message, updates the local NewsletterPreference state from the returned preferences object, and optionally updates URL parameters (e.g., status=success and a message parameter) without reloading the page.

If updateNewsletterPreferences returns success: false or the call fails, the UI shows an error message (optionally reflecting the returned message), preserves unsaved form values, and does not change the URL unless explicitly configured.

Clicking the "Back to Home" control or site logo triggers a navigation event that computes the destination from a returnTo URL parameter if available, defaulting to index.html; if there are unsaved changes, the page may optionally request confirmation before navigating away.
page_functionality.state_logic: The newsletter_preferences.html page does not use localStorage; instead, it relies on in-memory component state combined with URL parameters for controlling view state and navigation. On initial load, the page reads the current URL's query string. Supported parameters may include: (1) status: used to indicate the outcome of the most recent save operation (e.g., status=success or status=error). If present, the feedback component displays a corresponding message; after rendering, the page may clear or update this parameter via the History API to avoid repeated messages. (2) message: an optional human-readable message that can be displayed in the feedback banner, often set after redirecting back to this page from another context. The page treats this as display-only and does not hardcode specific message content. (3) returnTo: a relative URL to be used as the target when the user clicks "Back to Home" or the site logo; if absent or invalid, the page navigates to index.html by default. (4) focus: an optional hint indicating which field to focus on after load (e.g., frequency, email, or topics). The loader component reads this parameter and sets initial focus accordingly once getNewsletterPreferences has resolved. The actual newsletter data (frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, topics, email, createdAt, updatedAt) is never encoded in the URL; it is always obtained from getNewsletterPreferences on load and updated exclusively via updateNewsletterPreferences on form submission. All conditional UI logic (such as disabling schedule fields when frequency is "none") operates against the in-memory NewsletterPreference state and is not persisted in URL parameters. URL parameters are read-only inputs to control behavior and navigation, ensuring they do not conflict with dynamic data coming from the APIs.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, calling getNewsletterPreferences, and providing the loaded NewsletterPreference data, loading state, error state, and initial focus instructions to child components.

Displays and edits the newsletter preferences fields (frequency, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, topics, email) using the data from newsletter-preferences-loader; performs client-side validation and submits updated values via updateNewsletterPreferences.

Manages the delivery schedule subset of the form, binding to NewsletterPreference.frequency, NewsletterPreference.dayOfWeek, and NewsletterPreference.timeOfDay. It enforces logic for when dayOfWeek and timeOfDay are required or optional based on the selected frequency and disables or hides irrelevant controls (for example, when frequency indicates no delivery).

Renders a list of checkbox inputs for selecting newsletter topics, mapped directly to the NewsletterPreference.topics array. Each checkbox corresponds to one of the allowed topic keys defined in the NewsletterPreference model, and changes update the array in form state.

Manages the email input field that binds to NewsletterPreference.email. It handles user input, validation of email format, and displays inline error messages if the value is missing or invalid when the user attempts to save.

Acts as the primary action control for saving preferences. When activated, it triggers full-form validation and, if successful, calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the current form state. It also manages disabled/loading states during the API call and dispatches success or error events.

Displays contextual success or error messages related to loading or updating newsletter preferences. It listens to the outcomes of getNewsletterPreferences and updateNewsletterPreferences and can also read status and message URL parameters on load to show a pre-defined message.

Provides navigation elements such as the site logo and a "Back to Home" control. When triggered, it reads an optional returnTo URL parameter to determine the destination; if not present, it navigates to index.html. It may optionally check for unsaved changes before allowing navigation away from the form.

Tracks whether the current form state differs from the last successfully loaded or saved NewsletterPreference. If the user attempts to navigate away (via browser back, closing tab, or clicking navigation-controls) while changes are unsaved, it can prompt for confirmation before allowing the action.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: global hero and site nav remain at the very top (outside this feature). Inside the main centered content column, the newsletter area starts with a subtle textual label (within the form), followed by an optional feedback banner, then the main newsletter preferences form as a single, large editorial-style card. Inside the card, sections stack vertically: navigation controls at the top, then email input, delivery schedule, topics selector, and finally the save button at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: global hero and site nav remain at the very top (outside this feature). Inside the main centered content column, the newsletter area starts with a subtle textual label (within the form), followed by an optional feedback banner, then the main newsletter preferences form as a single, large editorial-style card. Inside the card, sections stack vertically: navigation controls at the top, then email input, delivery schedule, topics selector, and finally the save button at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the main content column centered and constrained to about 60–70% of the viewport width (max 1100–1200px), with generous side gutters. Vertically, the newsletter block begins just below the global nav with ~32–40px of white space. Within the form card, internal padding is ~24px on all sides, with 24–32px between major internal sections. The loader, feedback banner, and guard all live within this same column so the page preserves the hero + nav + centered-content rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a calm, low-density feel: only one primary card (the newsletter form) is visible above the fold in the main column, with generous vertical spacing between its sections. Controls are not packed side-by-side where it would feel cramped; labels sit above fields, and checkbox options have ample padding and breathing room. The layout avoids tight multi-column form grids in favor of simple vertical stacking, echoing the editorial, magazine-like style.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows down from the existing hero and global nav into the newsletter area. First, it meets the subtle section context (title/label within the form), then any feedback banner. From there, attention moves to the top of the card where navigation controls live, then follows a vertical path through email, schedule, and topics. The large, bottom-aligned save button becomes the final focal anchor, reinforcing a clear progression from understanding (copy and structure) to configuration (form controls) to confirmation (save and feedback).

layout.overall_description: Below the large photographic hero and the full-width navigation bar, the page transitions into a centered, editorial-style content column framed by generous white gutters on both sides. After a comfortable vertical gap, a single, prominent card holds all newsletter preferences: it spans most of the column width with soft internal padding and no heavy borders, feeling like a featured article rather than a dense settings panel. A short textual header at the top of this card anchors the context, followed by any status feedback that appears as a slim, colored band aligned to the card’s width. Within the card, form sections are stacked with ample white space: a small back or navigation control at the very top, then the email address, the delivery schedule choices, and a spacious grid of topic checkboxes. The primary save button sits at the bottom left of the card, with extra space below it so the card feels airy rather than crowded. Overall, the layout feels like a calm magazine feature about your newsletter, rather than a busy dashboard, mirroring the warm, family-oriented editorial style of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a light overlay confined to the main newsletter preferences card area within the centered content column. It dims just the form card (not the global hero or nav) with a semi-opaque white layer, and a small circular spinner and short loading label are vertically and horizontally centered over the card. The overlay matches the card’s width and height and respects its padding, so it feels like the card is temporarily paused rather than the whole page being blocked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned over the newsletter-preferences-form region, stretching to its edges; it scrolls with the page but blocks interaction with underlying controls while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main form is a single, large, editorial-style card centered within the page’s content column. It spans roughly 80–90% of the column width (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop), with 24px–32px of internal padding on all sides. The top edge of the card sits ~32–40px below the global nav bar, with clear white space above and below it to separate it from other sections. Inside, content is arranged in a clear vertical stack: a subtle page title and short description at the top (taking up the role of the ‘2026 Family Treasure’ label), followed by navigation controls, feedback banner (when present), and sub-sections for email, schedule, topics, and the save button, each separated by 24px–32px of vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the central column; its width is constrained by the page’s max content width and it scales down responsively on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main form card, the delivery schedule section appears as a clearly defined block positioned below the email-input-section and above the topics-selector, maintaining ~24px–32px of vertical spacing from each. It aligns left with the rest of the form content and spans the full inner width of the card, but the interactive controls (radio buttons, dropdowns, etc.) are grouped into a single vertical column so the layout remains light. A section heading sits at the top of this block, with controls stacked underneath and 12px–16px between each option, creating a comfortable reading rhythm rather than a dense control grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width subsection within the form; it does not scroll independently and adjusts its height to the number of schedule options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topics selector occupies a generous block toward the lower half of the form card, directly below the delivery-schedule-section and above the save-preferences-button, with ~24px–32px of vertical spacing separating them. It spans the full inner width of the card and features a short heading and helper text at the top, left-aligned. Individual topic checkboxes are arranged in a loose, multi-row flow: each option appears as a line or pill with ~12px–16px vertical spacing and consistent left alignment so the eye can scan down the list easily. On wider screens, options can wrap into two natural columns if space allows, but there should still be enough horizontal padding to feel airy rather than grid-like.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that expands vertically based on the number of topics; options wrap naturally onto new lines but remain within the form card’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email input section is positioned near the top of the newsletter-preferences-form, directly beneath the form’s title/intro text and above the delivery-schedule-section, separated by ~24px of vertical space on each side. The section is left-aligned and stretches across most of the card’s inner width, but the input field itself typically occupies about 60–70% of that width to maintain a balanced, editorial feel. The label appears directly above the input with 8px–12px spacing, and any helper or error text sits immediately below the field, keeping the entire email block compact yet breathable within the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width subsection that grows slightly in height when validation or helper text is shown; it remains within the form card and responds fluidly to smaller screen widths by allowing the input to fill more of the available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save button is placed at the very bottom of the newsletter-preferences-form card, following the topics-selector with about 24px–32px of vertical spacing. It is left-aligned with the form fields above, occupying a compact width (e.g., 200–260px) rather than stretching full-width, which preserves the minimal, editorial style. A comfortable amount of white space (at least 24px) is left below the button before the end of the card, so the button feels anchored but not cramped against the card’s edge, making it the final visual anchor in the user’s downward scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline action at the bottom of the form; it scrolls with the content and does not stick to the viewport, but may visually emphasize its state (disabled, loading) without changing its position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback banner appears as a slim, full-width strip inside the newsletter-preferences-form card, positioned between the navigation-controls and the first input section (typically just above the email-input-section), with about 16px–24px of white space separating it from both. It spans the entire inner width of the card and uses a subtle background tint to stand out while still feeling integrated with the clean layout. Text is left-aligned and kept to a single or double line, maintaining the low-density, editorial tone. When not present, the surrounding sections simply move closer together, preserving the linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears or disappears within the flow; it does not overlay content but pushes surrounding sections down or up when shown or hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Navigation controls (such as a back link or breadcrumb) sit at the top of the newsletter-preferences-form card, directly under the card’s title/intro text and above the feedback-banner or first form field, with about 16px of vertical spacing separating them. They are left-aligned and relatively compact, typically a single line of text or a small icon plus label, so they read as secondary aids rather than dominating the card. Their width is minimal relative to the card, occupying only the space needed for the text, preserving ample negative space to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element; it scrolls away with the form content and does not stick to the viewport. It may act as a click target for navigation but has no independent layout behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The unsaved changes guard has no dedicated visual block in the layout; instead, it passively watches the newsletter-preferences-form. When a user tries to navigate away using the navigation-controls or browser navigation while there are unsaved edits, it triggers a modal or browser-level confirmation that appears centered over the entire viewport, not just over the card. This keeps the main visual structure intact while still clearly alerting the user in a way that momentarily supersedes the editorial calm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual until activated; when triggered, it invokes a centered, full-viewport confirmation layer that overlays all page content, then disappears without affecting the underlying layout once dismissed.

name: Saved Lists & Collections
title: Saved Lists & Collections
description: Manage all saved recipe collections, activity lists, product lists, and reading lists in one place. View overviews, open list details, create new lists, rename or delete existing lists, remove individual items, and navigate back to item detail pages.

core_features: Load a unified overview of all saved list types with item counts using getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview.
Filter and browse lists by type (recipes, activities, products, reading) using getRecipeCollectionsOverview, getActivityListsOverview, getProductListsOverview, and getReadingListsOverview.
Open a specific list in detail view to see all saved items using getRecipeCollectionDetail, getActivityListDetail, getProductListDetail, or getReadingListDetail based on the selected listType.
Create new empty lists or collections for any supported type using createSavedListOrCollection.
Rename existing lists or collections using renameSavedListOrCollection.
Delete lists or collections (and all their items) using deleteSavedListOrCollection.
Remove individual items from a list using removeItemFromSavedListOrCollection and update item counts accordingly.
Navigate from items inside a list to their respective detail pages (recipe_detail.html, activity_detail.html, product_detail.html, article_detail.html) using the appropriate item IDs.
user_workflows:
Viewing all saved lists across types:
1) User opens saved_lists.html without URL parameters.
2) Page controller calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview to retrieve counts and basic info for all list types.
3) Summary strip shows total counts per type; overview panel shows grouped lists per type with their item counts.
4) User can scroll or scan the grouped lists and choose a list to open in detail view.

Focusing on a specific list from another page (e.g., recipe, activity, product, or article detail):
1) User navigates from a detail page via a link or button that points to saved_lists.html with query parameters listType and listId.
2) On load, the page controller reads listType and listId from the URL.
3) Page controller calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview for the summary, then calls the type-specific overview interface for that listType.
4) Page controller calls the corresponding detail interface (e.g., getRecipeCollectionDetail when listType is 'recipe_collection') with listId.
5) The matching list is highlighted/selected and its items are shown in the detail panel, ready for management.

Creating a new list:
1) User clicks a 'Create New List' action (global or type-specific) on saved_lists.html.
2) A new-list form opens, where the user selects listType and enters name and optional description.
3) On submit, the form component calls createSavedListOrCollection with listType, name, and description.
4) On success, the page controller updates the URL with the returned listType and listId.
5) The controller refreshes the relevant overview using the appropriate type-specific overview interface, and loads the new list’s detail using the corresponding get*Detail interface, showing an empty item list ready for future additions from other pages.

Renaming and deleting a list:
1) From the lists overview or the detail panel header, user clicks 'Rename' for a selected list.
2) Inline rename form opens with the current name pre-filled; on submit it calls renameSavedListOrCollection with listType, listId, and newName.
3) On success, the page updates the list name in the overview and detail panel (either via refetch of the relevant overview or local state update).
4) For deletion, user clicks a 'Delete List' action.
5) A confirmation dialog appears; on confirm it calls deleteSavedListOrCollection with listType and listId.
6) On success, the list is removed from the relevant overview, the detail panel is cleared, and the URL is updated to remove listId (and optionally listType) or to point to another existing list of the same type if desired.

Managing items within a list and navigating to item detail pages:
1) In the selected list’s detail panel, the page shows all items fetched via the appropriate get*Detail interface.
2) User clicks on an item’s title or main area.
3) The page navigates to the item’s detail page using a URL like recipe_detail.html?id={recipeId}, activity_detail.html?id={activityId}, product_detail.html?id={productId}, or article_detail.html?id={articleId}, depending on item type.
4) To remove an item from the list, user clicks a 'Remove' control for that item.
5) The item row component calls removeItemFromSavedListOrCollection with listType, listId, and the savedItemId corresponding to the membership entity (e.g., RecipeCollectionItem.id, ActivityListItem.id, ProductListItem.id, or ReadingListItem.id).
6) On success, the row is removed from the current list view, and the remainingItemCount from the response is used to update or validate the item count shown in the summaries and overviews.
interactions: Page load without listType/listId: onInit, the page controller reads query parameters, detects that no specific list is requested, calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview, and renders overviews for all list types without opening a detail panel.

Page load with listType and listId: onInit, the page controller reads query parameters, validates listType, and calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview for global counts, then calls the relevant type-specific overview interface and the corresponding get*Detail interface for listId to open that list in detail view.

Clicking a listType filter (such as recipes, activities, products, or reading): the list-type-filter component updates the listType query parameter in the URL, triggers the page controller to call the corresponding type-specific overview interface, and focuses that type’s section in the overview. The previously selected listId, if any, can be preserved if still valid for the selected type or cleared otherwise.

Clicking on a specific list in an overview: the lists-overview-panel updates the URL with listType and the clicked list’s listId, then asks the detail panel to call the appropriate get*Detail interface to load that list’s items.

Submitting the new list form: onSubmit, the new-list-form component validates inputs and calls createSavedListOrCollection. On success, the page controller updates URL parameters to the newly created list, refreshes the relevant overview using the appropriate type-specific overview interface, and loads the new list’s detail via the matching get*Detail interface.

Renaming a list: onSubmit of the rename-list-form, the component calls renameSavedListOrCollection. On success, the page updates the list name in both the overview and detail panel by refetching the relevant overview or by updating local state representing the current list and its entry in the overview.

Deleting a list: onConfirm in the delete-list-confirmation-dialog, the component calls deleteSavedListOrCollection. On success, the current list is removed from the overview, the detail panel is cleared, and the URL is updated to remove listId (and optionally listType). If other lists exist in the same type, the page may automatically select the next available list and update listId accordingly.

Removing an item from a list: onClick of an item's 'Remove' control, the list-item-manager-row calls removeItemFromSavedListOrCollection. On success, the item is removed from the visible list, and remainingItemCount is used to adjust the item count shown for that list in the overview and summary strip.

Clicking an item within a list: onClick of the item’s main area or title, the list-item-manager-row triggers navigation to the appropriate detail page using the item’s entity ID (recipeId, activityId, productId, or articleId) and the target URL defined in outgoing navigation.

Changing URL parameters via browser navigation (back/forward buttons): onPopState or equivalent, the page controller re-reads listType and listId from the URL and reloads the appropriate overviews and detail view using the same interfaces as on initial load.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by URL query parameters and transient in-memory state. Two query parameters are used: listType and listId. listType determines which category of lists is currently focused and must be one of 'recipe_collection', 'activity_list', 'product_list', or 'reading_list'. listId identifies a specific list within that type.

On initial load, the page controller reads listType and listId from the URL. It always calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview to populate the summary strip and allow cross-type navigation. If listType is absent, the page remains in a general overview mode, showing all types' lists via either getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview alone or combined with lazy calls to the type-specific overview interfaces when sections are expanded. No detail panel is opened until the user selects a list.

If a valid listType is present, the controller calls the matching overview interface (getRecipeCollectionsOverview, getActivityListsOverview, getProductListsOverview, or getReadingListsOverview) to load all lists of that type. If listId is also present, the controller calls the corresponding detail interface (getRecipeCollectionDetail, getActivityListDetail, getProductListDetail, or getReadingListDetail) using listId to populate the detail panel. If listId is missing but lists exist for the current type, the controller may automatically select the first list returned for that type, update listId in the URL, and load its details. If listId does not correspond to any existing list, the controller clears the detail panel and may remove listId from the URL or show a 'list not found' state without hardcoded content.

Any user action that changes the focused type or selected list (such as clicking a type filter, selecting a list, creating a new list, or deleting a list) updates the URL's listType and/or listId. The URL is treated as the single source of truth for the currently selected list. Browser navigation (back/forward) is handled by re-reading these parameters and re-calling the appropriate interfaces. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; all dynamic data is refreshed from the assigned interfaces based solely on the current URL and user interactions.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and high-level state for the Saved Lists & Collections page. On initialization and whenever URL parameters change, it reads listType and listId, calls getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview, and then invokes the appropriate type-specific overview and detail interfaces. It propagates current listType, listId, and loaded data to child components and updates the URL when children signal type or list selection changes.

Displays a high-level summary of all list types with their total counts using data from getSavedListsAndCollectionsOverview. Allows users to click a summary segment to filter the page to a specific listType, informing the page controller to update URL parameters and load the corresponding overview.

Provides controls to switch the focused listType (recipes, activities, products, reading). It reads the current listType from the page controller and lets the user change it. When the selection changes, it notifies the page controller, which updates the URL and reloads the relevant overview and detail data.

Shows lists for the currently focused listType, including list name, description, itemCount, and metadata (such as createdAt when needed). The page controller supplies the relevant overview data returned by getRecipeCollectionsOverview, getActivityListsOverview, getProductListsOverview, or getReadingListsOverview depending on listType. Users can select a list to open its detail, trigger rename and delete actions, or open the new-list-form scoped to the current listType.

Displays the full contents and metadata of the currently selected list, including all saved items. Based on listType and listId, it calls the appropriate detail interface: getRecipeCollectionDetail, getActivityListDetail, getProductListDetail, or getReadingListDetail. The panel renders item rows using list-item-manager-row, provides list-level actions like rename and delete by delegating to rename-list-form and delete-list-confirmation-dialog, and exposes navigation on item clicks to the appropriate detail pages.

Handles creation of new saved lists or collections. Can be opened from a global create control or from within a particular listType context. Collects listType, name, and optional description from the user and submits them via createSavedListOrCollection. On success, it communicates the new listType and listId back to the page controller so that the URL is updated and the new list is loaded into the detail panel.

Provides an inline or modal form to rename the currently selected list. It receives the current listType and listId from the page controller and pre-fills the existing name. On submit, it calls renameSavedListOrCollection with listType, listId, and newName. On success, it notifies the page controller to refresh or update the relevant overview data and the detail header so that the new name is reflected everywhere.

Confirms deletion of a list. Receives listType and listId for the list flagged for deletion. On user confirmation, it calls deleteSavedListOrCollection. On success, it signals the page controller to remove the deleted list from overviews, clear the detail panel, and update URL parameters to reflect the absence of the deleted list (and optionally to navigate to another list if available).

Represents a single item within a list in the detail panel. It receives the membership entity (RecipeCollectionItem, ActivityListItem, ProductListItem, or ReadingListItem) and the linked content entity (Recipe, Activity, Product, or Article). When the main row is clicked, it navigates to the corresponding detail page using the appropriate outgoing navigation URL and item ID. When the 'Remove' control is clicked, it calls removeItemFromSavedListOrCollection with the current listType, listId, and savedItemId equal to the membership entity's id. On success, it removes itself from the UI and notifies the detail panel/page controller so that item counts can be updated using remainingItemCount.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure with clearly separated blocks. A full‑width hero at the very top is owned by the saved-lists-page-controller, featuring a photographic background with an overlaid title and short description about Saved Lists & Collections. Directly below, a full‑width, white list-type-filter bar mimics the navigation bar from the mockup, presenting list categories in a horizontally scrollable row (or two rows on wrap). Below this, within the centered content column, the saved-lists-summary-strip appears as a small, left‑aligned, one‑line label/metric band (analogous to '2026 Family Treasure'). The main content region underneath is dominated by the lists-overview-panel as an editorial-style feature area; when a list is opened, the saved-list-detail-panel replaces or overlays this area in the same central column, like transitioning into a hero-style article card. Supporting forms (new-list-form, rename-list-form) and row‑level controls (list-item-manager-row) are nested inside these primary sections, preserving the single-column editorial flow without introducing visible sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure with clearly separated blocks.
