A full‑width hero at the very top is owned by the saved-lists-page-controller, featuring a photographic background with an overlaid title and short description about Saved Lists & Collections. Directly below, a full‑width, white list-type-filter bar mimics the navigation bar from the mockup, presenting list categories in a horizontally scrollable row (or two rows on wrap). Below this, within the centered content column, the saved-lists-summary-strip appears as a small, left‑aligned, one‑line label/metric band (analogous to '2026 Family Treasure'). The main content region underneath is dominated by the lists-overview-panel as an editorial-style feature area; when a list is opened, the saved-list-detail-panel replaces or overlays this area in the same central column, like transitioning into a hero-style article card. Supporting forms (new-list-form, rename-list-form) and row‑level controls (list-item-manager-row) are nested inside these primary sections, preserving the single-column editorial flow without introducing visible sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, roughly the top 30–35% of the viewport is used by the hero image and its overlaid heading/subtitle. Immediately below, the list-type-filter bar occupies around 8–10% of the initial viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding (about 16–24px). The remaining vertical space is used for the main content column, where the lists-overview-panel and, when active, the saved-list-detail-panel consume the full width of the central content column. Horizontally, all main content (summary strip, overview, detail, forms) sits inside a centered column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width (approx. 1100–1200px max), with 15–20% left/right whitespace gutters. Internal paddings generally follow 16–24px values, and transitions between major blocks use vertical margins in the 24–40px range, echoing the mockup’s comfortable rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a calm, low-density editorial feel. Each major block (hero, filter bar, summary strip, main content) is separated by generous white space so that at any given scroll position there’s typically one clear focal element: first the hero, then the filter bar, then the metric/tagline strip, then either the lists overview or the list detail. Within the lists-overview-panel, individual list cards are well spaced with ample padding and breathing room, avoiding tight grids or dense tables. In the saved-list-detail-panel, list items (list-item-manager-row) are vertically stacked with sufficient spacing so controls don’t feel cramped. Forms use clear spacing between fields and actions, preferring fewer, more prominent controls over clustered UI.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s top‑down and subtle Z‑pattern: the eye is first drawn to the central hero headline about Saved Lists & Collections, then to the supporting subtitle over the photo. From there, attention moves downward to the list-type-filter bar, scanning left‑to‑right across category tabs/chips, with an accent style on the active filter. The gaze then drops to the saved-lists-summary-strip, reading a concise overview (e.g., total lists and items) aligned left. Finally, it continues into the main content focus — initially the lists-overview-panel, which acts like a feature editorial block. Once a list is selected, the visual anchor becomes the saved-list-detail-panel header, then scans down the item rows. Dialogs (like delete-list-confirmation-dialog) temporarily interrupt this flow as centered overlays with dimmed background, clearly claiming visual priority while still feeling consistent with the minimal, flat aesthetic.

layout.overall_description: The Saved Lists & Collections page feels like an editorial feature in a modern family lifestyle magazine. A full-width hero image spans edge-to-edge at the top, with a warm, soft-toned family or home-life photo. Centered within this hero, a bold masthead-style title announces 'Saved Lists & Collections', with a short, friendly subtitle below explaining that this is the place to manage favorite recipes, activities, and products. Below the hero, stretching across the screen, a clean white navigation bar (the list-type-filter) sits on a flat background, its category chips or labels aligned in a single row that can scroll horizontally if needed, or wrap gracefully into two lines. Beneath that, the layout tightens into a centered content column, leaving broad left and right gutters. At the top of this column, a subtle saved-lists-summary-strip shows a small, left-aligned line summarizing the user’s saved content. Below it, the central column opens into a large, full-width content area: initially the lists-overview-panel, which presents lists as spacious editorial cards stacked vertically with comfortable gaps. When a list is opened, the lists overview area gently gives way to the saved-list-detail-panel in the same column, making it feel like you’ve stepped into a feature article for that list. Inline forms for creating or renaming lists appear as simple, full-width bands within this column, and item rows are evenly spaced lines with right-aligned controls, all respecting the base 8px grid. Any destructive action triggers a centered, minimal confirmation dialog that floats above a dimmed backdrop, keeping the underlying editorial layout visible but out of focus. Overall, the page feels airy, ordered, and calm, guiding users down the page in a sequence of clear, visually distinct sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component owns the overall page frame and positions all other elements according to the editorial layout pattern. At the very top of the viewport, it renders a full-width hero band that spans 100% of the browser width and approximately 30–35% of the initial viewport height. This hero uses a large photographic background image that reaches edge-to-edge horizontally, with no visible borders or shadows. Centered within the hero’s safe area (aligned to the central content column but visually centered on the page), there is a large, bold title ('Saved Lists & Collections') and a 1–2 line subtitle stacked vertically, both aligned center. The text sits on a semi-transparent overlay or a subtle gradient to ensure legibility while keeping the photo dominant. Below the hero, the page-controller aligns the list-type-filter as a full-width horizontal bar against a white background, with top and bottom padding of about 16–24px and subtle separation from the hero via whitespace (not a hard line). Further down, it establishes the centered content column (approx. max-width 1100–1200px) in which the saved-lists-summary-strip appears first, followed by either the lists-overview-panel or the saved-list-detail-panel, depending on user state. The page-controller ensures consistent left and right gutters (about 15–20% of viewport on large screens) and vertical spacing between major sections (24–40px), maintaining the low-density editorial styling across all child components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-level container. The hero section scrolls away normally. The page-controller also defines the scroll context for potentially sticky elements (like the list-type-filter) and ensures that all content remains centered within a constrained max-width column on wide screens, while gracefully scaling down to full width on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary strip is positioned within the main centered content column, directly below the list-type-filter bar with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It is left-aligned and spans the full width of the content column, but visually reads as a simple, single-line label. The strip occupies roughly the height of one line of text plus comfortable padding: around 8–12px padding above and below, and 16–24px padding left and right. It aligns its text flush-left with the column, in a smaller, subtler type style compared to headings (e.g., all caps label or light-weight sentence case) – something like '2026 Family Treasure' but adapted to this context (e.g., 'Your Saved Lists • 8 Collections • 127 Items'). There is generous whitespace below the strip (about 24–32px) before the lists-overview-panel or saved-list-detail-panel begins, so the strip functions as a quiet, transitional element between navigation and main content rather than a dense info bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that scrolls with the page. It always appears before the main content area and does not stick or overlay other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list-type-filter sits immediately below the full-width hero, occupying a horizontal band that spans 100% of the page width. It is contained within a white background strip, visually similar to a navigation bar, with no drop shadows and minimal chrome. Inside this band, the filter options are centered with respect to the main content column: the inner content is constrained to the same 1100–1200px width and sits centrally with equal left and right margins on large screens. The filter controls (e.g., chips, pills, or text links) are arranged horizontally in a single row whenever possible, each with generous horizontal padding (12–16px per side) and vertical padding (8–12px), leaving noticeable gaps (8–16px) between options. On narrower viewports or when there are many filters, the row can horizontally scroll or wrap into a second row, in which case both rows remain visually tight but not cramped. The active filter stands out with an accent color and bolder weight, echoing the slight emphasis seen on specific nav items in the mockup. The band has top and bottom padding of about 16–24px and a comfortable amount of whitespace (around 24px) before the summary strip below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter bar is full-width and may become sticky just beneath the hero area: once the user scrolls past the hero, the list-type-filter can remain fixed at the top of the viewport, ensuring list-type navigation is always accessible while the main content scrolls underneath. On small screens, it shrinks in height but maintains readable tap targets and horizontal scrolling for overflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lists-overview-panel occupies the main content area within the centered column when no specific list is actively opened. Positioned directly below the saved-lists-summary-strip, it begins about 24–32px beneath the summary strip and spans the full width of the content column. Visually, it reads like an editorial section of feature cards. At the top of the panel, aligned to the left, there can be a section heading such as 'All Saved Collections' with a comfortable margin below (16–24px). The body of the panel consists of one or two-column arrangements of list 'cards', depending on screen width: on large desktops, you might see a vertical stack of large, full-width list cards (each taking nearly the full content column width), or a loose two-column layout with each card occupying roughly 48% of the column width with 4% gutter, always maintaining generous inner padding (24px) and vertical spacing between cards (24–32px). Each card resembles a mini editorial feature: a clear list title at the top, optional small metadata row below, and a brief description or preview content, all contained in a flat, white block with soft rounded corners (if used) but no heavy shadowing. The overall height of the panel grows as more lists are present, but at any viewport height, only 1–3 cards are fully visible at once, preserving a low-density feel. Below the last card, there is ample bottom padding (32–48px) before the page ends or next section begins. When a list is selected, this entire panel either fades out or slides away and is replaced in the same central space by the saved-list-detail-panel, giving the feeling of entering a focused article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This panel scrolls naturally with the page and occupies the main content focus when visible. It does not float or overlay other elements; rather, it yields its entire vertical space to the saved-list-detail-panel when a list is opened, typically via routing or a state transition. On very small screens, cards collapse to a single-column stack that still follows the same spacing and padding logic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-list-detail-panel replaces the lists-overview-panel in the main content column when a specific list is opened. It begins at the same vertical position—about 24–32px below the saved-lists-summary-strip—and spans the full width of the centered content column. The top of the panel features a prominent list header area: the list name appears as a large, bold heading aligned left, with optional subtext (e.g., list description or item count) below in a smaller, softer style. A back or 'All Lists' link sits near the top left, either above or to the left of the heading, styled minimally so as not to overpower the title. The header has generous vertical padding (24–32px) and clear spacing below before the list items begin. Under the header, the list’s contents are stacked vertically as list-item-manager-row components, each spanning the full width of the panel (minus consistent internal padding). Rows are separated by subtle dividers or 8–16px vertical gaps, ensuring the list feels airy rather than like a cramped table. At the bottom or top of this panel, contextual actions such as 'Add item', 'Share', or 'Delete list' can appear, aligned right or left but always respecting a simple, flat styling. The panel can extend well beyond the viewport height, allowing users to scroll down through items without breaking the central column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This panel occupies the same spatial slot as the lists-overview-panel and is mutually exclusive with it in the main column. It scrolls with the page and does not float. On transition from the overview, it may animate in from the right or fade in, reinforcing the sense of stepping into a new 'article' while preserving the single-column, editorial structure. On narrow screens, it remains full width, with rows stacking naturally and any auxiliary controls wrapping or moving below headings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The new-list-form appears within the main content column, closely associated with the lists-overview-panel. In most states, it is revealed inline at the top of the lists-overview-panel, just beneath the saved-lists-summary-strip and before the list cards, with a vertical separation of about 24px from the summary strip above and 24px from the first card below. It spans the full width of the content column and presents a simple, flat input area—typically a single text field for the list name, possibly with a smaller optional description field. Fields are stacked vertically with 8–16px gaps between them, and the entire form has internal padding around 16–24px, giving it the feel of a soft card without heavy borders or shadows. Form actions (e.g., 'Create list', 'Cancel') are aligned to the right or left at the bottom of the form, with at least 16px spacing above and between buttons. When the form is collapsed or not in use, it may be represented by a single, left-aligned 'Create new list' button or link at the top of the overview, maintaining the spacious editorial rhythm by not crowding the header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is an inline block within the lists-overview-panel and scrolls with it. It does not overlay other content and does not stick to the viewport. It can expand/collapse smoothly in place, pushing the list cards down when opened and reclaiming that vertical space when closed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rename-list-form is an inline, contextual form that appears within the saved-list-detail-panel, usually replacing the static list title area temporarily. When activated, it occupies the same horizontal space as the list title: spanning from the left edge of the content column to the typical title width, leaving default right padding consistent with other header content. The form consists primarily of a single text input field pre-filled with the current list name, aligned left, with a width that covers roughly 60–70% of the content column for easy readability. Below or to the right of the field, small 'Save' and 'Cancel' controls appear, separated by at least 8–12px of horizontal space and aligned consistently with other button placements. Vertical padding above and below the form respects the original header spacing (16–24px), so the header region grows slightly but does not feel cramped. Overall, it feels like a smooth, in-place edit within the editorial header rather than a separate module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and transient within the saved-list-detail-panel. It does not create a new section, and it scrolls with the page as part of the header. When not active, it collapses entirely, handing space back to the regular heading. It does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete-list-confirmation-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay that temporarily sits above all other page content. When triggered, the rest of the page (hero, filters, main content) is dimmed with a semi-transparent overlay that covers the full viewport width and height, while this dialog is visually anchored in the center of the screen. The dialog itself is a medium-sized, flat rectangular panel with a white background, rounded corners if used elsewhere in the system, and generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides). It occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on desktop, with a maximum width constrained to stay within the central column visual area, and does not exceed about 40–50% of viewport height. The content is stacked vertically: a concise, bold title at the top ('Delete this list?'), a brief explanatory message beneath with 8–16px spacing, and a button row at the bottom with prominent 'Delete' (accent or danger styled) and secondary 'Cancel' buttons spaced 16–24px apart, typically right-aligned. There is ample whitespace around text and buttons, in line with the page’s low-density design. No intrusive drop shadows are used; if any, they are subtle and soft.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This dialog is fixed to the center of the viewport and remains in place even as the underlying page content is scroll-locked. It overlays all other elements until dismissed, after which control returns to the previous scroll position in the main layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each list-item-manager-row resides inside the saved-list-detail-panel and spans the full usable width of that panel. Rows are arranged in a single vertical column, one per saved item, with consistent vertical spacing: either a very light divider line or a gap of about 8–16px between rows. Height is determined by content but typically allows for a single-line or two-line item title on the left and controls on the right without feeling cramped; the row’s internal vertical padding is around 12–16px, and horizontal padding is 16–24px, aligning with the rest of the panel’s content. The left side of the row is reserved for the primary item information: title in a readable size, with optional subtitle or metadata beneath in a smaller font. The right side of the row is dedicated to actions such as 'View item', 'Remove from list', or drag/reorder handles; these controls are aligned right and vertically centered within the row. On wider screens, this creates a clean horizontal tension between left-aligned text and right-aligned controls, mirroring an editorial list with management affordances. On narrower screens, actions can wrap to a second line below the item title while the row still maintains comfortable padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rows are static elements that scroll naturally within the saved-list-detail-panel. They do not stick or float, but they visually repeat with consistent spacing to create a calm, readable list. If reordering is supported, a drag handle can appear at the far left, but this does not change their fundamental full-width, single-column behavior.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamically managed information about the blog’s mission, values, and content approach using static page content from the backend.

core_features: Retrieve About page content from the backend using getStaticPageContent with a fixed pageKey that corresponds to the About page.
Render the returned HTML body (bodyHtml) so users can read about the site’s purpose, values, and editorial approach.
Display the last updated timestamp for the About content using the lastUpdated field returned by getStaticPageContent.
Expose the title returned by getStaticPageContent as the page-level heading and for use in document metadata (e.g., browser tab title).
Enable navigation to the Contact page via a 'Contact us' link located inside the About page content, navigating to contact.html when clicked.
Handle error and loading states around fetching static content, so users see appropriate feedback if the content is not yet loaded or fails to load.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User navigates to about.html (typically by clicking an 'About' link in the footer).
2. On initial load, the page determines the pageKey for this static page (for example, derived from the route as the constant key for About).
3. The page calls getStaticPageContent(pageKey) to retrieve the About content object.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown by the page-level loader component.
5. When data is returned, the page sets the document title to the returned title, renders the title in the main heading, and injects bodyHtml into the content area.
6. The lastUpdated value is formatted and displayed in a metadata area (e.g., 'Last updated' text) without hardcoded content details.
7. User scrolls and reads the content; any inline links in bodyHtml become active navigation elements.

Navigate to Contact from About:
1. While reading the About content, the user locates a 'Contact us' link rendered within bodyHtml.
2. The user clicks this link.
3. The browser navigates to contact.html according to the href in the link, fulfilling the outgoing navigation requirement.
4. No additional URL parameters are required or appended by this page for the Contact navigation.

Handle content load errors:
1. User navigates to about.html.
2. The page attempts to call getStaticPageContent with the About pageKey.
3. If the call fails or returns an error, the loader hides and an error message component is displayed instead of the main content.
4. The user can trigger a retry action (e.g., by clicking a button) that re-invokes getStaticPageContent with the same pageKey.
interactions: Page load initialization: On first render of about.html, the page automatically triggers a call to getStaticPageContent with the About pageKey.
Content load success: When getStaticPageContent resolves successfully, the page updates internal state with title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated, then renders them in their respective components.
Content load failure: If getStaticPageContent rejects or returns an error, the page displays an error state and offers an interaction (e.g., a 'Try again' click) that retries the call.
Scroll interaction: As users scroll through the bodyHtml content, no additional data fetches are triggered; all content for this page is served by the single getStaticPageContent call.

Inline link click: When a user clicks any anchor link inside the rendered bodyHtml, the browser navigates to the href target. For the specific 'Contact us' link, this results in navigation to contact.html.

Retry loading: If an error is shown, clicking the retry control triggers another getStaticPageContent call with the same pageKey, replacing the error state with a loading state until the result returns.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page does not require any URL parameters to function and is defined as a static page at about.html. On load, the page determines a constant pageKey associated with the About page (for example, based on the filename) and uses that in getStaticPageContent(pageKey). Any URL query parameters present in about.html (e.g., about.html?anyParam=value) do not affect which content is retrieved; they are ignored when computing pageKey. Page state (loading, success, error) is managed in memory for the duration of the page view and is not stored in URL parameters. Navigation to other pages, such as contact.html via the 'Contact us' link in bodyHtml, is handled through standard anchor navigation without encoding additional state into the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates fetching of the About page content via getStaticPageContent when about.html loads, manages loading and error states, and passes the resulting data down to child components for rendering.

Receives the title and lastUpdated fields from the root loader component and exposes them as the main page heading and a metadata line showing when the About content was last updated. Also synchronizes the document title with the fetched title.

Takes the bodyHtml string from getStaticPageContent and injects it into the DOM as HTML, enabling users to read the full About information and click any links contained within it, including the 'Contact us' link that navigates to contact.html.

Listens to the loading state managed by the root loader component and displays a loading indicator while getStaticPageContent is in progress. It hides itself when content is successfully loaded or an error occurs.

Displays an error message if getStaticPageContent fails for the About page. Provides a user interaction (e.g., a button) that allows the user to retry fetching the content by delegating back to the root loader component.

Monitors click events within the rendered bodyHtml from the About page and allows normal navigation for links. Specifically, it ensures that when the 'Contact us' link is clicked, the browser navigates to contact.html according to the href defined in the HTML.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page content sits within the same global shell suggested by the mockup: a full-width hero and full-width navigation bar exist above (handled elsewhere in the app). Below that, this page uses a single, centered content column. Within this column, components flow top-to-bottom in a linear stack: the about-page-root defines the centered column and vertical rhythm, the about-content-header appears first as an editorial-style intro block, followed by the about-body-renderer as the main article content. The about-contact-navigation appears at the very bottom as a closing, distinct yet lightweight section. The about-loading-indicator and about-error-display temporarily replace or overlay the central content area while keeping the overall column placement and margins consistent.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page content sits within the same global shell suggested by the mockup: a full-width hero and full-width navigation bar exist above (handled elsewhere in the app). Below that, this page uses a single, centered content column. Within this column, components flow top-to-bottom in a linear stack: the about-page-root defines the centered column and vertical rhythm, the about-content-header appears first as an editorial-style intro block, followed by the about-body-renderer as the main article content. The about-contact-navigation appears at the very bottom as a closing, distinct yet lightweight section. The about-loading-indicator and about-error-display temporarily replace or overlay the central content area while keeping the overall column placement and margins consistent.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the About content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered, with 15–20% white space gutters on each side to mirror the editorial layout. Within that column, the about-content-header takes up the top 15–20% of the scrollable content height initially visible (not counting the global hero and navigation), with the about-body-renderer occupying the bulk of the remaining vertical space as long-form text. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header → body, body → contact navigation) is generous, in the 32–40px range, aligned to the 8px grid. The loading and error states use the same width but compress content into a visually contained block centered within the upper half of the viewport so they’re quickly noticed without feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density follows a calm, editorial pattern: single-column text with comfortable line lengths, large headings, and plenty of white space between sections. No dense grids or multiple cards appear in this view; instead, each component feels like a clearly separated block with breathing room around it. The header is visually bold but surrounded by whitespace, the body text is broken into readable chunks with 24–32px spacing between paragraphs or sub-sections, and the contact navigation is compact, with a subtle presence at the end. Loading and error states appear as single, focused messages rather than busy layouts, maintaining the low-density aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows down from the global hero and navigation to the top of the About column. First, users encounter the about-content-header, which anchors the page purpose with a small label and strong title, similar in role to the '2026 Family Treasure' treatment leading into a feature. From there, the eye naturally continues down into the about-body-renderer, which becomes the main reading experience. As the user scrolls, the narrative pulls them to the bottom where the about-contact-navigation provides a clear, yet understated, next step. When loading or when an error occurs, the visual focus is immediately captured by the centered indicator or message in the same column position where content would normally appear, preserving that same top-to-bottom scanning pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the site-wide full-width hero and navigation bar, the About page content appears as a single, elegant column centered in the browser window. The column is constrained to an editorial width (around 1100–1200px max) with generous white gutters on both sides, creating a magazine-like feel. At the top of this column, a softly spaced header block introduces the page with a discrete label and prominent title, left-aligned and surrounded by ample white space. Just below, the main About narrative flows downward as well-structured paragraphs and subsections, forming a comfortable reading zone that fills most of the vertical space while maintaining clear breathing room between ideas. At the very bottom of this column, a succinct, visually light contact/navigation prompt invites users to continue to a contact page without competing with the main story. If the content is still loading, a centered loading indicator appears in place of the article, and if something goes wrong, a centered error message block occupies that same central area. Throughout, the layout remains calm and clear, guiding the eye smoothly from top introduction to narrative to next-step action, in line with the modern, family-focused editorial aesthetic hinted at in the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-root acts as the structural container for all About page content within the broader site shell. It begins just below the global full-width hero and navigation bar, with a top margin of roughly 40–64px to clearly separate the page content from the nav. Horizontally, it defines a centered content column that spans approximately 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at around 1100–1200px max-width, with automatic left and right margins to create generous white gutters on large screens. Inside this container, all child components (header, body, contact navigation, and any loading/error states) are arranged in a linear vertical stack, aligned to the left edge of the column but visually centered in the viewport. Internal side padding of about 24px on desktop and 16–20px on smaller screens ensures text never touches the edges. Vertical spacing between major child blocks adheres to the 8px grid, typically 32–40px, preserving the airy editorial rhythm seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The container is part of the normal document flow (non-sticky, non-fixed). It establishes a centered flex or block layout context for its children and can enforce a minimum height of roughly 60–70% of the viewport so that even short content feels anchored and substantial within the page. On smaller screens, it expands to nearly full width (100% minus 16–20px side padding), maintaining the same top margin under the global nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the about-page-root, the about-content-header is the first visible block of About-specific content. It sits at the top of the column, starting about 24–32px below the container’s top edge, echoing the small '2026 Family Treasure' label and headline treatment from the mockup. Visually, it behaves like a compact editorial hero for the page: a subtle, smaller label or eyebrow line is placed at the top, left-aligned, spanning the width of the content column but occupying minimal height. Directly beneath this label, the main About title appears in a larger, bold type, potentially spanning one to three lines and occupying roughly 30–40% of the visible content area before the fold (excluding global hero/nav). There is generous whitespace above and below the title; specifically, the space between the bottom of the title and the start of the about-body-renderer is around 32–40px, clearly separating introduction from body text. Horizontally, the header matches the width of the main text column (no indentation), preserving a clean rectangular block impression. On smaller screens, the header remains at the top of the scroll, with similar padding, and the text reflows to maintain comfortable line lengths without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header scrolls with the rest of the page and remains in normal document flow. It does not stick to the top, but its relatively large typography and generous surrounding whitespace ensure it remains a strong visual anchor whenever it is in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-body-renderer sits directly beneath the about-content-header, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 32–40px. It forms the main reading area for the About narrative and extends downward as far as needed. Horizontally, it follows the same content width as the header, but the internal text layout adheres to comfortable editorial line lengths: paragraphs are left-aligned with a measure of roughly 60–75 characters per line on desktop, reducing in width on smaller screens as necessary. Internally, section headings, subheadings, and images (if present in the static HTML) stack vertically with clear separation: 24–32px between major subsections, 16–24px between paragraphs, and 32+px above any major visual break (e.g., an image or quote). The top of the body renderer begins within the upper half of the viewport when initially loaded, making the transition from header to body feel immediate and continuous. The overall effect is a calm, long-form article that fills most of the remaining vertical space on the page while still maintaining generous bottom padding (32–48px) before the next block (about-contact-navigation).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The body content scrolls naturally and can grow vertically without constraints, within the bounds of the about-page-root. On very wide screens, it remains centered with fixed max-width; on narrow screens, it fluidly adapts to full width minus consistent side padding, preserving the relaxed reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-contact-navigation appears as the final block within the about-page-root, immediately following the end of the about-body-renderer. It is separated from the last paragraph or section of the body by about 32–40px of vertical space, signaling a shift from narrative content to an action-focused section. Horizontally, it spans the same width as the body text, left-aligned, and is contained within a relatively shallow band of height—roughly 80–120px, depending on text length. This area might include a brief, soft prompt such as 'Want to connect with us?' set in regular text, followed by a more visually distinct inline link or text button that routes to the Contact page. The styling feels understated and editorial rather than button-heavy: the link might resemble one of the navigation chips in the mockup (stronger weight, accent color) but without heavy boxes or shadows. Generous padding (16–24px) inside this section ensures it does not feel cramped, and optional subtle separators (such as a thin top rule or slightly different background tint) can be used to gently distinguish it while keeping the overall page feeling cohesive and minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The contact navigation is part of the normal flow and sits at the bottom of the scrollable content column. On tall viewports with short About content, it may still appear above the fold, but it maintains its position anchored to the logical end of the article. No sticky or fixed behavior is applied; users naturally reach this block as they finish reading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-loading-indicator only appears while the About page content is being fetched. In this state, it effectively takes the place of the about-content-header and about-body-renderer within the about-page-root. The indicator is centered horizontally within the content column and vertically positioned within the upper half of the viewport so it is quickly noticeable but not pressed against the top edge. Imagine a subtle spinner or progress icon with a short status label beneath it; together they occupy a compact vertical stack roughly 80–140px tall. There is ample white space surrounding this stack on all sides, reflecting the low-density aesthetic of the site. Horizontally, the indicator is centered within the column but does not span the full width; the spinner and text are grouped tightly, creating a small focal point in the middle of an otherwise empty column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: While active, the loading indicator either replaces or overlays the main content area within the about-page-root. It can be implemented as a flex container that centers its children vertically within a minimum height area (roughly 50–60% of the viewport height). It scrolls with the page if the user has already scrolled down, but on initial load it appears in the first viewable portion of the content area just below the global nav, maintaining consistency with where the header and body would normally start.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-error-display is shown if the About content fails to load, occupying the same central column area that the header and body would normally use. It appears below the global hero and navigation with a top margin of about 40–64px, matching the spacing of the normal content start. The error message is wrapped in a visually distinct but still minimal block—a simple, full-width card within the content column, up to 480–600px wide, centered horizontally. Inside this block, text is left-aligned and stacked vertically: a short, bold error title at the top, a brief explanation beneath, and a retry action or navigation hint at the bottom. The block has comfortable internal padding of ~24px on all sides and is surrounded by generous white space above and below (24–32px from other elements or the container edges). There are no heavy shadows; at most, a thin border or a soft background tint differentiates it from the white page, aligning with the flat, clean aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When visible, the error display replaces the normal content within the about-page-root in the document flow. It does not float or stick; instead, it sits as a central, easily scannable message that users encounter immediately after the global nav. On devices where the viewport is tall, the message will appear within the upper half of the screen, preventing the need to scroll to discover that an error has occurred.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Contact page that shows dynamically managed contact information and allows visitors to send a message via a contact form, using API interfaces for static content and form submission.

core_features: Load and display static contact content (such as intro text, contact instructions, and response-time information) using getStaticPageContent.
Render a contact form bound to the submitContactForm interface with fields for name, email, subject, topic, and message.
Pre-fill contact form fields from URL parameters (for example, topic or subject passed from other pages).
Validate the contact form client-side before calling submitContactForm and handle API success and error responses.
Display a success or error status banner based on the submitContactForm response, including the returned ticketId when available.
Optionally persist submission results in the URL (e.g., status and ticketId) so the confirmation state can be restored on reload without local storage.
Provide navigation back to the home page (index.html) via a 'Back to Home' link or site logo.
user_workflows:
View contact information:
1. User clicks the 'Contact' link in the site footer.
2. Browser navigates to contact.html.
3. contact-page-shell reads URL parameters (if any) and calls getStaticPageContent with a pageKey derived from the route or an optional pageKey query parameter (defaulting to the contact page key).
4. contact-static-content displays the title and HTML body returned by getStaticPageContent, which may include instructions, email information, and response-time expectations.
5. The contact-form component is shown below the static instructions, ready for input.

Send a new contact message:
1. User opens contact.html (optionally with prefill parameters like ?topic=...&subject=...).
2. contact-form initializes its state using any relevant URL parameters (e.g., topic, subject, messagePrefill) while keeping fields editable.
3. User enters or edits their name, email, subject, topic, and message.
4. On clicking the form submit button, contact-form runs client-side validation (e.g., required fields and basic email format). If validation fails, inline error messages are set in component state and no API call is made.
5. If validation passes, contact-form sets isSubmitting state and calls submitContactForm with the current form values (name, email, subject, message, topic).
6. When submitContactForm returns:
   - If success is true: contact-form clears or locks the form, updates submission state, and notifies contact-form-status-banner of the success result (including ticketId and message). Optionally, contact-page-shell updates the browser URL (e.g., adding status=success&ticketId=...) using a navigation or history API without reloading.
   - If success is false or the call fails: contact-form-status-banner displays an error message and allows the user to try again.
7. User can copy or note the ticketId displayed in the success banner.
8. User may then click 'Back to Home' or the site logo to return to index.html.

Return to home after contacting:
1. After viewing contact details or submitting the form, user clicks the 'Back to Home' link or the site logo in the header.
2. contact-navigation-links handles the click and navigates to index.html (optionally adding a simple from=contact query parameter if needed for analytics or contextual behavior elsewhere).
3. The contact page does not persist additional state beyond any parameters included in the URL.

Revisit or share a confirmation state:
1. After a successful submission, contact-page-shell can update the URL to include status=success and ticketId=<value> (and optionally subject or topic).
2. If the user reloads contact.html with these parameters, contact-page-shell reads them on load.
3. Instead of calling submitContactForm again, contact-form-status-banner uses the URL values (status and ticketId) to show a non-interactive confirmation banner indicating that a message has already been sent, while contact-form may either be reset or shown as an optional new form.
4. User can still navigate away using the standard navigation links.
interactions:
Page load and content fetch:
- Trigger: contact.html is loaded via navigation.
- Behavior: contact-page-shell reads URL parameters (pageKey, topic, subject, messagePrefill, status, ticketId) and calls getStaticPageContent({ pageKey }). The resulting title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated are passed to contact-static-content for rendering.

Form field editing:
- Trigger: User types in or changes any field (name, email, subject, topic, message) in contact-form.
- Behavior: onFieldChange updates the internal form state and clears any field-level validation errors for that field.

Form submission:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary submit button or presses Enter while focused in the form.
- Behavior: onSubmit runs validation on required fields; if valid, sets isSubmitting=true and calls submitContactForm with the current form state values. The response updates submission state and drives status banner visibility and content.

Form submission success handling:
- Trigger: submitContactForm returns with success=true.
- Behavior: onSubmitSuccess stores the response (success, ticketId, message) in component state; contact-form-status-banner is shown in success mode; optionally, contact-page-shell updates the URL to contain status=success and ticketId so success state can be restored on reload.

Form submission error handling:
- Trigger: submitContactForm returns with success=false or the API call fails.
- Behavior: onSubmitError sets a submissionError state; contact-form-status-banner displays an error message. The form stays editable so the user can correct information and resubmit.

Status banner dismiss:
- Trigger: User clicks a close/dismiss control on contact-form-status-banner.
- Behavior: onDismiss hides the banner in local page state. Optionally, if the status was stored in URL parameters, contact-page-shell can remove status and ticketId parameters from the URL using history manipulation.

Back to Home navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to Home' link or site logo.
- Behavior: onClickBackToHome or onClickLogo navigates to index.html. Optional query parameter from=contact may be appended; this does not affect the Contact page behavior but can be used by index.html if needed.

Topic and subject prefill from URL:
- Trigger: contact.html is opened with URL parameters such as topic and subject (for example, from another page linking to contact.html with contextual information).
- Behavior: contact-page-shell reads these parameters and passes them as initial values to contact-form, which sets default form field values accordingly while still allowing the user to change them.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page relies on URL parameters and in-memory page state, not localStorage, to manage its behavior. Recognized URL parameters include: (1) pageKey: optional parameter used by contact-page-shell to determine which static content to load via getStaticPageContent. If not present, a default contact-specific pageKey is used. (2) topic: optional string used to preselect or prefill the 'topic' field in contact-form; the value is passed directly as the initial topic state and, if unchanged, will be submitted as the topic field to submitContactForm. (3) subject: optional string used to prefill the 'subject' field in contact-form. (4) messagePrefill: optional string used to prefill the 'message' field; used for contextual deep links from other pages. (5) status: optional string flag (e.g., 'success' or 'error') that, when present, informs contact-form-status-banner which mode to display on initial load. For example, after a successful submission followed by a redirect using the POST-Redirect-GET pattern, the app can redirect to contact.html?status=success&ticketId=... so a success message appears without re-submitting the form. (6) ticketId: optional string that, in combination with status=success, allows the page to show the reference/ticket identifier after reload or when a confirmation URL is shared. On initial page load, contact-page-shell reads these parameters and initializes its internal state, then passes relevant values as props to contact-form and contact-form-status-banner. When submitContactForm completes successfully, the page may choose to update the URL to include status and ticketId (via navigation or history.replaceState), ensuring that refreshing the page or sharing the URL preserves the confirmation state without storing any data in localStorage. Core static content (instructions, response-time expectations, and contact policy) always comes from getStaticPageContent and is not hardcoded in the page.

components.functionality: Orchestrates the Contact page lifecycle: reads URL parameters, loads static contact content via getStaticPageContent, initializes form defaults (topic, subject, message), manages global submission state (including success/error mode and ticketId), and passes data to child components. It also manages optional URL updates after form submission.
data_binding: URLSearchParams: pageKey, topic, subject, messagePrefill, status, ticketId
Child component props for contact-static-content, contact-form, and contact-form-status-banner
event_handlers: onInit: invoked on initial page load; reads URL parameters, determines effective pageKey, and triggers getStaticPageContent({ pageKey }).
onStaticContentLoaded: invoked when getStaticPageContent resolves; stores the returned content object in state and renders contact-static-content.
onFormSubmitSuccess: receives submitContactForm result from contact-form; updates submission state (success, message, ticketId) and may update the URL (e.g., status=success&ticketId=...).
onFormSubmitError: receives error information from contact-form; updates submission state to an error mode.
onStatusBannerDismiss: updates local state to hide the status banner and, if status and ticketId are present in the URL, may clear those query parameters via history manipulation.
onUrlParamChange: if the Contact page remains mounted while URL parameters change (e.g., internal navigation), re-reads parameters and updates form defaults or status display accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays the static content of the Contact page, including the main heading, descriptive text, any contact instructions, and information such as expected response time and how messages are used. All visible text and structure come from the bodyHtml and title returned by getStaticPageContent, ensuring content is centrally managed.
data_binding: getStaticPageContent.result.title: rendered as the main heading or section title.
getStaticPageContent.result.bodyHtml: rendered as HTML within the content area, which may include paragraphs, lists, and contact instructions.
getStaticPageContent.result.lastUpdated: optionally shown as a small 'last updated' note if present in the static content design.
event_handlers: onContentRender: lifecycle hook to render bodyHtml safely (e.g., using a trusted HTML renderer) after getStaticPageContent has completed.

components.functionality: Provides the interactive contact form used to send messages. It manages controlled inputs for name, email, subject, topic, and message, supports prefilled values from URL parameters, performs basic client-side validation, calls submitContactForm with the collected data, and notifies the shell about success or error outcomes.
data_binding: Initial form state: derived from URLSearchParams topic, subject, messagePrefill, combined with empty defaults for name and email.
Form fields: { name, email, subject, topic, message } bound to corresponding input elements.
Validation state: per-field and global errors, such as 'required' violations or invalid email format, stored in component state.
Submission state: { isSubmitting, lastResponse, lastError } where lastResponse may include success, ticketId, and message from submitContactForm.
submitContactForm.request: binds current form values to parameters (name, email, subject, message, topic) when onSubmit is triggered.
event_handlers: onFieldChange(fieldName, value): updates the specified form field in state and clears any related validation error.
onSubmit(event): prevents default form submission, runs client-side validation, and if valid, sets isSubmitting=true and calls submitContactForm({ name, email, subject, message, topic }).

onSubmitSuccess(apiResult): called when submitContactForm returns successfully with success=true; sets isSubmitting=false, stores apiResult (including ticketId and message), optionally resets or disables the form, and calls a callback (e.g., onFormSubmitSuccess) provided by contact-page-shell.

onSubmitError(apiResultOrError): called when submitContactForm fails or returns success=false; sets isSubmitting=false, stores error details, and calls a callback (e.g., onFormSubmitError) provided by contact-page-shell so the status banner can be updated.
onReset(optional): allows clearing form fields and validation errors if a reset action is provided in the UI.

components.functionality: Displays dynamic feedback about the contact form submission, including success confirmation with a ticketId or error messages if the submission failed. It reacts both to in-page submission results and to URL parameters (status and ticketId) on initial load so that confirmation can be shown after redirects or refreshes.
data_binding: submitContactForm.result: { success, ticketId, message } when available from a recent form submission.
URLSearchParams: status and ticketId, used to initialize the banner mode when the page is loaded or reloaded.
Page-shell submission state: flags such as submissionStatus ('idle', 'success', 'error') and submissionMessage, passed from contact-page-shell.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: reads status and ticketId from URL parameters on first render; if status indicates success, shows a confirmation message that includes ticketId; if status indicates error, shows a generic error notification.
onUpdateFromForm: triggered when contact-form signals a new submission result; updates the banner content and mode accordingly.
onDismiss: hides the banner in local state and notifies contact-page-shell so any associated URL parameters (status, ticketId) can optionally be removed.

components.functionality: Provides functional navigation elements for the Contact page, specifically a 'Back to Home' link and handling of the site logo click to navigate to index.html, fulfilling the outgoing navigation requirement.
data_binding: Navigation targets: index.html as the destination for both 'Back to Home' and logo clicks.
Optional context parameters: a simple query parameter such as from=contact may be appended when navigating away; this is derived from the current page context rather than hardcoded display content.
event_handlers: onClickBackToHome: navigates to index.html (optionally adding from=contact to the URL).
onClickLogo: navigates to index.html (optionally with the same contextual parameter).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom editorial stack as the mockup: a full-width hero and global chrome live inside the page shell at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width navigation band. Below this, the main contact content forms a single centered column. Within that column, the order is: a compact static-content label/intro (mirroring the small '2026 Family Treasure' line), then a status banner band (when active), followed by a large, card-like contact form that reads as the primary feature block. Finally, a secondary row of navigation links sits at the bottom of the column, visually echoing the nav bar but at a smaller scale.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom editorial stack as the mockup: a full-width hero and global chrome live inside the page shell at the very top, followed immediately by a full-width navigation band. Below this, the main contact content forms a single centered column. Within that column, the order is: a compact static-content label/intro (mirroring the small '2026 Family Treasure' line), then a status banner band (when active), followed by a large, card-like contact form that reads as the primary feature block. Finally, a secondary row of navigation links sits at the bottom of the column, visually echoing the nav bar but at a smaller scale.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the hero area inside the page shell occupies roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport, the global navigation band about 8–10%, and the remaining 55–60% is given to the main contact content stack (static content, status banner, form, and navigation links). Horizontally, all main content (static content, banner, form, and navigation links) is constrained to a centered column of about 1100–1200px max width, with the inner content for text and form fields occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop. Generous vertical gaps (24–40px) separate each block, with consistent left alignment within the central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is kept deliberately low-density and breathable. Each block has its own clear vertical space: static content is a light, single-line label plus brief copy; the status banner is a slim horizontal strip; the form sits in a large, comfortable card with 24–32px internal padding; navigation links have ample horizontal padding and line spacing and can wrap to a second row instead of crowding. No component visually touches the viewport edges; white gutters on both sides prime the page for calm, easy reading.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the central hero headline and supporting text within the page shell, then naturally drops to the full-width navigation band. From there, attention moves down into the main column: it catches the smaller, left-aligned static content label and description (which acts as a soft section tag), then is pulled into the large, prominent contact form card as the key call-to-action. Any status banner just above the form momentarily interrupts the flow with an accent color and short feedback text, then attention continues down to the bottom navigation links that provide optional next steps. Overall, the flow mirrors the mockup’s movement from brand and structure to focused interaction and then to secondary navigation.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page reads like a modern editorial feature with a clear call-to-action at its core. At the very top, a full-width hero image with a warm, family-oriented photograph stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, occupying about a third of the initial screen height. Centered over this image is a bold masthead-style page title such as “Get in touch” with a short, softer subtitle beneath it. Directly under the hero, a full-width, flat navigation bar spans the page, showing the primary site sections in a horizontally scrollable or wrapping list, echoing the original mockup’s nav presentation. Below this chrome, the page transitions into a centered content column: first a compact, left-aligned line introduces the section (e.g., “Contact & Support” and a brief sentence), sitting with generous top and bottom margins so it feels like a quiet label. Immediately underneath, when present, a slim status banner bar runs the full width of the column to communicate form submission feedback using subtle coloration. The primary visual weight comes next: a large, white, card-like contact form that fills the center of the screen, taking up most of the column width with ample padding and well-spaced fields, mimicking the presence and scale of the “100 BEST …” feature card from the mockup. After the form, the page gently tapers off into a band of navigation links arranged horizontally near the bottom of the main column, lighter and less tall than the main nav but visually related, offering secondary paths without competing with the form. Together, the components create a warm, spacious, editorial contact experience that guides visitors smoothly from brand impression to taking action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the full browser width, establishing the 12-column centered grid and the overarching visual structure. At the very top of the shell is a full-width hero section that spans edge-to-edge horizontally and takes up roughly 30–35% of the initial viewport height. This hero features a large, warm family-oriented photograph as its background, with the main page title and subtitle vertically and horizontally centered over the image. The title uses a bold, editorial type scale; the subtitle sits just below in a lighter weight, both aligned centrally within the hero. Directly beneath the hero, still inside the page shell, sits the global site navigation bar: a flat, white band that stretches full width and is about 64–80px tall, containing horizontally arranged primary navigation items that can scroll or wrap to a second row. Below this chrome, the shell constrains all subsequent content (static contact info, status banner, form, and bottom navigation links) to a centrally aligned column with a max width of roughly 1100–1200px. The column is centered within the viewport with generous side gutters (about 15–20% of the viewport width on large screens). Vertical spacing within the shell follows the 8px-based scale: 32–40px between major sections and 24px padding inside bands, ensuring a calm, editorial flow from top hero to the main contact content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The shell scrolls normally with the page, defining the global grid and full-width hero at the top. The hero and navigation bar remain at the top of the document flow (no sticky behavior assumed), with all other components flowing beneath inside the constrained central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The static content section sits just below the global navigation bar, as the first element within the centered main content column. It begins approximately 32–40px beneath the bottom edge of the nav bar, creating a clear separation. Horizontally, it aligns to the left within the central column and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (up to about 720–800px on large screens), mirroring the width used by the primary article card in the mockup. At the top of this section, a single, small line of text acts like a section label—similar to “2026 Family Treasure” in the mockup—e.g., “Contact & Support” or “2026 Family Connections”. This label is left-aligned and sits with 4–8px of spacing above and below, so it feels like a quiet, understated tag. Below the label, one or two short paragraphs of descriptive contact information (e.g., how to reach the team, typical response times, or alternative channels) are arranged in a single column with comfortable line height and a 16–24px gap between lines/paragraphs. The entire section has about 24–32px of vertical margin before the next component, ensuring it reads as a discrete introductory block before the more interactive elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document flow within the central column; it neither sticks nor overlays other elements. On smaller screens, it maintains full width within the column and reflows text naturally while preserving top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status banner is positioned immediately below the static content section and directly above the contact form, acting as a slim feedback strip in the vertical flow. When visible, it appears about 16–24px beneath the bottom of the static content text. It spans the full width of the main content column (matching the left alignment and right boundary of the contact form), typically around 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop. The banner height is modest, roughly 40–56px depending on text length, and it uses a solid or subtly tinted background color to stand out from the white page—aligned with the site’s accent color palette but still flat and shadow-free. The status text is left-aligned within the banner with about 16–24px padding on all sides, keeping it readable and calm rather than urgent or loud. The space between the banner and the top edge of the contact form below is about 16–24px, so the banner feels tightly associated with the form while remaining a separate visual band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The banner is part of the regular scroll flow, appearing and disappearing conditionally based on the form state. It does not stick to the top of the viewport; instead, it stays anchored just above the form within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is the main focal element of the page’s content area and is placed directly beneath the status banner (or beneath the static content if no banner is present). It starts approximately 24px below the element above it, occupying the full width of the central content column while maintaining the same left alignment. Visually, it behaves like the large “100 BEST …” feature card from the mockup: a substantial, rectangular, white card that stands out due to its size and contained content rather than any heavy borders or shadows. The card’s width is roughly 60–70% of the viewport on desktop, up to about 720–800px, and it includes generous internal padding of around 24–32px on all sides. At the top of this card, a left-aligned form title (e.g., “Send us a message”) and a short single-line helper text or subheading mirror an editorial headline block, with about 8–16px spacing between the two. Below this, form fields (name, email, subject, message, etc.) are stacked vertically with consistent spacing of 16–24px between each field group, maintaining a sense of openness. The primary submit button is aligned either left with the field labels or slightly inset but still within the card, with about 24–32px of padding around it. Bottom padding of 32–40px ensures ample whitespace beneath the last control. The card itself is completely flat (no heavy shadows), relying on whitespace and content density to create hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form is a standard block element within the central column, scrolling normally with the page. It is not fixed or sticky. On smaller screens, it expands to nearly full width inside the mobile content margins while preserving its generous vertical spacing and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact navigation links are located at the bottom of the main content column, appearing after the contact form with a clear separation of about 32–40px of vertical space. They function as a lighter, contextual counterpart to the main navigation bar. Horizontally, this section aligns with the same left and right bounds as the form card, giving a sense of continuity in the column. The links themselves are arranged in a horizontal row by default—e.g., options such as “FAQ”, “Support Center”, “Privacy”, or “Back to Articles”—with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between each label. If there are more links than can comfortably fit on one line, they wrap to a second row while maintaining consistent spacing, similar to the multi-row nav behavior in the mockup. The section may include a subtle top border line or a slightly muted text color compared to the primary nav, so it feels tertiary and does not compete with the form. Vertical padding of about 16–24px above and below the links ensures the block breathes and provides a gentle visual taper at the end of the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This section is fully within the normal scroll flow and aligned to the central column. It is not sticky or floating. On smaller screens, the links reflow into multiple lines with left alignment, maintaining ample vertical spacing to preserve the airy, editorial feel.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the site's privacy policy content, including how user data and preferences are handled, using a static content API.

core_features: Load privacy policy content from the static content service via getStaticPageContent and render it as HTML.
Display the policy title and last updated date returned by getStaticPageContent so users can see when the policy was last revised.
Support deep-linking to specific policy sections using URL parameters so users can jump directly to content about topics such as data collection or newsletter preferences.
Provide in-page navigation to quickly move between major policy sections, using section anchors derived from the loaded HTML content.
Offer a simple in-page text search/filter to help users find specific terms within the policy text without additional API calls.
Handle loading and error states if the policy content cannot be retrieved, ensuring the user receives clear feedback.
user_workflows:
View full privacy policy:
1) User navigates to privacy.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2) On page load, the privacy-policy-loader component derives a pageKey for this route (optionally considering URL parameters such as locale or version) and calls getStaticPageContent.
3) The response (title, bodyHtml, lastUpdated) is stored in page state.
4) The privacy-policy-content component renders bodyHtml, and the page title is updated using the returned title.
5) The metadata bar displays the lastUpdated value.
6) User scrolls through the policy to read how data is collected, used, and protected.

Open policy at a specific section (deep link):
1) User clicks a link elsewhere on the site pointing to privacy.html with a section-related URL parameter (for example, privacy.html?section=newsletter or an equivalent configuration).
2) The privacy-policy-loader reads the section parameter from the URL.
3) After getStaticPageContent resolves and bodyHtml is rendered, the content component scrolls to the corresponding section anchor if it exists.
4) The section navigation highlights the active section based on the current section parameter.

Navigate between policy sections using in-page navigation:
1) User arrives at privacy.html and the policy content is loaded via getStaticPageContent.
2) The section navigation component analyses the rendered headings in bodyHtml to build a list of sections.
3) User clicks a section link in the navigation.
4) The click handler updates the URL (for example, by updating a section parameter or hash) without reloading the page.
5) The content component scrolls smoothly to the corresponding section anchor and updates any active state highlighting.

Search within the privacy policy text:
1) User types a search term into the privacy-policy-search input.
2) The search component updates the URL with a query-related parameter (for example, q) and emits an event with the current search term.
3) The content component listens to search term changes and performs a client-side filter/highlight of matching text within bodyHtml.
4) Optionally, the content component scrolls to the first match and shows match count or indicators, without additional API requests.

Handle content loading failure:
1) User navigates to privacy.html.
2) The loader component calls getStaticPageContent with the derived pageKey.
3) If the call fails or returns incomplete data, the loader sets an error state.
4) The content component detects the error state and displays a generic error message and a retry control.
5) If the user clicks retry, the loader calls getStaticPageContent again using the same pageKey.
interactions: Initial page load triggers getStaticPageContent based on a pageKey derived from the current route and optional URL parameters (such as locale or version).
Clicking the footer "Privacy Policy" navigation link takes the user to privacy.html (optionally with parameters like locale), causing the loader to request the corresponding localized policy content.
Clicking an in-page section link in the section navigation updates the section-related URL parameter or hash and scrolls to the matching heading in the rendered policy content.
Modifying the search input updates a query-related URL parameter and triggers client-side filtering or highlighting of matching terms within bodyHtml.
Clicking a retry button after a failed load attempts to call getStaticPageContent again with the same derived pageKey.

Using the browser's back/forward navigation updates URL parameters (such as section or query), which the components observe and use to adjust scroll position, active section, and search highlights accordingly without re-calling the API unless the pageKey changes.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page is driven by URL parameters and the getStaticPageContent response. A primary parameter (for example, locale or version) can be used to derive the pageKey passed to getStaticPageContent so that different variants of the privacy policy can be loaded without changing the code. A section-related parameter (for example, section) and/or URL hash indicates which section of the policy should be scrolled into view after bodyHtml is rendered. A query-related parameter (for example, q) holds the user's in-page search term and is applied purely on the client to highlight or navigate to matches in the rendered content. Changing parameters that affect pageKey triggers a fresh call to getStaticPageContent; changing parameters that affect section or query only adjusts client-side state (scroll position, active navigation state, and highlighting) without additional API calls. The entire visible state of the page (selected section, search term, and policy variant) is recoverable from the combination of URL parameters and the latest getStaticPageContent result.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page preserves the mockup’s stacked, linear structure: a full-width photographic hero at the very top with the page masthead ('Privacy Policy') centered over the image; directly beneath it, a full-width navigation bar for site sections (not legal subnav), still spanning edge-to-edge of the browser. Below the nav, the layout snaps into a centered content column where a small, left-aligned meta label (e.g., 'Last updated • 2026') introduces the policy area. Under that label, the main privacy policy content appears as a single, continuous editorial article: first a short, highlighted introductory block (like a feature card) that summarizes the policy in friendly language, followed by structured text sections (headings, paragraphs, lists) laid out as clean, long-form reading content within the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page preserves the mockup’s stacked, linear structure: a full-width photographic hero at the very top with the page masthead ('Privacy Policy') centered over the image; directly beneath it, a full-width navigation bar for site sections (not legal subnav), still spanning edge-to-edge of the browser. Below the nav, the layout snaps into a centered content column where a small, left-aligned meta label (e.g., 'Last updated • 2026') introduces the policy area. Under that label, the main privacy policy content appears as a single, continuous editorial article: first a short, highlighted introductory block (like a feature card) that summarizes the policy in friendly language, followed by structured text sections (headings, paragraphs, lists) laid out as clean, long-form reading content within the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 30–35% of the initial viewport height is occupied by the hero image and its masthead text; the navigation bar beneath it occupies about 8–10% of the vertical space. The remainder of the viewport is dedicated to the privacy policy body within a centered content column that uses about 60–70% of the viewport width, with 15–20% whitespace gutters on either side on desktop. Major vertical breaks occur: a clear gap (around 32–40px) between hero and nav, between nav and the content label, and between the label and the first policy block, with similar 24–32px gaps between major policy sections (top-level headings). Internal text spacing (line height and paragraph spacing) is generous but not exaggerated, supporting readability without feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density stays deliberately low and calm despite being a legal page. Copy is broken into short paragraphs with strong typographic hierarchy for headings and subheadings, generous line height, and clear spacing between sections. There are no sidebars, no dense multi-column legal tables, and no competing cards; the user mostly sees one primary text column at a time. Occasional sub-section callouts (e.g., a lightly tinted box for ‘Key Takeaways’) are used sparingly and full-width within the column, mirroring the editorial card style of the mockup. Overall, the page feels like a lifestyle-article rendition of a privacy policy: easier to read, with lots of white space, minimal visual noise, and clear separation between topics.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first lands on the central hero headline 'Privacy Policy' in large, bold type over the warm lifestyle image, then moves slightly down to a human, friendly subtitle that frames the policy in family-oriented terms. From there, the gaze travels downward to the full-width navigation bar, scanning left to right across the main site categories (with one or two categories visually emphasized, echoing the mockup). Scrolling brings the eye to a smaller, left-aligned meta label (e.g., 'Updated 2026') that subtly introduces the main content. Below that, a more prominent introductory block summarizes how the site respects family privacy, acting like the hero-style article card in the mockup and becoming the next focal point. As the user scrolls further, they follow a straightforward vertical reading path through clearly separated headings and sections, with each new section visually introduced by increased whitespace and bolder headings, maintaining a gentle, guided reading rhythm down the page.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page adopts the same modern editorial aesthetic as the main blog, but applies it to legal content in a friendly, approachable way. At the very top, a full-bleed, edge-to-edge hero image spans the entire browser width, showing a warm family lifestyle scene. Centered both horizontally and vertically within this hero is the page title 'Privacy Policy' in a large, bold masthead style, with a single, softer subtitle line just beneath it that briefly reassures visitors about their data and family safety.
