This hero area fills roughly the upper third of the viewport. Immediately below, a full-width white navigation bar sits flush against the hero, extending from the left to right edges of the browser. Inside this bar, the actual nav content is constrained to the same central width as the main content column (about 1100–1200px) and aligned to a 12-column grid. Navigation links are arranged in a single or double row depending on available width, with generous horizontal padding around each item and occasional accent color or weight to highlight key sections, matching the mockup’s spacious, pill-like nav feel. Scrolling slightly reveals the main content region: the page tightens into a centered column occupying around two-thirds of the available width on desktop, with wide, even gutters on both sides. At the top of this column, aligned to the left, a small, understated line of text (e.g., 'Last updated • March 2026') sits with generous breathing room above and below, echoing the '2026 Family Treasure' label from the mockup and acting as a calm gateway into the legal content. Just below this label, a more visually prominent introductory block spans the full width of the content column—this block may sit on a very light background or simply use stronger typography, presenting a concise, friendly summary of how the site collects and uses data, mirroring the large feature card feel of '100 BEST...' from the mockup. Beneath this, the detailed privacy sections flow in a single vertical stream: each section begins with a clear, bold heading (e.g., 'Information We Collect', 'How We Use Your Information'), followed by short paragraphs and bullet lists, all left-aligned in the central column. Sections are separated by 24–40px of vertical space, creating a sense of individual chapters instead of one wall of text. There are no sidebars or competing modules; the user’s entire focus stays on the main text. The result is a calm, magazine-like legal page that visually connects to the rest of the site’s lifestyle-focused design while guiding the user gently downward through the policy, step by step, without visual clutter or confusion.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Displays the site's Terms of Use, including permitted content usage, disclaimers, and rules for user-generated content, using dynamically loaded static content.

core_features: Load the Terms of Use content from the backend using the getStaticPageContent interface and a pageKey associated with this route.
Render the returned title, HTML body, and last-updated date for the Terms of Use in a readable, scrollable layout.
Provide in-page navigation to jump to specific sections within the Terms of Use (e.g., permitted use, liability, intellectual property, comments), based on anchors/headings in the loaded HTML.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the Terms of Use using URL parameters so users can share links that open at a given section.
Reflect the last updated date from the backend so users understand when the terms were most recently changed.
user_workflows:
View full Terms of Use:
1. User clicks the "Terms of Use" link in the footer or navigates directly to terms.html.
2. The page router resolves this path and initializes the Terms of Use page.
3. On initial load, the page calls getStaticPageContent with the configured pageKey for the Terms of Use.
4. The data loader component receives { title, bodyHtml, lastUpdated } and passes them to the content viewer component.
5. The content viewer renders the title, shows the last updated date, and injects the bodyHtml (which contains the legal sections, headings, and any anchors).
6. The in-page navigation component parses headings/anchors within bodyHtml and builds a list of section links if available.
7. The user scrolls through the content, optionally using the in-page navigation links to jump to specific sections.

Navigate directly to a specific section:
1. User receives a URL to the Terms of Use page that includes a section query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section={sectionId}).
2. The page router loads the Terms of Use page and passes the current URL (including query parameters) to the state logic.
3. The data loader calls getStaticPageContent with the Terms of Use pageKey and obtains bodyHtml.
4. Once bodyHtml has been rendered into the DOM, the scroll/anchor component reads the section query parameter and looks for a matching element ID within the rendered content.
5. If a matching section element is found, the component scrolls the page to that element (and may optionally focus it for accessibility).
6. If no matching section is found, the page remains at the top of the content.

Share a link to a specific Terms of Use section:
1. User opens the Terms of Use page and scrolls to a section they want to share (e.g., the part describing permitted use of content or comments policy).
2. The user clicks a "copy link" or similar control associated with a section in the in-page navigation or near a heading (if implemented using anchors derived from bodyHtml).
3. The section link component reads the section's anchor ID and constructs a URL of the form terms.html?section={sectionId}.
4. The URL is copied to the clipboard or shown to the user for manual copying.
5. When another user opens this URL, the direct-into-section workflow is executed, scrolling directly to the shared section.

Understand when terms last changed:
1. User opens the Terms of Use page via the footer or direct link.
2. The data loader retrieves lastUpdated from getStaticPageContent.
3. The content viewer displays the lastUpdated value in a standard location (e.g., below the page title).
4. If the backend later updates the Terms of Use, a subsequent page load retrieves and displays the new lastUpdated value without any client-side code changes.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Trigger: Page initialization (onInit / onMount).
- Behavior: The page calls getStaticPageContent with the Terms of Use pageKey. While awaiting the response, the loader component can show a loading state. On success, it passes the data to the content viewer and in-page navigation components; on error, it triggers an error state (e.g., a message that the terms could not be loaded).

Click on in-page navigation link:
- Trigger: User clicks a link in the in-page navigation list.
- Behavior: The click handler prevents default navigation if needed, updates the URL's section query parameter to the selected section ID using the History API, and scrolls smoothly to the corresponding section element in the rendered bodyHtml.

Manual scrolling:
- Trigger: User scrolls through the Terms of Use content.
- Behavior: The scroll handler may optionally update the currently active section in the in-page navigation (e.g., highlighting the section whose heading is currently in view). This interaction does not call any backend interface.

Direct navigation with ?section parameter:
- Trigger: Page load where the current URL contains a section query parameter.
- Behavior: After getStaticPageContent completes and bodyHtml is rendered, the scroll/anchor component reads the section parameter, finds the element with a matching ID, and scrolls it into view.

Copy link to current section:
- Trigger: User clicks a "copy link" control near a section heading or in the in-page navigation.
- Behavior: The event handler reads the associated section ID, updates or ensures the URL contains ?section={sectionId}, and copies the full URL to the clipboard using browser APIs. No backend calls are made.

Navigation away from page:
- Trigger: User clicks a different navigation link (e.g., to articles, recipes, or planner tools).
- Behavior: The router navigates to the new page. No additional API calls related to Terms of Use are made.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Terms of Use page is primarily driven by a single backend call and simple URL-based state.

- Backend state:
  - The page makes exactly one call to getStaticPageContent, passing a pageKey that identifies the Terms of Use content. This pageKey is configured at the routing level (for example, derived from the route name) so that the component logic does not need to hardcode the key.
  - The response provides the canonical title, bodyHtml, and lastUpdated fields, which become the source of truth for the page.

- URL parameters:
  - section (optional): A query parameter representing the ID of a section anchor within bodyHtml.
    - On initial load, if section is present, the page waits for bodyHtml to be rendered, then attempts to scroll to the element with id equal to the section parameter value.
    - When a user clicks a section link in the in-page navigation, the page updates the section parameter in the URL (using pushState/replaceState) without reloading the page, then scrolls to that section.
    - If the section parameter does not match any element ID, the page remains at the top and the parameter is effectively ignored.
  - No other URL parameters are required for core functionality.

- Client-side state:
  - loadingState: Tracks whether getStaticPageContent is in 'loading', 'success', or 'error' state; controls whether to show loading indicators or error messages.
  - contentState: Holds the latest { title, bodyHtml, lastUpdated } returned by getStaticPageContent.
  - navigationState: Holds the list of derived section anchors (e.g., an array of { id, label, level }) parsed from bodyHtml, and the currently active section based on scroll position or last clicked navigation link.

All stateful behavior on this page is derived from getStaticPageContent responses and the URL, without using localStorage or any other persistent client-side storage.

components.functionality: Fetches the Terms of Use static content via getStaticPageContent when the page initializes and manages loading/error states.
Receives { title, bodyHtml, lastUpdated } from the loader and renders the Terms of Use page title, last-updated information, and HTML body content.
Parses the rendered bodyHtml (e.g., heading and anchor elements) to build a structured list of sections for in-page navigation and exposes them to other components.

Displays a list of sections derived from the Terms of Use content and allows users to jump to specific sections by clicking navigation links, updating the URL's section parameter and scrolling accordingly.
Controls scrolling to the correct section based on the URL parameter or user interaction, ensuring the page scrolls to the element whose ID matches the current section parameter value.
Generates shareable URLs pointing to specific Terms of Use sections by combining the current base URL with the appropriate section query parameter and exposes an optional action to copy this link.
Reflects the current loading or error state of the Terms of Use content (e.g., showing loading feedback while getStaticPageContent is pending and an error message if the call fails).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column center-aligned grid with a constrained content width (approximately 1100–1200px) and generous outer margins on larger screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The Terms of Use page reuses the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom editorial structure: a full-width hero at the very top (with the page title and a warm family-oriented image), followed immediately by a full-width, white in-page navigation bar dedicated to Terms sections. Beneath this, a single centered content column holds the parsed Terms text, organized into clearly separated sections. Within this column, section labels and headings follow the same editorial hierarchy as the mockup’s '2026 Family Treasure' tag and feature card title, but adapted to legal content. No traditional sidebars are visible; all components align to this central column and full-width bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Terms of Use page reuses the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom editorial structure: a full-width hero at the very top (with the page title and a warm family-oriented image), followed immediately by a full-width, white in-page navigation bar dedicated to Terms sections. Beneath this, a single centered content column holds the parsed Terms text, organized into clearly separated sections. Within this column, section labels and headings follow the same editorial hierarchy as the mockup’s '2026 Family Treasure' tag and feature card title, but adapted to legal content. No traditional sidebars are visible; all components align to this central column and full-width bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 30–35% of the viewport remains a hero area, and another ~8–10% is allocated to the full-width Terms in-page navigation bar. Below that, the main Terms content occupies a centered column spanning about 60–70% of the viewport width (max ~1100–1200px), leaving 15–20% white gutters on each side on desktop. Vertical spacing between major elements (hero → nav, nav → content, content sections) uses 24–40px gaps, aligned to the 8px base grid. The load state indicator occupies only a few pixels of height at the very top of the page and does not substantially change the space allocation.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is calm and spacious. The Terms text is set in an easy-to-read measure with generous line-height and ample white space between sections (32–40px). The in-page navigation items are padded and well-separated, more like editorial category chips than dense tabs. Only one main scrolling column of legal content is presented; auxiliary behaviors (parsing, scrolling control, link generation, loading feedback) do not introduce visually heavy UI. The result is a low-density, editorial legal page instead of a crowded document viewer.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: the eye first lands on the central hero title 'Terms of Use' and supporting subtitle, then moves down to the full-width in-page navigation bar listing key sections (e.g., Use of Content, User Conduct, Disclaimers). From left to right, users scan these navigation items, with the current/primary section subtly accented. As they scroll down, the eye catches a smaller, left-aligned meta line at the top of the content column (e.g., 'Updated January 2026'), then flows into the first Terms section. The scroll controller and section parser work together so that as the reader moves vertically through the document, the in-page nav updates to reflect their position, maintaining a smooth hierarchy from global title to section structure to detailed paragraphs.

layout.overall_description: On desktop, the Terms of Use page opens with a full-width hero image occupying roughly the top third of the viewport, featuring a warm, family-oriented photograph with the 'Terms of Use' title and a brief, reassuring subtitle centered over the image. Immediately beneath this, spanning the full browser width, sits a flat white in-page navigation bar dedicated to the Terms sections: a horizontal row (or two rows if needed) of softly padded text links representing major sections like 'Overview', 'Acceptable Use', and 'Privacy & Data'. This bar aligns visually with the grid and feels like the legal counterpart to the category navigation in the mockup. As you scroll past the hero, the navigation bar softly sticks to the top edge of the viewport, becoming a steady reference point. Below this bar, the main content appears as a single, centered column ~1100–1200px wide, offset by generous left and right white gutters. At the very top of this column, a small, left-aligned line such as 'Updated January 2026 • Family Wellbeing Co.' echoes the mockup’s editorial section label. The Terms document itself is broken into clearly defined sections, each with a bold heading, a comfortable paragraph width, and ample vertical spacing so each section reads like its own editorial block. Invisible helpers ensure that clicking navigation items scrolls smoothly to the corresponding section, aligning the section heading just below the sticky navigation bar. A thin, accent-colored progress bar at the top of the page provides unobtrusive feedback while the Terms content loads, after which the page settles into a calm, low-density reading experience consistent with a modern lifestyle blog’s legal page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader governs the main content region that begins just beneath the full-width in-page navigation bar. While the Terms content is being fetched, the area where the centered Terms text would normally appear is replaced by a simple, centered loading state. This loading state lives inside the same central content column that the Terms text will occupy: roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, maxing out at 1100–1200px, and centered horizontally with equal white gutters on either side. Vertically, there is a 24–32px gap below the in-page navigation bar before the loader’s content begins, preserving the editorial breathing room. The loader’s visual elements (e.g., a short line of text like 'Loading Terms of Use…') are centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible portion of this content column above the fold, with ample padding of about 24px around any text or icon to maintain the spacious, minimal look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader occupies the main content column in place of the Terms text until data is ready, then gracefully disappears. It does not change the overall page structure; it simply fills the area under the in-page nav and above the page fold with a centered message while the content viewer remains hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content viewer is the primary visual body of the page, occupying a single, centered column directly below the full-width in-page navigation bar. It begins approximately 24–32px beneath the bottom edge of the nav bar, providing a clear separation between navigation and reading content. Horizontally, it spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, constrained to roughly 1100–1200px, and is centered so that equal white gutters of about 15–20% width appear on both sides on larger screens. At the very top of this column, aligned to the left edge of the text, the viewer renders a small, muted line of meta information (e.g., 'Updated January 2026 • Legal'), visually analogous to the '2026 Family Treasure' label in the mockup; this sits about 8–12px above the first main heading. Below that, each major Terms section is presented as a vertical block with a bold section heading followed by paragraphs of text; headings sit about 24px above their first paragraph, and separate sections are divided by 32–40px of vertical white space, preserving the calm editorial rhythm. Margins of 24px on the left and right inside the content column maintain a comfortable text measure. As users scroll, the content viewer aligns each section so the section heading moves just beneath the sticky in-page nav bar before settling into place, ensuring smooth legibility and maintaining the document’s vertical hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The content viewer scrolls naturally with the page as a continuous central column. It does not float or fix itself, but its top edge position relative to the sticky in-page navigation is managed indirectly by the section scroller so headings never hide behind the nav bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section parser operates within the bounds of the content viewer’s central column and shapes how the Terms text is visually segmented. It identifies headings and converts them into distinct vertical sections, each treated like a self-contained editorial block. Each parsed section starts with its heading aligned flush to the left text edge of the content column and placed approximately 32px below the end of the previous section’s text. Directly above each heading, the parser reserves an invisible buffer of around 80–96px to account for the height of the sticky in-page navigation bar; this ensures that when a section is scrolled or jumped to, the heading appears just below the nav instead of being covered by it. Within each section, the parser enforces consistent paragraph spacing of 12–16px between lines of text and 16–24px between distinct paragraphs or bullet groups, maintaining the page’s low-density, legal-document-as-editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The parser itself is invisible but dictates the internal vertical rhythm inside the content column. It defines anchor positions slightly above each heading so that scroll operations land sections in a visually comfortable position just under the sticky navigation, without introducing additional visible UI elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page navigation appears as a full-width, flat white bar that sits directly under the hero section and spans edge-to-edge across the viewport, mirroring the navigation band seen in the mockup. Vertically, this bar is about 56–72px tall, with 16–20px of padding above and below its contents. Inside, the navigational items align to the same central grid as the content: the clickable section labels (e.g., 'Overview', 'User Conduct', 'Intellectual Property') are arranged in a horizontal row, constrained to the 1100–1200px central content width and centered on the page, leaving equal white gutters on both sides. Each label is rendered as minimal, text-only or chip-like items with generous horizontal padding (12–16px left and right) and 8–12px vertical padding, and 16–24px gaps between items. If the labels exceed the available width, they wrap to a second line or become horizontally scrollable within the bar, but always within this central alignment. The current or active section’s label is differentiated with a slightly bolder weight and a warm accent color underline or pill background, echoing the subtle emphasis seen in the mockup’s nav categories. There is a 24px gap between the bottom of this navigation bar and the beginning of the main Terms content viewer, clearly separating navigation from reading space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This bar is initially positioned directly beneath the hero and scrolls out of the hero area, then becomes sticky: once the page is scrolled so that the bar reaches the top of the viewport, it pins itself there and remains fixed at the top of the screen while the central content scrolls underneath. Its background remains solid white, creating a clean top edge under which the Terms headings appear as users navigate through the document or click section links.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section scroller operates within the main viewport’s vertical axis, coordinating the relationship between clicks in the in-page navigation bar and the vertical position of sections in the content column. While invisible, its effect is clearly felt in how sections come into view: when a user selects a section label from the in-page nav, the page scrolls smoothly so that the corresponding Terms heading lines up just below the sticky navigation bar, leaving a comfortable 16–24px gap between the bar’s bottom edge and the section heading. As the user scrolls manually, the scroller detects which section’s heading is currently closest to this offset position and subtly updates the styling of the matching nav item in the bar, maintaining the editorial flow from navigation to text. The scroll motion is gentle and eased, keeping with the calm, lifestyle tone rather than feeling abrupt or mechanical.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This controller does not occupy dedicated space; it manipulates scroll behavior within the existing page layout. It respects the sticky positioning of the in-page nav and the 80–96px invisible offsets above each section defined by the parser, ensuring that automated scrolls never overshoot or hide headings behind the navigation bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The link helper works within the structure created by the section parser and the in-page navigation bar, assigning clean, human-readable anchors to each major Terms section. While it has no visible UI of its own, its influence is reflected in the consistency between the text labels in the navigation bar and the underlying URL fragments. For example, a section titled 'User Generated Content' in the content viewer becomes a labeled item 'User Generated Content' in the nav bar, and the link helper ensures the page can be addressed with a URL like /terms#user-generated-content. Visually, this means that deep-linked visits land users directly within the central content column at the correct section, with the relevant nav item already highlighted and the heading aligned just beneath the sticky nav, preserving the editorial layout from the first frame the user sees.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The link helper does not consume physical space; it hooks into the page’s anchors and scroll behavior. It ensures that external or internal links jump to the correct vertical position in the central content column, honoring the same section offsets used by the scroller and parser so the visual layout is always consistent when arriving via a link.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The load state indicator presents as a very thin, accent-colored progress bar aligned to the very top edge of the page, running full-width from the left to the right edge of the viewport. It sits above the hero image and in-page navigation bar, occupying only about 2–3px in height, and rests flush against the browser’s top chrome, echoing the minimal, flat aesthetic of the mockup. When active, it subtly animates from left to right, signaling that the Terms content is loading or updating. The bar uses the same warm accent color found in highlighted nav items, tying it into the site’s visual language. Once loading completes, the bar either retracts smoothly or fades out, leaving a clean edge at the top of the hero. Because it is so thin, it does not disrupt the vertical rhythm or consume noticeable space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The indicator is fixed at the very top of the viewport while active, independent of scrolling. It overlays above all other page content, including the hero and sticky navigation, without casting shadows or adding chrome. When inactive, it is completely hidden and does not affect layout or spacing.
